description: An abdominal aortic aneurysm is a permanent, localized bulge in the aorta, the main blood vessel that runs through your abdomen. It often grows slowly without any symptoms. The danger is that if it ruptures, it causes massive internal bleeding. Symptoms of rupture include sudden, severe pain in your belly or back, dizziness, and fainting.

An abdominal hernia occurs when an organ or fatty tissue pushes through a weak spot in your abdominal wall. You may notice a soft bulge in your belly or groin that goes away when you lie down. It may cause discomfort when you lift heavy objects or cough. The main risk is that the tissue can become trapped or lose blood supply, which requires emergency care.

A nasal abscess is a pocket of pus that forms inside the nose tissues due to a bacterial infection. It causes pain, swelling, redness, and sometimes difficulty breathing through the affected nostril. The infection can spread to nearby areas including the brain if left untreated, so prompt medical attention is important.

A lung abscess is a pus-filled cavity that forms inside lung tissue, often after pneumonia or from breathing foreign material into the lungs. Symptoms include fever, cough with foul-smelling or bad-tasting phlegm, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. This condition requires hospital care and strong antibiotics.

A pharyngeal abscess is a collection of pus in the deep throat tissues. It usually develops as a complication of severe tonsillitis or throat infection. Symptoms include severe sore throat, fever, muffled voice, difficulty swallowing, and trouble opening your mouth fully. In severe cases, it can block your airway.

Acanthosis nigricans is a skin condition where you develop dark, velvety patches of skin in body folds and creases such as the neck, armpits, and groin. It is not dangerous by itself but often signals underlying insulin resistance or prediabetes. Management focuses on treating the underlying cause.

Acariasis is an infestation with mites, including scabies or house dust mites. It causes itching, rash, and skin irritation. While uncomfortable and bothersome, it is usually mild and treatable with topical or oral antiparasitic medications. In immunocompromised individuals, secondary infections can occur.

Achalasia is a rare disorder of the esophagus where the lower esophageal sphincter fails to relax properly, preventing food and liquid from passing into the stomach. It causes progressive difficulty swallowing, regurgitation, chest pain, and weight loss. Without treatment, it can lead to malnutrition, aspiration pneumonia, and increased risk of esophageal cancer over time.

Acne is a common skin condition that occurs when hair follicles become clogged with oil and dead skin cells. This leads to pimples, blackheads, whiteheads, and sometimes cysts. It affects appearance rather than overall health, though severe cases can cause scarring and psychological distress. Treatment is readily available and serious complications are rare.

Actinic keratosis is a rough, scaly patch on the skin caused by years of sun exposure. It is considered precancerous because a small percentage can progress to squamous cell carcinoma. While actinic keratosis itself is treatable, it requires monitoring because of its malignant potential.

Acute bronchiolitis is a common viral lung infection in infants and young children. It causes inflammation and mucus buildup in the small airways, leading to coughing, wheezing, and difficulty breathing. While most cases are mild and resolve at home, severe cases can lead to respiratory distress and hospitalization, especially in premature babies.

Acute bronchitis is inflammation of the bronchial tubes, the airways that carry air to your lungs. It is usually caused by a viral infection and results in cough, mucus production, fatigue, and sometimes fever. Most cases resolve on their own within a few weeks. However, in elderly or immunocompromised individuals, it can worsen into pneumonia.

Acute bronchospasm is the sudden tightening of the muscles around the airways. This makes it difficult to breathe and causes wheezing, chest tightness, and coughing. It can be triggered by asthma, allergies, infections, or irritants. Severe bronchospasm can cause life-threatening oxygen deprivation if not treated immediately.

Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but serious complication that occurs in the third trimester. Fat accumulates in liver cells, leading to liver failure. Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, jaundice, and confusion. It can rapidly progress to multi-organ failure for both mother and baby unless prompt delivery and intensive care are provided.

Acute angle-closure glaucoma is a sudden, severe increase of pressure inside the eye. It causes intense eye pain, headache, nausea, vomiting, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. It can permanently damage the optic nerve and cause blindness within hours if not treated immediately.

Acute kidney injury is a sudden episode of kidney failure or damage that occurs within hours or days. Waste products build up in the blood because the kidneys cannot filter them properly. Causes include dehydration, infections, medications, or reduced blood flow to the kidneys. Severe cases require dialysis and can lead to permanent kidney damage.

Acute otitis media is an infection of the middle ear, which is very common in children. It causes ear pain, fever, irritability, and sometimes temporary hearing loss. Most cases are mild and resolve on their own or with antibiotics. Complications such as eardrum rupture or spread of infection are rare.

Acute pancreatitis is a sudden inflammation of the pancreas, an organ behind your stomach that helps with digestion and blood sugar control. It causes severe upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and fever. It can range from mild, self-limited disease to severe necrotizing pancreatitis with multi-organ failure and sepsis.

Acute respiratory distress syndrome, or ARDS, is a life-threatening lung condition where widespread inflammation leads to fluid buildup in the air sacs. This fluid prevents oxygen from reaching the bloodstream. It typically occurs in critically ill patients with conditions like sepsis, pneumonia, or major trauma. ARDS requires mechanical ventilation and intensive care.

Acute sinusitis is a temporary inflammation of the sinuses, the air-filled spaces in your face. It is usually caused by a viral infection like the common cold. Symptoms include facial pain, nasal congestion, headache, and thick nasal discharge. Most cases resolve in one to two weeks without antibiotics.

Acute stress reaction is a temporary psychological response to a traumatic or very stressful event. It can cause anxiety, irritability, insomnia, poor concentration, and physical symptoms like a rapid heartbeat. Symptoms typically resolve within days to weeks after the stressful event ends. Professional support may help recovery.

Adhesive capsulitis, also known as frozen shoulder, is a condition where your shoulder joint becomes stiff and painful due to thickening of the joint capsule. It develops gradually and limits your range of motion. It typically resolves over one to three years. While not dangerous, it can significantly impact daily activities.

Adjustment reaction, or adjustment disorder, is a maladaptive emotional or behavioral response to an identifiable life stressor such as divorce, job loss, or illness. Symptoms include sadness, anxiety, and difficulty coping. They usually resolve within six months after the stressor ends. Professional help may be needed if symptoms persist.

An adrenal adenoma is a benign, non-cancerous tumor of the adrenal gland, often discovered incidentally on imaging tests done for other reasons. Most are non-functioning and cause no symptoms or health problems. Some may produce excess hormones, leading to conditions like Cushing syndrome, which require monitoring or treatment.

Adrenal cancer is a rare but aggressive malignancy of the adrenal glands, which produce hormones that regulate many body functions. Symptoms may include abdominal pain, weight loss, hormone imbalances such as excess cortisol or androgens, and a palpable mass. It often spreads to other organs and has a poor prognosis.

Alcohol abuse, also called alcohol use disorder, is a pattern of excessive drinking that leads to significant impairment or distress. Signs include inability to control drinking, neglecting responsibilities, and continued use despite negative consequences. Severe cases can cause liver damage, pancreatitis, brain damage, accidents, and premature death.

Alcohol intoxication is the acute effect of consuming excessive alcohol. It causes slurred speech, impaired coordination, confusion, and vomiting. In severe cases, known as alcohol poisoning, it leads to respiratory depression, coma, or death. Severe intoxication requires emergency medical care.

Alcohol withdrawal is a set of symptoms that occur when a person who is dependent on alcohol suddenly stops drinking. Symptoms range from mild anxiety and insomnia to severe complications like seizures, hallucinations, and delirium tremens, which can be fatal. Severe withdrawal requires medical management.

Alcoholic liver disease is a spectrum of liver damage caused by chronic heavy alcohol use. It includes fatty liver, alcoholic hepatitis, and cirrhosis. Symptoms may include jaundice yellowing of the skin, abdominal swelling, fatigue, and confusion. Without stopping alcohol use, it progresses to liver failure and death.

An allergy is an abnormal immune reaction to a normally harmless substance such as pollen, food, or medication. Symptoms range from mild sneezing, itching, and rash to severe anaphylaxis, which causes difficulty breathing, drop in blood pressure, and loss of consciousness. Anaphylaxis is life-threatening and requires immediate epinephrine.

Allergy to animals is typically a reaction to proteins found in pet dander, saliva, or urine. It causes symptoms such as sneezing, runny nose, itchy eyes, wheezing, and skin rashes. While uncomfortable, it is generally mild and manageable with antihistamines, avoidance, or immunotherapy. Severe reactions are rare.

Alopecia refers to hair loss, which can be localized or widespread, temporary or permanent. Types include alopecia areata which is autoimmune patchy hair loss, male or female pattern baldness, and stress-related shedding. While not physically harmful, it can cause psychological distress. Hair typically regrows in many cases.

Alzheimer disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder and the most common cause of dementia. It causes memory loss, confusion, personality changes, and eventually loss of ability to perform basic daily tasks. It is incurable and ultimately fatal, typically within eight to ten years after diagnosis. Late stages require full-time care.

Amblyopia, also called lazy eye, is a developmental vision disorder where one eye fails to achieve normal visual acuity. It is often due to strabismus, which is crossed eyes, or a difference in prescription between the two eyes. It is treatable in childhood with patching, glasses, or drops. If untreated, it can cause permanent vision loss in the affected eye.

Amyloidosis is a rare disease where abnormal proteins called amyloid build up in organs and tissues, causing them to malfunction. Symptoms depend on which organs are affected and may include fatigue, swelling, shortness of breath, and neuropathy. Some forms are localized and mild, but systemic amyloidosis can lead to organ failure and death.

Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, or ALS also known as Lou Gehrig disease, is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that affects motor neurons. It leads to muscle weakness, atrophy, paralysis, and eventually respiratory failure. There is no cure, and most patients die within three to five years of diagnosis. Treatment focuses on managing symptoms.

An anal fissure is a small tear in the lining of the anus. It is often caused by passing hard or large stools. It causes sharp pain during bowel movements and sometimes bright red blood on toilet paper. Most fissures heal on their own with stool softeners and dietary changes, while chronic fissures may require medication or surgery.

An anal fistula is an abnormal tunnel that connects the anal canal to the skin near the anus. It usually results from a previous anal abscess. Symptoms include pain, swelling, and drainage of pus or stool. It does not heal on its own and typically requires surgical repair. Untreated fistulas can lead to recurrent infections.

Anemia is a condition where the blood lacks enough healthy red blood cells to carry adequate oxygen to your tissues. It causes fatigue, weakness, pale skin, and shortness of breath. Causes include iron deficiency, vitamin deficiencies, chronic diseases, or blood loss. Most cases are mild to moderate and treatable, but severe anemia can lead to heart failure.

disease_name: anemia due to chronic kidney disease
description: Anemia is common in chronic kidney disease because damaged kidneys produce less erythropoietin, a hormone that stimulates red blood cell production. Symptoms include fatigue, pale skin, and difficulty exercising. The severity matches the stage of kidney disease. Advanced cases require iron supplements, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or blood transfusions.

Anemia due to malignancy, or cancer, can result from the cancer itself such as bone marrow infiltration or bleeding tumor, or from chemotherapy. Symptoms include severe fatigue, weakness, and shortness of breath. The severity depends on the underlying cancer and degree of anemia. Management includes treating the cancer, transfusions, and growth factors.

Anemia of chronic disease is a mild to moderate anemia that occurs in people with long-term inflammatory conditions such as autoimmune diseases, infections, or cancer. The body inflammatory response interferes with iron use and red blood cell production. It improves when the underlying condition is treated. Symptoms include fatigue and pale skin.

Angina is chest pain or discomfort caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle, usually due to coronary artery disease. It can feel like pressure, squeezing, or tightness in the chest, sometimes spreading to the arms, neck, or jaw. Stable angina is predictable and manageable with medication, but unstable angina is a medical emergency that can precede a heart attack.

Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory arthritis that primarily affects the spine and the sacroiliac joints in the pelvis. It causes back pain, stiffness, and eventually fusion of the vertebrae, which is called bamboo spine. It is not life-threatening but can be severely disabling if untreated, leading to reduced mobility and posture changes.

Anxiety is a normal emotion characterized by feelings of tension, worried thoughts, and physical changes like increased blood pressure. Anxiety disorders involve persistent, excessive fear or worry that interferes with daily life. Most cases are mild to moderate and highly treatable with therapy and medication. Severe anxiety can lead to panic attacks and depression.

Aortic valve disease includes aortic stenosis, which is narrowing, and aortic regurgitation, which is leaking. Both impair blood flow from the heart to the body. Symptoms include chest pain, shortness of breath, fatigue, and fainting. Mild cases may be without symptoms, but severe untreated disease leads to heart failure, arrhythmias, and sudden death.

Aphakia is the absence of the lens of the eye. It is most commonly caused by cataract surgery or trauma. It causes severe farsightedness and loss of ability to focus on near objects. Vision can be corrected with special glasses, contact lenses, or an intraocular lens implant. It is not dangerous or painful.

Aphthous ulcers, also known as canker sores, are small, painful shallow sores that develop on the inside of the mouth or lips. They are not contagious and usually heal on their own within one to two weeks. While uncomfortable and sometimes recurrent, they are benign and rarely cause complications.

Aplastic anemia is a rare but serious condition where the bone marrow fails to produce enough red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. This leads to severe fatigue, frequent infections, and easy bleeding. Without treatment such as immunosuppressive therapy or bone marrow transplant, it can be fatal within months.

Appendicitis is inflammation of the appendix, a small pouch attached to the large intestine. It causes sudden pain that starts near the navel and shifts to the lower right abdomen, along with nausea, vomiting, and fever. Without prompt surgical removal called appendectomy, the appendix can rupture, leading to peritonitis, sepsis, and death.

Arrhythmia refers to any abnormal heart rhythm. It can range from harmless, like occasional skipped beats, to life-threatening, like ventricular fibrillation. Symptoms include palpitations, dizziness, fainting, and chest discomfort. Many arrhythmias are mild and require no treatment, but severe arrhythmias can cause sudden cardiac arrest and death.

Arthritis of the hip is inflammation of the hip joint. The most common type is osteoarthritis, which is wear and tear, but other types include rheumatoid arthritis. It causes pain, stiffness, and reduced range of motion, making walking difficult. While not life-threatening, it can be severely disabling and reduce quality of life.

Ascending cholangitis is a bacterial infection of the bile duct, usually caused by an obstruction such as a gallstone. Symptoms include fever, yellowing of the skin called jaundice, abdominal pain, and confusion. It can rapidly progress to sepsis and septic shock, with high death rate if not treated urgently with antibiotics and bile duct drainage.

Asperger syndrome is a developmental disorder on the autism spectrum. It is characterized by difficulties in social interaction and nonverbal communication, along with restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior and interests. Unlike other autism spectrum disorders, language and intellectual development are typically normal. It is not life-threatening.

Aspergillosis is an infection caused by the fungus Aspergillus. It can affect the lungs in allergic, chronic, or invasive forms. Allergic forms cause asthma-like symptoms and are mild. Invasive aspergillosis occurs in immunocompromised people, such as transplant or chemotherapy patients, and spreads to the blood and organs with very high death rate.

Asthma is a chronic lung disease where airways become inflamed and narrow, causing wheezing, coughing, chest tightness, and difficulty breathing. Severity varies. Mild intermittent asthma requires only occasional rescue inhalers. Severe persistent asthma can cause frequent attacks and emergency visits. Severe asthma attacks can be fatal.

Astigmatism is a common vision condition where the cornea or lens has an irregular curve. This causes blurred or distorted vision at all distances. It is usually present from birth and often occurs with nearsightedness or farsightedness. It is easily corrected with glasses, contact lenses, or surgery and is not dangerous.

Atelectasis is the partial or complete collapse of a lung or a lobe of the lung. It often occurs after surgery or due to a blocked airway. It can cause cough, shallow breathing, and sometimes fever. Small areas are usually without symptoms and resolve on their own. Most cases are mild and reversible with deep breathing exercises.

Athlete foot, also called tinea pedis, is a common fungal infection of the feet. It causes itching, scaling, and cracking between the toes. It is contagious but easily treated with over-the-counter antifungal creams. If untreated, it can spread to toenails or other body parts, but serious complications are rare.

Atonic bladder, also called flaccid bladder, is a condition where the bladder muscle fails to contract properly. This leads to incomplete emptying, urinary retention, and overflow incontinence. Causes include nerve damage from diabetes, spinal cord injury, or medications. While not life-threatening, it can lead to recurrent urinary tract infections and kidney damage.

Atrial fibrillation is a common heart arrhythmia where the upper chambers of the heart beat irregularly and rapidly. Symptoms include palpitations, fatigue, and shortness of breath. While often manageable with medication, atrial fibrillation significantly increases the risk of stroke by five times and heart failure.

Atrial flutter is a type of abnormal heart rhythm similar to atrial fibrillation but more organized. It causes a fast but often regular heartbeat, leading to palpitations, dizziness, and shortness of breath. Like atrial fibrillation, it increases stroke risk and can cause heart failure if untreated. It is often treatable with medication or catheter ablation.

Atrophic skin conditions involve thinning of the skin layers, making it fragile, translucent, and prone to tearing or bruising. Causes include aging, chronic sun exposure, corticosteroid use, or certain diseases such as Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. While generally mild and cosmetic, severe skin atrophy can lead to slow-healing wounds.

Atrophic vaginitis is thinning, drying, and inflammation of the vaginal walls due to decreased estrogen. It typically occurs after menopause. Symptoms include vaginal dryness, burning, pain during intercourse, and increased urinary tract infections. It is not dangerous but can significantly impact quality of life and sexual function.

Atrophy of the corpus cavernosum refers to the degeneration or shrinkage of the paired cylinders of erectile tissue in the penis. It is often due to aging, hormonal changes, or lack of use from erectile dysfunction. It can worsen erectile dysfunction but is not painful or life-threatening. It can affect sexual function and psychological well-being.

ADHD is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by persistent inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity that interferes with daily functioning. Severity ranges from mild where symptoms barely disrupt life to moderate where there are significant challenges at school, work, or relationships. It is not life-threatening but can lead to secondary issues like poor academic performance, accidents, and low self-esteem. Treatment with medication and therapy is very effective.

Autism spectrum disorder is a developmental condition affecting communication, social interaction, and behavior, with varying levels of functioning. Severity ranges from mild where individuals may live independently to severe where substantial support is needed for daily living. While not life-threatening, severe autism can lead to self-injurious behaviors, seizures, and difficulty with basic self-care. Early intervention improves outcomes.

Autonomic nervous system disorders, also called dysautonomia, affect involuntary functions like heart rate, blood pressure, digestion, and temperature control. Symptoms include dizziness, fainting, abnormal sweating, bladder problems, and gastrointestinal issues. Some forms are mild to moderate, while others are severe and progressive, leading to disability. Not usually immediately life-threatening but can significantly reduce quality of life.

Avascular necrosis is the death of bone tissue due to disruption of blood supply. This leads to tiny breaks in the bone and eventual collapse. It most commonly affects the hip, but also shoulders, knees, and other joints. It causes pain and limited motion, and without treatment leads to severe arthritis. Early treatment can prevent collapse, but advanced avascular necrosis requires joint replacement.

Balanitis is inflammation of the head of the penis. It is usually due to poor hygiene, infection from fungus or bacteria, or skin irritation. Symptoms include redness, swelling, itching, and pain. It is generally mild and resolves with proper hygiene and antifungal or antibiotic creams. Complications are rare but can include tight foreskin or recurrent infections.

Bell palsy is sudden, one-sided weakness or paralysis of the facial muscles due to inflammation of the facial nerve, often from a viral infection. It causes drooping of one side of the face, drooling, and difficulty closing the eye. While alarming, most cases resolve spontaneously within weeks to months, often completely. Rarely, residual weakness remains.

Benign kidney cysts are fluid-filled sacs in the kidneys that are non-cancerous and very common, especially with age. Most cause no symptoms and are found incidentally on imaging tests done for other reasons. Rarely, large cysts can cause pain, bleeding, or infection. They are generally harmless and require no treatment unless they cause symptoms.

Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo is an inner ear disorder causing brief episodes of intense spinning sensation triggered by head movements. It results from tiny calcium crystals becoming dislodged into the semicircular canals of the ear. While very uncomfortable and sometimes nauseating, it is not dangerous and often resolves spontaneously or with canalith repositioning maneuvers like the Epley maneuver.

Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate gland common in older men. It causes urinary symptoms such as frequent urination, weak stream, difficulty starting, and waking at night to urinate. It is not life-threatening but can significantly impact quality of life. Severe cases can lead to urinary retention, bladder stones, and kidney damage.

Leukorrhea is normal, physiological vaginal discharge that increases during pregnancy, ovulation, or sexual arousal. It is usually clear to white, odorless or mild-smelling, and not associated with itching or irritation. This is a normal bodily function and not a disease. It requires no treatment unless accompanied by signs of infection such as color change, foul odor, or itching.

Bipolar disorder is a mental health condition characterized by extreme mood swings between mania, which is euphoria, high energy, and impulsivity, and depression, which is sadness and low energy. Severity varies. Mild forms cause less impairment. Severe mania or depression can lead to psychosis, self-harm, suicide, or reckless behavior. With treatment, many people stabilize.

Birth trauma refers to physical injuries sustained by a newborn during the birthing process. These can include bruises, fractures such as clavicle, nerve damage such as brachial plexus, or bleeding inside the skull. Severity ranges from minor bruises that heal quickly to severe brain damage leading to cerebral palsy or death. Most birth traumas are mild and resolve spontaneously.

Bladder cancer is a malignancy of the bladder lining, often presenting with blood in the urine. Most cases are non-muscle invasive, which are moderate and treatable with surgery and follow-up. Muscle-invasive or metastatic bladder cancer is life-threatening with poorer outcomes. Treatment includes surgery, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and radiation.

Bladder disorder is a broad term for any condition affecting the urinary bladder. This includes overactive bladder, interstitial cystitis, neurogenic bladder, or structural problems. Symptoms include frequent urination, urgency, incontinence, pain, or retention. Most bladder disorders are not life-threatening but can significantly impair quality of life.

Bladder obstruction is a blockage that prevents urine from flowing out of the bladder. It is often due to an enlarged prostate in men, urethral stricture, or bladder stones. It causes difficulty urinating and a weak stream, and can lead to acute urinary retention which is an emergency, or chronic kidney damage if prolonged. Acute complete obstruction requires catheterization.

Blepharitis is chronic inflammation of the eyelid margins. It causes redness, itching, burning, and crusting near the eyelashes. It is often due to bacterial overgrowth or skin conditions like rosacea or seborrheic dermatitis. It is not dangerous or contagious but can be bothersome and chronic. Treatment includes warm compresses, eyelid hygiene, and sometimes antibiotics.

Blepharospasm is an involuntary, forceful contraction of the eyelid muscles. It causes excessive blinking and eye closure. It is a focal dystonia, which is a movement disorder, that can interfere with reading, driving, or daily activities. While not life-threatening, severe cases can cause functional blindness where you cannot open your eyes. Treatment includes botulinum toxin injections.

Bone cancer is a malignant tumor of the bone. It can be primary, such as osteosarcoma or Ewing sarcoma, or metastatic from another cancer. Primary bone cancer is rare but aggressive, causing bone pain, fractures, and swelling. Metastatic bone cancer is more common and indicates advanced stage disease. Treatment involves surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation.

Bone disorder encompasses many conditions including osteoporosis which is brittle bones, Paget disease, osteomalacia which is soft bones, and osteogenesis imperfecta. Symptoms include bone pain, fractures, and deformity. Severity varies greatly. Mild osteopenia causes no immediate issues. Severe osteoporosis leads to frequent fractures. Some forms can be life-threatening in infancy.

A bone spur on the heel bone, also called a calcaneal spur, is a bony outgrowth often associated with plantar fasciitis. Many are without symptoms. When painful, it causes heel pain with walking, especially the first steps in the morning. It is not dangerous and often resolves with conservative treatment such as stretching, orthotics, and anti-inflammatory medications.

Brachial neuritis, also called Parsonage-Turner syndrome, is a rare condition of sudden, severe shoulder and arm pain followed by weakness and muscle wasting. It is caused by inflammation of the brachial plexus nerves. The pain improves within days to weeks, but weakness can take months to years to resolve. Most patients recover significant function.

Brain cancer is the abnormal growth of cells in the brain. It can be primary, such as glioblastoma or meningioma, or metastatic from other cancers. Symptoms include headaches, seizures, cognitive changes, weakness, and personality changes. Severity is almost always severe to life-threatening, especially for high-grade gliomas. Treatment includes surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy.

Breast cancer is a malignancy that forms in the breast tissue cells. Severity depends on stage at diagnosis. Early-stage localized cancer is moderate and highly curable with over ninety percent five-year survival. Advanced metastatic cancer that has spread to bones, liver, or lungs is life-threatening with lower survival rates. Treatment includes surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, and hormone therapy.

A breast cyst is a fluid-filled sac in the breast. It is very common in women aged thirty to fifty. They are benign and often feel smooth, round, and movable. Most cause no symptoms or mild tenderness. Large cysts may cause discomfort. They do not increase cancer risk and often resolve on their own. No treatment is needed unless they cause symptoms.

Mastitis is an infection of the breast tissue. It is usually in breastfeeding women and causes pain, swelling, redness, warmth, and fever. It is typically caused by bacteria entering through cracked nipples. With antibiotics and continued milk removal, most cases resolve in days. If untreated, an abscess may form. Complications are uncommon with prompt treatment.

A broken tooth occurs when a tooth cracks, chips, or fractures due to injury, decay, or biting hard objects. It may cause pain, sensitivity to hot or cold, and sharp edges that can cut the tongue or cheek. Small breaks are mild and painless. Large breaks extending to the nerve can be moderate with significant pain. It is not dangerous unless infection develops.

A bunion is a bony bump that forms on the joint at the base of the big toe. It causes the big toe to angle toward the second toe. It can be painful, especially with tight shoes, and may lead to arthritis or other toe deformities. Severity ranges from mild with no pain to moderate with pain when walking and difficulty fitting shoes. It is not dangerous.

A burn is tissue damage caused by heat, chemicals, electricity, radiation, or friction. Severity depends on depth, which can be superficial, partial-thickness, or full-thickness, and the body surface area involved. Minor burns that are small and superficial are mild and heal on their own. Major burns that are large, deep, or on the face, hands, or genitals can cause infection, shock, organ failure, and death.

Bursitis is inflammation of a bursa, which is a small fluid-filled sac that cushions bones, tendons, and muscles. It usually occurs near joints like the shoulder, elbow, hip, or knee. It causes pain, swelling, and limited motion. Most cases are acute and resolve in days to weeks with rest, ice, and anti-inflammatory medications. Chronic bursitis can be moderate and require steroid injections.

A callus is a thickened, hard area of skin that forms in response to repeated friction, pressure, or irritation. It often occurs on the hands or feet. They are generally painless and protective. However, excessive callus on the feet can cause discomfort or cracking. Calluses are benign and not dangerous. Regular filing, moisturizing, or reducing pressure resolves them.

Carbon monoxide poisoning occurs when carbon monoxide gas, which is colorless and odorless, builds up in the bloodstream. It displaces oxygen and causes tissue hypoxia. Symptoms include headache, dizziness, confusion, nausea, and loss of consciousness. Severe poisoning leads to brain damage, coma, and death. This is a medical emergency requiring one hundred percent oxygen or hyperbaric oxygen therapy.

Carcinoid syndrome is a set of symptoms caused by neuroendocrine tumors called carcinoid tumors that secrete serotonin and other vasoactive substances into the bloodstream. Symptoms include flushing, diarrhea, wheezing, and heart palpitations. Severity ranges from mild intermittent flushing to severe diarrhea and carcinoid heart disease which is valvular damage and heart failure. It reduces quality of life.

Cardiac arrest is the sudden loss of heart function, breathing, and consciousness. It is caused by an electrical malfunction in the heart such as ventricular fibrillation. Without immediate CPR and defibrillation, death occurs within minutes. Survivors may have brain damage or other organ failure. This is the most acute life-threatening event in medicine, requiring emergency response.

Cardiomyopathy is a group of diseases that affect the heart muscle. It makes it harder for the heart to pump blood, leading to heart failure, arrhythmias, and sudden death. Types include dilated, hypertrophic, and restrictive. Severity varies from mild, without symptoms forms to severe, progressive heart failure requiring transplant. Some types are a common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes.

Carpal tunnel syndrome is caused by compression of the median nerve as it travels through the wrist. It leads to numbness, tingling, and weakness in the hand and fingers, specifically the thumb, index, and middle fingers. It is usually mild, with symptoms that come and go. Without treatment, it can become moderate with permanent nerve damage and muscle wasting. It is not life-threatening.

Cat scratch disease is a bacterial infection typically transmitted by a cat scratch or bite. It causes a red, swollen bump at the site, then swollen lymph nodes, fever, and fatigue. Most cases resolve without treatment within weeks. Rarely, it can cause serious complications like brain inflammation or eye inflammation. Overall severity is mild, especially in healthy individuals.

A cataract is a clouding of the eye natural lens. It leads to blurred vision, glare, and difficulty seeing at night. It develops slowly with age. Cataracts are not painful or dangerous, but they can significantly impair vision if untreated. Surgery to remove the cataract is very safe and restores vision. Severity ranges from mild slight blurring to moderate affecting daily activities.

Celiac disease is an autoimmune disorder where ingestion of gluten, a protein in wheat, barley, and rye, damages the small intestine. It causes diarrhea, bloating, weight loss, fatigue, and nutritional deficiencies. Untreated, it can lead to osteoporosis, infertility, and rarely intestinal lymphoma. Severity ranges from mild without symptoms to severe with malabsorption and weight loss.

Cellulitis or abscess of the mouth refers to bacterial infection spreading in the soft tissues of the mouth, gums, or jaw. Examples include Ludwig angina and dental abscess. It causes pain, swelling, redness, and can extend to the neck and throat, potentially blocking the airway. This can be a medical emergency if breathing is compromised. Severity ranges from mild localized infection to life-threatening airway compromise.

Central atherosclerosis refers to the buildup of plaque made of fat, cholesterol, and calcium in the major arteries, especially the aorta and its branches. This leads to hardening and narrowing of the arteries. It underlies many severe conditions including aortic aneurysm, stroke, kidney disease, and peripheral artery disease. Severity is often without symptoms for years, but once symptoms appear it can be life-threatening.

disease_name: central retinal artery or vein occlusion
description: Central retinal artery occlusion is a blockage of the main artery supplying the retina. It causes sudden, painless, severe vision loss in one eye. Central retinal vein occlusion causes similar but often milder vision loss. Central retinal artery occlusion is an eye emergency because vision loss is often permanent if not treated within hours. Both can signal underlying vascular disease.

Cerebral edema is the buildup of excess fluid in the brain tissue. This increases pressure inside the skull, which can compress brain tissue and blood vessels, leading to brain damage or death. Causes include head trauma, stroke, infection, tumors, or altitude sickness. Symptoms include headache, nausea, seizures, confusion, and coma. This is a medical emergency requiring intensive care to lower brain pressure.

Cerebral palsy is a group of permanent movement disorders caused by brain damage before, during, or shortly after birth. It affects muscle tone, coordination, and posture. Severity ranges widely. Mild cases cause slight awkwardness. Severe cases cause inability to walk, speech impairment, intellectual disability, and seizures. It is not progressive but can be disabling. Severe cases may shorten lifespan due to breathing complications.

Cervical cancer is a malignancy of the cervix, almost always caused by HPV infection. Early-stage cervical cancer is highly curable with surgery or radiation, making it moderate in severity. Advanced metastatic cervical cancer is life-threatening with poorer survival. Screening with Pap smear and HPV testing detects precancerous changes, allowing prevention. It is preventable and treatable when caught early.

Cervical disorder is a broad term for conditions affecting the cervix. These include cervicitis which is inflammation, polyps which are benign growths, stenosis which is narrowing, or dysplasia which are precancerous changes. Most are mild and often without symptoms. Cervical dysplasia requires monitoring or treatment because it can progress to cervical cancer. Routine Pap smears are crucial.

Cervicitis is inflammation of the cervix. It is often due to infection such as chlamydia, gonorrhea, or bacterial vaginosis, or from irritation. Symptoms include abnormal vaginal discharge, bleeding between periods or after intercourse, and pelvic pain. It is usually mild and treatable with antibiotics. If untreated, it can lead to pelvic inflammatory disease and infertility.

A chalazion is a small, painless lump in the eyelid caused by a blocked oil gland. It can cause swelling, redness, and sometimes blurred vision if large enough. Unlike a stye, it is not usually infected. Most chalazia resolve on their own with warm compresses and massage within weeks. If persistent, it can be drained. Severity is mild, with no complications.

Chickenpox is a highly contagious viral infection causing an itchy, blister-like rash, fever, and fatigue. In healthy children, it is usually mild and self-limiting, resolving in five to seven days. However, in adults, pregnant women, and immunocompromised individuals, it can be severe, causing pneumonia, brain inflammation, or death. With the vaccine, cases are much milder.

Chlamydia is a common sexually transmitted infection caused by Chlamydia trachomatis. Many people have no symptoms, but it can cause discharge, painful urination, and pelvic pain. Untreated, it can lead to pelvic inflammatory disease, infertility, and ectopic pregnancy in women, and epididymitis in men. It is easily cured with antibiotics, so severity is mild if treated.

Cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder. It is usually due to a gallstone blocking the cystic duct. It causes severe right upper abdominal pain, fever, nausea, and vomiting. Without treatment with antibiotics and often gallbladder removal, it can lead to gangrene, perforation, peritonitis, and sepsis, which can be fatal. It is a common surgical emergency.

Choledocholithiasis is the presence of gallstones in the common bile duct. This leads to bile duct obstruction, yellowing of the skin called jaundice, abdominal pain, and often infection of the bile duct or pancreatitis. This is a serious condition because it can cause life-threatening infection or acute pancreatitis. It requires endoscopic removal of the stones.

A cholesteatoma is an abnormal growth of skin cells in the middle ear behind the eardrum. It can erode the bones of the middle ear, leading to conductive hearing loss, dizziness, and facial nerve paralysis. It is not cancerous but can cause serious complications if untreated, including meningitis, brain abscess, and permanent hearing loss. Surgical removal is required.

Chondromalacia patellae is softening and breakdown of the cartilage on the underside of the kneecap. It causes knee pain, especially when climbing stairs, squatting, or sitting with bent knees. It is very common in young athletes and usually resolves with physical therapy and strengthening. Severity is mild to moderate. It rarely requires surgery and does not lead to severe complications.

Chorioretinitis is inflammation of the choroid, which is the vascular layer of the eye, and the retina. It is often due to infections such as toxoplasmosis, CMV, or herpes, or autoimmune diseases. Symptoms include floaters, blurred vision, and sensitivity to light. It can cause permanent vision loss if the central part of the retina is involved. Treatment depends on the cause.

Chronic back pain is persistent pain in the back lasting more than twelve weeks. It is often due to degenerative disc disease, arthritis, muscle strain, or nerve compression such as sciatica. It is not life-threatening but significantly impacts quality of life, work, and mental health. Severity ranges from mild occasional discomfort to severe debilitating pain that limits function.

Chronic constipation is infrequent bowel movements or difficulty passing stools for weeks or longer. It causes straining, bloating, and discomfort. While not dangerous for most, severe constipation can lead to fecal impaction, hemorrhoids, anal fissures, or rarely bowel obstruction. It is seldom life-threatening but can reduce quality of life. It is treatable with lifestyle changes, fiber, and laxatives.

Chronic open-angle glaucoma is a progressive eye disease where the optic nerve is damaged, usually due to increased pressure inside the eye. It develops slowly without early symptoms, causing gradual peripheral vision loss that can progress to blindness if untreated. It is not curable but can be managed with eye drops, laser, or surgery to prevent further vision loss.

CIDP is a neurological disorder where the body immune system attacks the myelin sheaths of peripheral nerves. This leads to progressive weakness, numbness, and impaired motor function. Unlike Guillain-Barré syndrome, CIDP lasts longer than eight weeks and has a relapsing or progressive course. While not immediately life-threatening, severe weakness can lead to breathing difficulties and significant disability.

Chronic kidney disease is the gradual loss of kidney function over months to years. Stages range from mild stage one to kidney failure stage five requiring dialysis or transplant. Symptoms include fatigue, swelling, and changes in urination. Advanced chronic kidney disease can cause life-threatening complications like high potassium, fluid overload, and cardiovascular disease.

Chronic knee pain is persistent pain in the knee joint lasting over three months. It is commonly due to osteoarthritis, tendinitis, bursitis, or previous injury. It can limit mobility and reduce quality of life but is not life-threatening. Severity ranges from mild discomfort to severe pain that interferes with walking and daily activities. Treatment includes physical therapy, medications, injections, or surgery.

COPD is a progressive lung disease causing obstructed airflow, chronic bronchitis, and emphysema. Symptoms include chronic cough, sputum production, and shortness of breath. It is not curable and worsens over time, leading to respiratory failure, heart problems, and death. Severity ranges from mild with few symptoms to very severe requiring oxygen or lung transplant. It is a leading cause of death worldwide.

Chronic otitis media is persistent or recurrent middle ear infection with eardrum perforation and discharge. It can cause hearing loss and, in rare cases, lead to abnormal skin growth in the ear or intracranial complications like meningitis or brain abscess. Most cases are mild and managed with antibiotics and ear care. Severity is mild to moderate, but long-term neglect can worsen outcomes.

Chronic pain disorder is pain that persists beyond the usual healing time, often more than three to six months. It affects physical and emotional health. It can arise from conditions like fibromyalgia, arthritis, or nerve damage. Severity varies from mild discomfort to incapacitating pain requiring opioids or procedures. It is not directly life-threatening but can lead to depression and disability.

Chronic pancreatitis is progressive inflammation of the pancreas leading to permanent damage, fibrosis, and loss of digestive and hormone functions. Symptoms include chronic abdominal pain, poor absorption of nutrients, weight loss, and diabetes. It increases risk of pancreatic cancer. Severity ranges from moderate manageable with enzymes and pain control to severe debilitating pain and life-threatening complications.

Chronic rheumatic fever results from repeated episodes of acute rheumatic fever. It causes permanent damage to heart valves, called rheumatic heart disease. This leads to valve narrowing or leaking, which can cause heart failure, arrhythmias, and stroke. While preventable with antibiotic prophylaxis, established rheumatic heart disease is moderate to severe and may require valve surgery.

Chronic sinusitis is persistent inflammation of the sinuses lasting over twelve weeks. It causes nasal congestion, facial pain, headache, and reduced sense of smell. It is not dangerous but significantly impacts quality of life. Causes include allergies, nasal polyps, or structural issues. Most cases are mild to moderate, treatable with nasal sprays, antibiotics, or sinus surgery if stubborn.

A chronic ulcer is a break in the skin or mucous membrane that fails to heal within weeks. It is often due to poor circulation from vein or artery problems, diabetes, or pressure from bedsores. It is painful and prone to infection, bone infection, and sepsis. Severity ranges from moderate slow-healing but manageable to severe requiring amputation or causing life-threatening infection.

Cirrhosis is advanced scarring of the liver caused by long-term liver damage from alcohol, hepatitis, or fatty liver. It impairs liver function, leading to yellowing of the skin, fluid in the abdomen, bleeding veins in the esophagus, brain dysfunction, and liver cancer. It is irreversible and eventually fatal without liver transplant. Severity ranges from moderate without symptoms to life-threatening with complications.

Coagulation disorders affect the blood ability to clot. This can lead to excessive bleeding, such as in hemophilia, or excessive clotting, called thrombophilia. Mild disorders may cause easy bruising or prolonged bleeding from cuts. Severe hemophilia can cause spontaneous joint bleeds and life-threatening internal bleeding after injury or surgery. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening.

Cold sores, also called fever blisters, are small, fluid-filled blisters around the lips. They are caused by herpes simplex virus type one. They heal in seven to ten days but come back. They are contagious but not serious in healthy people. In immunocompromised individuals, the virus can cause severe, widespread infection. Severity is mild for most, but can be moderate in immunocompromised people.

A colonic polyp is a small clump of cells that forms on the lining of the colon. Most are benign, such as hyperplastic or inflammatory polyps, but some types called adenomas can develop into colorectal cancer over years. Polyps themselves are without symptoms and mild, but their cancer potential makes them important to remove. Severity is mild if removed.

Colorectal cancer is cancer of the colon or rectum. It often develops from polyps. Early-stage cancer is moderate and curable with surgery. Advanced metastatic cancer is life-threatening with lower survival rates. Symptoms include blood in stool, change in bowel habits, and weight loss. It is the third most common cancer worldwide. Severity depends on stage.

The common cold is a viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, mostly caused by rhinovirus. It causes runny nose, sneezing, sore throat, and cough. It is self-limiting, usually resolving in seven to ten days. It is mild in healthy individuals but can be moderate in infants, elderly, or those with asthma or COPD. Complications are rare.

Complex regional pain syndrome is a chronic pain condition usually affecting an arm or leg after injury. It is characterized by prolonged or excessive pain, swelling, color changes, and temperature sensitivity. It is not life-threatening but can cause severe disability and psychological distress. Severity ranges from mild resolving within months to severe causing permanent impairment and muscle wasting.

A concussion is a mild traumatic brain injury caused by a blow to the head or body that shakes the brain inside the skull. Symptoms include headache, confusion, dizziness, memory issues, and sensitivity to light and noise. Most concussions resolve in days to weeks. Repeated concussions or severe forms can cause long-term brain damage. Severity is mild to moderate.

Conduct disorder is a childhood psychiatric disorder characterized by persistent aggressive or destructive behavior, violation of rules, and lack of empathy. It is not life-threatening directly, but can lead to legal problems, substance abuse, injuries from risky behavior, and progression to antisocial personality disorder. Severity ranges from mild to severe depending on behavior frequency. Early intervention is critical.

Conductive hearing loss occurs when sound cannot travel efficiently through the outer ear canal to the eardrum and middle ear bones. Causes include earwax impaction, middle ear fluid from otitis media, eardrum perforation, or abnormal bone growth in the ear. It is often mild and frequently reversible with medical or surgical treatment. Severity ranges from mild to moderate.

Congenital heart defects are structural heart problems present at birth. They range from small holes, such as ventricular septal defect, to complex malformations, such as tetralogy of Fallot. Severity varies widely. Minor defects are mild and often need no treatment. Moderate defects require medication or surgery. Severe defects are life-threatening and require multiple surgeries in infancy.

Congenital malformation syndromes are sets of physical abnormalities present at birth, often involving multiple organ systems. Examples include Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, and VACTERL association. Severity ranges from mild with cosmetic or minor functional issues to severe with major organ failure, intellectual disability, or shortened lifespan. Some are life-threatening in infancy.

Conjunctivitis, also called pink eye, is inflammation of the conjunctiva, the clear membrane covering the white of the eye. It causes redness, itching, discharge, and tearing. It is usually mild and self-limiting, caused by viruses, bacteria, or allergies. Bacterial forms may need antibiotics. It rarely causes serious complications such as corneal inflammation. Severity is mild.

Allergic conjunctivitis is inflammation of the conjunctiva triggered by allergens like pollen, dust, or pet dander. It causes intense itching, redness, tearing, and usually affects both eyes. It is not contagious and not dangerous. Symptoms are seasonal or persistent. Antihistamine eye drops provide relief. Severity is mild, though severe chronic forms can affect vision if untreated.

Bacterial conjunctivitis is caused by bacteria such as Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, or Haemophilus. It produces thick, sticky yellow or green discharge, crusting of eyelids, and redness. It is highly contagious but usually mild, resolving with antibiotic eye drops within days. Untreated, it rarely spreads to the cornea or deeper eye structures. Severity is mild.

Viral conjunctivitis is the most common form. It is often associated with the common cold or adenovirus. It causes watery discharge, redness, and sensitivity to light and is highly contagious. It resolves on its own in one to two weeks without antibiotics. Severe forms called epidemic keratoconjunctivitis can cause corneal inflammation and vision blurring. Overall severity is mild to moderate.

Connective tissue disorders include autoimmune diseases like lupus, scleroderma, Marfan syndrome, and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. They affect collagen and elastin, causing joint pain, skin changes, and organ involvement in the heart, lungs, and kidneys. Severity ranges from mild with skin and joint symptoms to life-threatening with conditions like aortic dissection in Marfan syndrome or lung problems in scleroderma.

Contact dermatitis is a skin rash caused by direct contact with an irritant such as soap or chemicals, or an allergen such as poison ivy or nickel. It causes redness, itching, blisters, and swelling at the contact site. It is not dangerous and clears within weeks after avoiding the trigger. Topical steroids speed healing. Severity is mild.

Conversion disorder, also called functional neurological symptom disorder, is a psychiatric condition where psychological stress manifests as physical symptoms like blindness, paralysis, seizures, or weakness without a medical cause. Symptoms are real but not due to organic disease. It is not life-threatening but can cause disability. Prognosis is good with psychiatric treatment.

Corneal infection, called keratitis, is inflammation of the cornea. It is often from bacteria, viruses such as herpes, fungi, or parasites like acanthamoeba. It causes eye pain, redness, blurred vision, and light sensitivity. It is a serious condition because it can lead to corneal scarring, perforation, and blindness. Emergent eye care is needed.

A corneal abrasion is a scratch on the clear surface of the eye called the cornea. It causes severe pain, tearing, light sensitivity, and a feeling of something in the eye. Most heal within twenty-four to forty-eight hours with antibiotic drops to prevent infection. Large or infected abrasions can cause corneal ulcers. Severity is mild to moderate.

Corneal disorders include dystrophies such as Fuchs dystrophy and keratoconus, degenerations, and infections. They can cause blurred vision, pain, and light sensitivity. Severity ranges from mild irregular astigmatism correctable with glasses to severe conditions like keratoconus requiring corneal transplant or Fuchs dystrophy leading to blindness. Many are progressive but treatable.

Coronary atherosclerosis is the buildup of plaque in the coronary arteries. This narrows them and reduces blood flow to the heart. It is the underlying cause of angina, heart attack, and sudden cardiac death. It develops silently over decades. Severity ranges from without symptoms mild plaque to life-threatening multi-vessel severe narrowing. It is the leading cause of death worldwide.

Cranial nerve palsy is weakness or paralysis of one or more of the twelve cranial nerves. It can cause double vision from nerves three, four, or six, facial droop from nerve seven, or swallowing difficulty from nerves nine and ten. Causes include diabetes, high blood pressure, trauma, tumor, or inflammation. Many resolve spontaneously within months.

Crohn disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting any part of the digestive tract. It causes abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, and fatigue. It can lead to narrowed areas, abnormal tunnels called fistulas, abscesses, and malnutrition. Severity ranges from mild with limited flare-ups to moderate or severe with frequent flare-ups and complications requiring surgery.

Croup is a viral respiratory infection in young children. It causes swelling of the larynx and trachea, producing a characteristic barking cough, noisy breathing called stridor, and hoarseness. Most croup is mild and managed at home with humidified air. Severe croup causes breathing distress and requires steroids, epinephrine, or hospitalization.

A crushing injury occurs when a body part is subjected to high pressure or force, often by heavy objects in accidents or building collapses. It causes tissue damage, fractures, internal bleeding, nerve injury, and compartment syndrome. Severe crush injuries can lead to crush syndrome, which is release of toxins into the bloodstream after pressure is released, acute kidney injury, and death.

Cryptococcosis is a fungal infection caused by Cryptococcus neoformans. It typically affects the lungs and nervous system. In healthy people, it may be mild or without symptoms. In immunocompromised people such as those with HIV or transplant recipients, it causes cryptococcal meningitis with headache, fever, and confusion, which is life-threatening with high death rate without treatment.

Cryptorchidism is the absence of one or both testicles from the scrotum, also called undescended testicles. It is common in premature infants. Most testicles descend spontaneously by age six months. If not, surgery called orchiopexy is needed by twelve to eighteen months to reduce risks of infertility and testicular cancer. It is not painful or life-threatening.

Cushing syndrome results from prolonged exposure to high cortisol levels. It can be from medication like glucocorticoids or a tumor in the pituitary or adrenal gland. Symptoms include weight gain in the face and trunk, thin skin, easy bruising, high blood pressure, diabetes, and osteoporosis. Untreated, it can cause severe complications like heart attack, stroke, and infections.

An eyelid cyst is a fluid-filled sac on the eyelid. Examples include a chalazion from a blocked oil gland or a sweat gland cyst. Most are painless, benign, and resolve on their own or with warm compresses. Large cysts may cause mechanical irritation or blurred vision. They do not cause serious problems. Severity is mild.

Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disorder causing thick, sticky mucus to build up in the lungs, pancreas, and other organs. This leads to recurrent lung infections, pancreas problems causing poor nutrient absorption, malnutrition, and infertility. It is progressive and incurable. Historically fatal in childhood, modern treatments have extended survival into the forties and fifties, but it remains life-threatening.

Cysticercosis is a parasitic infection caused by the larval stage of the pork tapeworm. When larvae encyst in muscles, skin, or brain, which is called neurocysticercosis, symptoms vary. Muscle cysts are often without symptoms. Brain cysts cause seizures, headaches, and fluid buildup in the brain, and can be fatal if untreated. Severity ranges from mild without symptoms to severe with seizures.

Cystitis is inflammation of the bladder, usually from bacterial infection, which is a urinary tract infection. Symptoms include painful urination, frequent urination, lower abdominal pain, and sometimes blood in the urine. It is mild and resolves with antibiotics. Untreated, it can spread to the kidneys causing pyelonephritis, which is more severe. Severity is mild in most cases.

De Quervain tenosynovitis is painful inflammation of the tendons on the thumb side of the wrist. It causes pain when turning the wrist, making a fist, or grasping objects. It is often due to repetitive hand or wrist movements. Treatment includes splinting, anti-inflammatory medications, and steroid injections. Surgery is rarely needed. Severity is mild to moderate.

Decubitus ulcers, also called pressure sores or bedsores, are injuries to skin and underlying tissue caused by prolonged pressure. They typically occur over bony areas like the sacrum, heels, and elbows in bedridden or immobile patients. Severity ranges from stage one with just redness to stage four with deep tissue loss and bone exposure. Deep ulcers can lead to bone infection, sepsis, and death.

Deep vein thrombosis is a blood clot in a deep vein, usually in the leg. It causes pain, swelling, redness, and warmth. The major danger is that the clot can break off and travel to the lungs, causing a pulmonary embolism, which can be fatal. Deep vein thrombosis itself is moderate, but its complication of pulmonary embolism is life-threatening. Prompt blood thinner treatment is essential.

Degenerative disc disease is age-related wear and tear on the spinal discs that act as cushions between vertebrae. As discs lose hydration and elasticity, they can cause chronic back or neck pain, stiffness, and sometimes nerve compression. It is not life-threatening but can significantly impact mobility and quality of life. Severity ranges from mild occasional discomfort to severe chronic pain.

Delirium is an acute, fluctuating disturbance in attention, awareness, and cognition. It is often caused by medical illness, infection, surgery, medication toxicity, or withdrawal. It develops over hours to days and is characterized by confusion, disorientation, hallucinations, and altered consciousness. Delirium is a medical emergency because it often indicates a serious underlying condition and is associated with increased death rate.

Dementia is a progressive neurodegenerative syndrome characterized by decline in memory, thinking, behavior, and ability to perform daily activities. Alzheimer disease is the most common cause. Severity ranges from mild with forgetting recent events and difficulty with complex tasks to severe with complete dependence, loss of communication, inability to eat, and being bedridden. Late-stage dementia is life-threatening due to complications like pneumonia and infections.

Dengue fever is a mosquito-borne viral infection causing high fever, severe headache, joint and muscle pain, rash, and nausea. Most cases are mild to moderate and resolve in one to two weeks. However, severe dengue, called dengue hemorrhagic fever or dengue shock syndrome, causes bleeding, plasma leakage, low blood pressure, and organ failure, which is life-threatening. Death rate is high without treatment but low with proper care.

Dental caries, also called tooth decay or cavities, is the destruction of tooth enamel caused by bacteria producing acid from sugars. This leads to holes in the teeth. Early caries is mild and without symptoms. Deeper caries causes toothache and sensitivity, and can lead to pulp infection, abscess, or tooth loss if untreated. While generally mild, severe untreated caries can cause a dental abscess that spreads to the neck or brain, which is life-threatening.

Depression, also called major depressive disorder, is a mood disorder causing persistent sadness, loss of interest, changes in appetite and sleep, fatigue, feelings of worthlessness, and difficulty concentrating. Severity ranges from mild with slight impairment to moderate with significant distress to severe with inability to function, psychotic features, and suicidal thoughts. Severe depression carries a risk of suicide, which is life-threatening.

Sun-induced dermatitis includes polymorphous light eruption and photoallergic reactions. It causes an itchy or burning rash, redness, and small bumps on sun-exposed skin. It is not cancerous but can be uncomfortable and come back. Severity is mild, though severe cases may cause blistering or systemic symptoms like fever and chills. Avoiding sun and using sunscreen prevents it. It is not life-threatening.

Developmental disabilities are a diverse group of chronic conditions such as intellectual disability, autism, cerebral palsy, and Down syndrome that arise during development. They affect physical, learning, language, or behavioral functioning. Severity ranges from mild with slight delays and independent living possible to severe with profound impairment requiring full-time care. They are not progressive or directly life-threatening, though severe forms may shorten lifespan due to medical complications.

A deviated nasal septum is a displacement of the wall between the nostrils. It is often present from birth or caused by trauma. It can cause nasal obstruction, frequent sinus infections, nosebleeds, snoring, and sleep apnea. Most cases are mild and without symptoms. Moderate to severe deviation can impair quality of life but is not dangerous. Surgical correction called septoplasty is optional.

Diabetes mellitus, including type one and type two, is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by high blood glucose levels due to insufficient insulin production or resistance. It is a leading cause of blindness, kidney failure, heart attacks, stroke, and lower limb amputation. Severity ranges from well-controlled with mild and near-normal life expectancy to poorly controlled with severe complications and reduced life expectancy. Uncontrolled diabetes can lead to diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar state, which are life-threatening emergencies.

Diabetes insipidus is a rare disorder where the kidneys excrete large amounts of dilute urine. It is due to deficient antidiuretic hormone or kidney resistance to that hormone. Symptoms include excessive thirst and urination. It is not related to diabetes mellitus. If untreated, severe diabetes insipidus can cause dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, and high blood sodium, which can be life-threatening, especially in infants or unconscious patients.

Diabetic ketoacidosis is a life-threatening complication of diabetes, mainly type one, where severe insulin deficiency leads to high blood glucose, accumulation of ketones which are acidic byproducts, and metabolic acidosis. Symptoms include extreme thirst, frequent urination, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, deep rapid breathing called Kussmaul breathing, confusion, and coma. It requires emergency hospitalization with fluids, insulin, and electrolytes.

Diabetic kidney disease, also called diabetic nephropathy, is progressive kidney damage caused by long-standing diabetes. It leads to protein leakage in urine, declining filtration rate, and eventually end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplant. It is the leading cause of dialysis worldwide. Severity ranges from microalbuminuria which is mild and reversible to kidney failure which is life-threatening without dialysis or transplant.

Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is nerve damage caused by chronic high blood sugar. It affects the feet and hands. Symptoms include numbness, tingling, burning pain, and loss of sensation. It is not life-threatening but can lead to foot ulcers, infections, and amputations due to unnoticed injuries. Severity ranges from mild discomfort to severe pain and disability. Managing blood sugar and pain medications are key treatments.

Diabetic retinopathy is damage to the retina blood vessels caused by long-term diabetes. It leads to vision loss and blindness. Non-proliferative retinopathy causes leaky vessels and is mild to moderate. Proliferative retinopathy grows abnormal vessels that bleed or cause retinal detachment and is severe. It is treatable with laser, injections, or surgery. Strict glucose control prevents progression.

Diaper rash, also called diaper dermatitis, is a common skin irritation in infants caused by prolonged contact with urine, feces, or friction. It presents as red, inflamed skin in the diaper area. Most cases are mild and resolve with frequent diaper changes, barrier creams, and air exposure. Severe cases may become infected with yeast, requiring antifungal cream. It is not dangerous or life-threatening.

Ankle dislocation occurs when the talus bone separates from the tibia and fibula. It is often due to trauma such as falls, sports, or car accidents. It causes severe pain, deformity, swelling, and inability to bear weight. It is often associated with fractures and ligament tears. Emergency reduction is needed. While not life-threatening, it can lead to long-term instability, arthritis, and nerve or vessel damage if untreated.

Elbow dislocation is separation of the bones of the elbow joint, which are the humerus, radius, and ulna. It is usually from a fall on an outstretched hand. It causes severe pain, deformity, and inability to move the elbow. It can be simple with no fracture or complex with fracture and ligament damage. Emergency reduction is required. Complications include nerve damage, stiffness, and recurrent dislocation.

Finger dislocation occurs when the bones of a finger joint, usually the middle knuckle, separate. It is often from a sports or impact injury. The finger appears crooked, swollen, and painful. Reduction, which is relocating the joint, is performed by a doctor. It is rarely serious, though ligament tears or fractures may occur. Most heal well with splinting and therapy.

Foot dislocation involves displacement of bones within the foot. Examples include Lisfranc dislocation of the midfoot or subtalar dislocation. It is usually from high-energy trauma like car crashes or falls. It causes severe pain, swelling, deformity, and inability to walk. Loss of blood flow to the foot can occur, requiring emergency reduction. Long-term outcomes include arthritis and chronic pain.

Hip dislocation is displacement of the femoral head from the acetabulum. It is usually from high-energy trauma like car accidents or falls from height. It causes severe pain, inability to move the leg, and the leg may be shortened and rotated. The most common type is posterior dislocation, which risks sciatic nerve injury and death of the femoral head bone. Emergency reduction under sedation is required. It is a serious injury with potential for permanent disability or joint replacement.

Knee dislocation is a rare but serious injury where the tibia and femur separate. It is often from high-energy trauma but can occur from low-energy in obese individuals. It causes severe pain, deformity, and instability. Crucially, it can damage the artery behind the knee, risking limb loss, and nerves. It is an orthopedic emergency requiring immediate reduction and assessment of blood vessels.

Patellar dislocation occurs when the kneecap slips out of its groove at the knee joint, usually to the outside. It is often from twisting injuries. It causes severe pain, inability to straighten the knee, and visible deformity. Most reduce spontaneously or with gentle extension. First-time dislocations may be treated without surgery, while recurrent dislocations may require surgery. There is risk of future instability and cartilage damage.

Shoulder dislocation is separation of the humeral head from the glenoid cavity. It is usually anterior, meaning forward, from trauma or sports. It causes severe pain, inability to move the arm, and a squared-off shoulder appearance. Emergency reduction is needed. Complications include recurrent dislocations, rotator cuff tears, nerve injury, and arthritis.

Vertebral dislocation, also called spinal dislocation, is displacement of one vertebra relative to another. It is often from high-energy trauma like car accidents or falls. It can cause spinal cord compression, leading to paralysis of the legs or all four limbs, loss of bladder and bowel control, and respiratory failure if the neck is involved. This is a life-threatening and life-altering injury requiring emergency spinal stabilization and sometimes surgery.

Wrist dislocation, such as lunate or perilunate dislocation, occurs from high-energy falls or impacts. It causes severe pain, swelling, deformity, and limited motion. It can damage the median and ulnar nerves, causing carpal tunnel syndrome, and the blood supply to the lunate bone, causing bone death. Emergency reduction and often surgery are required. Without treatment, chronic pain, arthritis, and disability result.

Dissociative disorders involve a disconnection between thoughts, identity, consciousness, and memory. They often occur as a response to trauma. Types include dissociative amnesia, depersonalization and derealization disorder, and dissociative identity disorder. Severity ranges from mild with episodes of zoning out to moderate with significant memory gaps and identity alteration. It is not life-threatening but can impair functioning.

Diverticulitis is inflammation or infection of small pouches called diverticula that can form in the wall of the colon. It causes abdominal pain, usually in the left lower side, fever, nausea, and change in bowel habits. Mild diverticulitis is treated with antibiotics and diet. Severe cases can cause abscess, perforation, widespread abdominal infection, or abnormal tunnel, which are surgical emergencies and life-threatening.

Diverticulosis is the presence of small bulging pouches called diverticula in the colon wall. It is common in older adults. Most people have no symptoms. It is generally harmless but can lead to diverticulitis or bleeding. Bleeding can be heavy requiring transfusion but is rarely life-threatening. Severity is mild in most cases.

Down syndrome, also called trisomy twenty-one, is a genetic disorder causing intellectual disability, characteristic facial features, and increased risk of congenital heart defects, leukemia, early Alzheimer disease, and other medical issues. Severity varies. Mild cases have moderate intellectual disability and few medical problems. Severe cases have significant heart defects or other organ problems that can be life-threatening in infancy. Life expectancy is now fifty to sixty years with proper medical care.

Drug abuse, also called substance use disorder, is the harmful or hazardous use of psychoactive substances. It leads to addiction, health problems, mental disorders, accidents, overdose, and death. Severity ranges from mild with use causing mild impairment to severe with loss of control, tolerance, withdrawal, and major life disruption. Overdose of opioids, cocaine, or stimulants can be life-threatening. Long-term abuse causes organ damage and infectious diseases.

Barbiturate abuse involves misuse of sedative-hypnotic medications such as phenobarbital and pentobarbital. It causes sedation, slow breathing, and addiction. Overdose leads to coma, respiratory arrest, and death. Withdrawal can be life-threatening, causing seizures and delirium. Medical detoxification is necessary.

Cocaine abuse is a stimulant use disorder causing euphoria and increased energy. It also causes severe cardiovascular effects including heart attack, stroke, aortic dissection, seizures, and sudden death. Chronic use leads to addiction, cognitive impairment, and nasal septal perforation. Overdose causes dangerously high body temperature, seizures, and cardiac arrest.

Methamphetamine abuse is addiction to a potent stimulant. It causes severe physical and psychological harm including dental decay called meth mouth, skin sores, weight loss, psychosis, violent behavior, and cardiovascular collapse. Overdose causes dangerously high body temperature, seizures, stroke, cardiac arrest, and death. Chronic use leads to severe brain damage and organ failure.

Opioid abuse involves heroin or prescription painkillers like oxycodone. It causes addiction, slow breathing, constipation, and overdose. Opioid overdose is life-threatening, causing pinpoint pupils, unconsciousness, and respiratory arrest. It is reversible with naloxone. Chronic use leads to infectious diseases from needle sharing and death. It is a leading cause of accidental death worldwide.

Drug poisoning occurs from taking excessive or wrong medication. It can be accidental, especially in children or elderly, or intentional such as overdose. Symptoms depend on the drug. Acetaminophen causes liver failure. Anticoagulants cause bleeding. Beta-blockers cause slow heart rate and shock. Severity ranges from mild nausea to life-threatening coma, organ failure, and death. It requires emergency medical evaluation and sometimes antidotes.

Drug reactions are adverse effects from medications. They range from mild rashes to severe, life-threatening conditions like Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, or anaphylaxis. Most are mild, causing hives, itching, and nausea, and resolve when the drug is stopped. Severe reactions cause widespread skin sloughing, organ failure, and death. Severity is highly variable.

Drug withdrawal is the set of symptoms that occur when a person stops or reduces use of a substance they are dependent on. Withdrawal from alcohol or benzodiazepines can be life-threatening, causing seizures and delirium tremens. Opioid withdrawal is extremely uncomfortable but rarely fatal, though dangerous in people with other medical conditions. Severity ranges from mild anxiety and sweating to severe seizures and heart instability.

Dry eye disease, also called keratoconjunctivitis sicca, is a condition of insufficient tear production or poor tear quality. It leads to burning, stinging, gritty sensation, and intermittent blurry vision. When the cause is unknown, it is chronic but not dangerous. Severe dry eye can cause corneal abrasions, ulcers, and vision impairment but is rarely life-threatening.

Dumping syndrome is a condition that occurs after gastric surgery. It happens when food moves too quickly from the stomach to the small intestine. It causes nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dizziness, and rapid heartbeat after eating. Early dumping occurs within thirty minutes. Late dumping, which is low blood sugar, occurs after one to three hours. It is not life-threatening but can be debilitating.

Dyshidrosis, also called dyshidrotic eczema or pompholyx, is a skin condition causing small, deep, itchy blisters on the palms of hands and soles of feet. It is often triggered by stress, allergies, or heat. It is not contagious or dangerous but can be very itchy and come back. Blisters dry and peel over two to four weeks. Treatment includes topical steroids and moisturizers.
