Dysthymic disorder, also called persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic form of depression lasting at least two years. It has less severe symptoms than major depression, including low energy, poor self-esteem, and hopelessness. It is not life-threatening but impairs quality of life and can lead to major depression. Treatment includes psychotherapy and antidepressants.

Eardrum damage, also called tympanic membrane perforation, is a hole or tear in the thin membrane separating the ear canal from the middle ear. It is caused by infection, trauma, loud noise, or pressure changes. Symptoms include pain, hearing loss, ringing in the ears, and sometimes drainage. Most small perforations heal spontaneously within weeks. Large or infected perforations may need surgical repair.

Earwax impaction, also called cerumen impaction, occurs when wax accumulates in the ear canal, blocking sound transmission. It causes hearing loss, earache, ringing in the ears called tinnitus, and dizziness. It is not dangerous but can be uncomfortable. Removal by irrigation, curette, or softening drops resolves the issue.

Eating disorders include anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, and binge-eating disorder. They are serious mental illnesses with severe physical consequences. Anorexia has the highest death rate of any psychiatric disorder due to starvation, heart arrhythmias, electrolyte imbalances, and suicide. Bulimia causes electrolyte disturbances, esophageal rupture, and dental erosion. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening. Early treatment is crucial.

Ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized egg implants outside the uterus, typically in a fallopian tube. It causes abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, and if not diagnosed, the tube can rupture, causing massive bleeding inside the abdomen, shock, and death. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate surgery or medication. Rupture has significant death rate.

Ectropion is the outward turning of the lower eyelid. This causes the inner surface to be exposed, leading to tearing, irritation, redness, and dryness. It can be age-related, due to paralysis, or due to scarring. If severe, it can cause corneal drying and ulceration, risking vision loss. Surgical repair is effective.

Eczema, also called atopic dermatitis, is a chronic inflammatory skin condition causing dry, itchy, red, and scaly patches. It is very common in children. It is not contagious or life-threatening but causes significant discomfort and sleep disturbance. Severe cases can become infected with bacteria or herpes, requiring antibiotics. Severity ranges from mild with small patches to moderate with widespread rash impacting quality of life.

Edwards syndrome, also called trisomy eighteen, is a severe genetic disorder caused by an extra chromosome eighteen. It leads to profound intellectual disability, low birth weight, heart defects, kidney malformations, and clenched hands with overlapping fingers. Most affected infants die within the first year of life, with a median of five to fifteen days. Few survive to adulthood.

Emphysema is a form of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease where the air sacs in the lungs are progressively destroyed. This reduces oxygen exchange and causes shortness of breath. It is usually caused by smoking. It is not curable and worsens over time, leading to respiratory failure, heart problems, and death. Severity ranges from mild with shortness of breath on exertion to severe requiring oxygen and having life-threatening flare-ups.

Empyema is a collection of pus in the pleural space, which is between the lung and chest wall. It is usually a complication of pneumonia, lung abscess, or chest trauma. Symptoms include fever, chest pain, cough, and shortness of breath. It requires antibiotics and drainage through a chest tube or surgery. Without treatment, it can cause sepsis, respiratory failure, and death.

Encephalitis is inflammation of the brain tissue. It is usually caused by viral infection such as herpes simplex or West Nile virus, or by autoimmune reactions. Symptoms include fever, headache, confusion, seizures, personality changes, and focal neurological deficits. It is a medical emergency. Severe cases cause coma, brain damage, and death. Even with treatment, death rate is significant, and survivors may have permanent neurological deficits.

Endocarditis is infection of the heart inner lining, called the endocardium, and heart valves. It is usually caused by bacteria such as Staphylococcus or Streptococcus. It causes fever, heart murmur, weight loss, and systemic emboli leading to stroke, kidney damage, or septic embolism. It is a serious condition with high death rate if untreated, requiring prolonged intravenous antibiotics and sometimes valve surgery.

Endometrial cancer is cancer of the lining of the uterus, called the endometrium. It usually presents with postmenopausal bleeding. It is often diagnosed early because of this symptom, and early-stage cancer has a high cure rate with over ninety-five percent five-year survival with hysterectomy. Advanced or metastatic endometrial cancer is more serious with lower survival. Severity ranges from moderate for early curable cases to life-threatening for advanced cases.

Endometrial hyperplasia is thickening of the uterine lining due to excess estrogen without enough progesterone. It can cause abnormal bleeding. Simple hyperplasia without abnormal cells is benign and mild. Complex hyperplasia with abnormal cells is precancerous with moderate risk of progressing to endometrial cancer. Severity ranges from mild for benign cases to moderate for precancerous cases requiring progesterone or hysterectomy.

Endometriosis occurs when endometrial-like tissue grows outside the uterus, such as on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, or pelvis. It causes chronic pelvic pain, painful periods, pain during intercourse, and infertility. It is not cancerous or life-threatening but can significantly impair quality of life and fertility. Severity ranges from mild with occasional pain to severe with chronic debilitating pain and extensive scar tissue.

Endophthalmitis is a severe inflammation of the interior of the eye. It is usually due to bacterial or fungal infection after eye surgery, penetrating trauma, or spread from the bloodstream. It causes severe eye pain, redness, vision loss, and can lead to blindness or loss of the eye. It is an eye emergency requiring injection of antibiotics into the eye and sometimes surgery to remove infected fluid.

disease_name: envenomation from spider or animal bite
description: Envenomation occurs when venom is injected by a bite or sting from spiders, snakes, scorpions, or insects. Severity depends on the species. Black widow spider causes severe pain and muscle spasms. Brown recluse spider causes necrotic skin lesions. Snake bites from rattlesnakes or cobras can cause tissue destruction, bleeding disorders, paralysis, and death. Antivenom is available for some. Severity ranges from mild local pain to life-threatening anaphylaxis or hemorrhage.

Ependymoma is a rare tumor of the central nervous system. It arises from ependymal cells lining the ventricles of the brain or the spinal cord. Most are low-grade and moderate, treatable with surgical removal and sometimes radiation. High-grade anaplastic ependymoma is more aggressive. Spinal ependymomas may cause weakness or numbness. Brain ependymomas cause headaches and fluid buildup in the brain.

Epididymitis is inflammation of the epididymis, which is the coiled tube at the back of the testicle. It is often caused by bacterial infection, such as sexually transmitted infections like chlamydia or gonorrhea in young men, or urinary tract infections in older men. Symptoms include testicular pain, swelling, fever, and urinary symptoms. It is usually mild and resolves with antibiotics and anti-inflammatory medications. Severe cases may cause abscess or chronic pain.

Epidural hemorrhage, also called epidural hematoma, is bleeding between the skull and the outermost layer of the brain covering, called the dura mater. It is usually due to a skull fracture tearing an artery. It causes a lucid interval, which is brief consciousness followed by rapid neurological decline, then severe headache, vomiting, one-sided weakness, loss of consciousness, and death if not urgently removed via surgery. This is a life-threatening emergency.

Epilepsy is a neurological disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures due to abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Severity ranges from mild with brief staring spells or focal seizures easily controlled with medication to severe with frequent generalized seizures or status epilepticus, which is prolonged seizure activity that is life-threatening. Most people with epilepsy live normal lives with treatment.

Erectile dysfunction is the inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for satisfactory sexual activity. It is not life-threatening but can be a marker for underlying cardiovascular disease, diabetes, or hormonal disorders. Severity ranges from mild with occasional difficulty to severe with complete inability. Treatment includes oral medications, injections, vacuum devices, or implants.

Erythema multiforme is an immune-mediated skin reaction. It is often triggered by infections, especially herpes simplex, or medications. It causes target-like red patches on the skin, often on the hands, feet, face, and sometimes the mouth lining. Erythema multiforme minor is mild and resolves in weeks. Erythema multiforme major, which overlaps with Stevens-Johnson syndrome, involves mucous membranes and can be severe.

Esophageal cancer is a malignancy of the esophagus. The two main types are squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma. It causes progressive difficulty swallowing, weight loss, and chest pain. It is often diagnosed at advanced stages because early symptoms are subtle. Overall five-year survival is poor at less than twenty percent, making it life-threatening. Early-stage cancer confined to the lining has better outcomes.

Esophageal varices are abnormally dilated veins in the lower esophagus. They are usually due to high blood pressure in the portal vein from cirrhosis. They are without symptoms until they rupture, causing massive, life-threatening upper gastrointestinal bleeding with vomiting blood or black tarry stools. Ruptured varices have a high death rate. Treatment includes endoscopic banding, injection, and medications to reduce portal pressure.

Esophagitis is inflammation of the esophagus. It is commonly due to gastroesophageal reflux disease, infections such as Candida, herpes, or CMV, or medications causing pill esophagitis. Symptoms include heartburn, painful swallowing, and chest pain. Most cases are mild and respond to acid suppression or treatment of the cause. Severe erosive esophagitis can cause bleeding, narrowed areas, or Barrett esophagus, which is a precancerous condition.

Essential tremor is a neurological disorder causing involuntary, rhythmic shaking. It most often affects the hands, head, or voice, especially during action. It is not life-threatening but can progress and interfere with daily activities like eating and writing. Severity ranges from mild and barely noticeable to moderate with significant disability. Treatment includes beta-blocker medications or deep brain stimulation for severe cases.

Eustachian tube dysfunction occurs when the tube connecting the middle ear to the back of the throat fails to open or close properly. It causes ear fullness, popping, muffled hearing, and sometimes pain or ringing in the ears. It is very common, often triggered by allergies, colds, or air travel. Most cases are mild and resolve spontaneously or with decongestants or nasal steroids. Chronic cases may require ear tubes.

Extrapyramidal symptoms are medication-induced movement disorders. They are most commonly caused by antipsychotic medications that block dopamine. Symptoms include acute dystonia which is muscle spasms, akathisia which is restlessness, parkinsonism which is rigidity and tremor, and tardive dyskinesia which is involuntary mouth and tongue movements. These symptoms are not life-threatening but can be distressing and disfiguring. Acute reactions are reversible with medication, but tardive dyskinesia may be permanent.

Eye alignment disorders, also called strabismus, occur when the eyes are not properly aligned. This causes double vision or lazy eye, called amblyopia. Types include esotropia which is inward turning, exotropia which is outward turning, and hypertropia which is vertical misalignment. It is common in children and can be corrected with glasses, patching, exercises, or surgery. It is not life-threatening but can impair vision if untreated in childhood.

Factitious disorder, also called Munchausen syndrome, is a mental illness where a person deliberately fakes, exaggerates, or induces physical or psychological symptoms to assume the sick role. This occurs without external incentives, unlike malingering. It can cause harm from unnecessary medical procedures. It is not directly life-threatening but can lead to complications from self-induced illness. Treatment is psychotherapy, which is often challenging.

Female genital infections include vaginitis from bacterial vaginosis, yeast, or trichomoniasis, cervicitis, and vulvitis. Symptoms include discharge, itching, odor, burning, and pain. Most are mild and easily treated with topical or oral antibiotics or antifungals. Untreated, some can spread upward to cause pelvic inflammatory disease, infertility, or ectopic pregnancy.

Female infertility of unknown cause, also called unexplained infertility, is the diagnosis when standard fertility testing for ovulation, fallopian tube patency, and semen analysis is normal but pregnancy has not occurred after twelve months of trying. It is not a disease but a label of exclusion. It is not life-threatening but emotionally distressing. Many eventually conceive with assisted reproduction such as IUI or IVF.

Fetal alcohol syndrome is a group of physical, behavioral, and cognitive abnormalities caused by maternal alcohol consumption during pregnancy. Features include growth retardation, distinct facial features such as a smooth philtrum and thin upper lip, intellectual disability, and behavioral problems. Severity ranges from mild to severe. It is not progressive but is permanent and not curable. Life expectancy may be reduced due to associated health problems.

Fibroadenoma is a benign, solid breast tumor. It is most common in young women. It feels like a smooth, firm, rubbery, movable lump. It is not cancerous and does not increase breast cancer risk, except the complex type slightly increases risk. Many resolve spontaneously or remain stable. Removal is for symptoms or patient preference.

Fibrocystic breast changes are benign, non-cancerous lumps, cysts, and fibrous tissue in the breasts. They often cause pain, tenderness, and lumpiness that change with the menstrual cycle. It is very common and not dangerous. It does not significantly increase breast cancer risk. Management includes supportive bras, vitamin E, and rarely cyst drainage.

Fibromyalgia is a chronic disorder characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, and cognitive difficulties called fibro fog. It is not life-threatening or progressive, but can severely reduce quality of life. Severity ranges from mild and manageable with lifestyle changes to moderate or severe and disabling, requiring multidisciplinary care. Treatment includes exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy, antidepressants, and nerve pain medications.

Flat feet, also called fallen arches, is a condition where the arch of the foot collapses, causing the entire sole to contact the ground. It is often hereditary or acquired. Many people have no symptoms. When painful, it causes foot pain, arch pain, and altered walking pattern. It is not dangerous. Treatment includes supportive shoes, orthotics, and stretching.

Floaters are small specks, strings, or cobwebs that drift across your field of vision. They are caused by age-related changes in the vitreous humor of the eye. Most are benign and mild. However, a sudden increase in floaters, especially with flashes of light or a shadow in vision, may indicate retinal tear or detachment, which is an emergency.

Influenza, commonly called the flu, is a contagious viral respiratory illness causing fever, cough, sore throat, muscle aches, and fatigue. Most healthy people recover in one to two weeks with mild illness. However, severe cases can cause pneumonia, respiratory failure, worsening of chronic conditions, and death, especially in elderly, infants, pregnant women, and immunocompromised individuals. Annual vaccination reduces severity.

Fluid overload, also called hypervolemia, is an excess of fluid in the blood and tissues. It is usually due to heart failure, kidney failure, liver cirrhosis, or excessive intravenous fluids. Symptoms include swelling called edema, shortness of breath called pulmonary edema, and weight gain. Severe fluid overload causes breathing distress and death from fluid in the lungs. It requires diuretics, fluid restriction, and treatment of the underlying cause.

Folate, which is vitamin B9, deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia with large, immature red blood cells, fatigue, weakness, paleness, and mouth sores. In pregnancy, it causes neural tube defects in the fetus. It is mild and reversible with oral folate supplements and dietary changes such as leafy greens and legumes. Severe deficiency can worsen heart disease due to high homocysteine levels.

Food allergy is an immune reaction to certain foods such as peanuts, shellfish, eggs, and milk. Symptoms range from mild with hives, itching, and swelling to severe, life-threatening anaphylaxis with difficulty breathing, drop in blood pressure, and loss of consciousness. Most food allergies are mild, but those with a history of anaphylaxis need emergency epinephrine. Severity is highly variable.

A foreign body in the ear includes beads, insects, or cotton swab tips. It causes pain, hearing loss, and sometimes infection or eardrum perforation. Most are not emergencies unless sharp, if they are button batteries which require urgent removal due to tissue death, or live insects. Removal by irrigation, suction, or instruments is needed. Severity is mild for most objects, but button batteries are medical emergencies causing severe burns.

A foreign body in the eye, such as dust, metal, or sand, causes pain, tearing, redness, and a scratching sensation. Most superficial foreign bodies are flushed out or removed with a cotton swab. Penetrating foreign bodies from high-velocity metal are emergencies and can cause vision loss. Severity ranges from mild for superficial to severe for penetrating. Most are mild.

disease_name: foreign body in the gastrointestinal tract
description: Swallowed foreign bodies include coins, batteries, magnets, and bones. Most pass spontaneously and are mild. Sharp objects like needles or fish bones risk perforation. Button batteries and multiple magnets are emergencies. Batteries cause tissue death in the esophagus within hours. Magnets attract across the bowel wall causing perforation or abnormal tunnel. Severity ranges from mild for smooth small objects to severe or life-threatening for batteries, magnets, sharp objects, or obstruction.

Nasal foreign bodies such as beans, beads, or small toys are common in children. They cause nasal obstruction, foul-smelling discharge, and sometimes bleeding. Most are mild, but button batteries are emergencies due to rapid tissue death and perforation of the nasal septum. Small magnets are also dangerous. Removal is typically done by an ENT doctor. Severity is mild for most, but button batteries and magnets are severe.

Throat foreign bodies such as fish bones, chicken bones, or dentures cause pain, difficulty swallowing, drooling, and sometimes airway blockage if in the voice box. Foreign bodies in the esophagus can cause perforation or abscess. Sharp objects risk infection in the chest cavity. Urgent endoscopy is often needed. Severity ranges from mild for small smooth objects to severe for sharp, impacted, or those causing airway blockage.

Vaginal foreign bodies such as retained tampons, condoms, or sex toys can cause foul-smelling discharge, pain, bleeding, and infection. Toxic shock syndrome from a retained tampon is rare but life-threatening. Most are mild and removed in the office. Severity is mild for most, with toxic shock syndrome as a severe complication.

Ankle fracture is a break in one or more bones of the ankle joint, including the tibia, fibula, or talus. It is usually from a twisting injury or fall. Symptoms include pain, swelling, bruising, and inability to bear weight. Most are stable and moderate, treated with casting. Unstable or displaced fractures require surgery. Complications include post-traumatic arthritis and improper healing.

Arm fractures include breaks in the humerus of the upper arm, and the radius and ulna of the forearm. They occur from falls, direct trauma, or sports. Symptoms include pain, deformity, swelling, and loss of function. Most are stable and moderate, treated with casting or splinting. Displaced or joint surface fractures require surgery. Complications include nerve injury, improper healing, and compartment syndrome.

Facial bone fractures include breaks in the nasal, maxillary, zygomatic, orbital, and mandible bones. They result from blunt trauma such as assaults, falls, or car accidents. They cause pain, swelling, bruising, deformity, and sometimes vision problems, double vision, or teeth misalignment. Most are moderate. However, certain patterns can be severe with airway blockage or fluid leakage from the brain. Fractures involving the sinuses risk meningitis.

Finger fractures, also called phalangeal fractures, are common from jamming or crushing injuries. They cause pain, swelling, bruising, and deformity. Most are mild, treated by buddy taping or splinting. Displaced or joint surface fractures may need reduction or pinning. Improper healing can cause stiffness or deformity.

Foot fractures include toe, metatarsal, and calcaneus or heel fractures. Toe fractures are very common and mild, treated with buddy taping. Metatarsal stress fractures are mild but require rest. Calcaneus fractures from high-energy falls are more severe and often require surgery. Severity ranges from mild for toes to moderate for calcaneus fractures.

Hand fractures include breaks in the metacarpals and carpal bones. They often result from punching or crushing injuries. Boxer fracture of the fifth metacarpal neck is common. Most are stable and mild, treated with splinting or casting. Displaced fractures, especially of the scaphoid bone, may require surgery. Scaphoid nonunion can cause arthritis.

Jaw, or mandible, fractures result from facial trauma such as assaults, falls, or sports. Symptoms include pain, teeth not aligning properly, inability to open the mouth fully, and numbness of the lower lip from nerve injury. Most require surgery to fix the bone with plates or wiring the jaws shut. Complications include nonunion, infection, and airway blockage if displaced.

Leg fractures, specifically of the tibia and fibula, are from high-energy trauma such as car accidents, falls, or sports. Tibial shaft fractures are common and often require surgery with an intramedullary nail. Complications include compartment syndrome which is an emergency, improper healing, and delayed healing. Fibula fractures alone are mild. Severity ranges from mild for nondisplaced fibula to severe for open tibial fracture or compartment syndrome.

Neck fractures, also called cervical spine fractures, result from high-energy trauma such as car accidents, diving, or falls. They can cause spinal cord injury, paralysis of all four limbs, respiratory failure, and death. Immobilization and emergency neurosurgical evaluation are critical. Stable fractures of the spinous process are moderate. Unstable fractures such as odontoid, teardrop, or burst fractures are life-threatening.

Patella, or kneecap, fracture occurs from a direct blow or forceful contraction of the quadriceps muscle such as falling on a bent knee. Symptoms include swelling, inability to straighten the knee, and a palpable gap. Nondisplaced fractures are moderate, treated with casting. Displaced fractures with inability to straighten the knee require surgery. Complications include post-traumatic arthritis and quadriceps weakness.

Pelvic fractures are from high-energy trauma such as car accidents or falls from height, or low-energy in elderly with osteoporosis. Stable pelvic fractures such as avulsion or isolated ramus fractures are moderate. Unstable fractures such as vertical shear or open-book fractures are life-threatening due to massive bleeding, organ injury to the bladder or urethra, and shock. These require urgent stabilization and sometimes embolization to stop bleeding.

Rib fractures result from blunt chest trauma such as falls, cough, or CPR. One or two ribs without complications are mild, treated with pain control. Multiple rib fractures with flail chest, which is three or more ribs broken in two places causing paradoxical chest wall movement, cause respiratory failure and are life-threatening. Pneumothorax, hemothorax, or lung bruising can occur. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening depending on the number and complications.

Shoulder fractures include breaks in the clavicle, scapula, and proximal humerus. Clavicle fractures are very common from falls on an outstretched hand. They are usually moderate, treated with a sling. Scapular fractures are rare, from high-energy trauma, and often associated with chest injuries. Proximal humerus fractures range from nondisplaced which are moderate to displaced which are severe and may require surgery. Complications include nerve injury, stiffness, and bone death of the humeral head.

Skull fractures result from significant head trauma such as falls, assaults, or car accidents. Linear or simple fractures are moderate if there is no underlying brain injury. Depressed fractures push bone inward, risking brain injury and require surgical elevation. Basilar skull fractures at the base of the skull cause bruising around the eyes, bruising behind the ears, and leakage of brain fluid from the nose or ears, and risk meningitis. Severity ranges from moderate to life-threatening if associated with bleeding inside the skull or brain injury.

Vertebral fractures can be compression, burst, or fracture-dislocation. Compression fractures from osteoporosis or minor trauma are moderate, causing back pain, treated with bracing. Burst fractures from high-energy involve bone pushing backward into the spinal canal, causing spinal cord injury and paralysis. Fracture-dislocations are unstable and often cause complete spinal cord injury, which is life-altering and life-threatening if in the neck due to respiratory failure. Severity ranges from moderate to life-threatening.

Friedreich ataxia is a genetic, degenerative neurological disorder causing progressive loss of coordination called ataxia, muscle weakness, and sensory loss. It also causes heart enlargement and failure and diabetes. It is not curable and progresses over decades, leading to wheelchair dependence and reduced lifespan, typically thirty to fifty years. Death is often from heart failure or complications of immobility.

Frostbite is freezing of skin and underlying tissues due to extreme cold exposure. It causes numbness, white or blue skin, and hardness. Mild frostnip is reversible. Deep frostbite causes blisters, tissue death, and can lead to gangrene and amputation. Severity ranges from mild for superficial to severe for deep, requiring removal of dead tissue or amputation. It is not immediately life-threatening unless associated with dangerously low body temperature.

Fungal hair infection, called tinea capitis, is a scalp infection caused by dermatophytes. It causes scaly patches, hair loss, and sometimes painful, swollen lymph nodes. It is common in children. It is not dangerous and is treated with oral antifungal medications because topical treatments are ineffective. Complications include permanent hair loss or a large inflammatory mass called a kerion.

Fungal skin infections, called tinea corporis, tinea pedis, and tinea cruris, cause ring-shaped, scaly, itchy rashes. They are not dangerous and respond to topical antifungal creams. Severe or widespread infections, or those in immunocompromised people, may require oral antifungal medications.

G6PD deficiency is an inherited enzyme deficiency affecting red blood cells. It makes them vulnerable to oxidative stress. Affected individuals are usually without symptoms until exposed to certain drugs, infections, or fava beans, which trigger sudden breakdown of red blood cells. Most episodes are mild to moderate and self-limited. Severe cases cause significant anemia requiring blood transfusion.

Galactorrhea is milk production from the breast unrelated to childbirth or nursing. It is often due to elevated prolactin, called hyperprolactinemia. Idiopathic cases have no identified cause. It is not dangerous, though it may cause menstrual irregularities or infertility. Treatment with dopamine agonists may be offered if bothersome.

Gallstones are hardened deposits of digestive fluid, mostly cholesterol or bilirubin, in the gallbladder. Most are without symptoms and mild. Symptomatic gallstones cause episodic right upper abdominal pain called biliary colic. Complications such as gallbladder inflammation, bile duct stones, or pancreatitis are moderate to severe.

A ganglion cyst is a fluid-filled lump arising from a joint or tendon sheath. It is most common on the wrist, hand, or foot. It is benign, often painless, and may change size. It is not dangerous. Treatment is observation or drainage. Surgery is for persistent pain.

Gas gangrene, also called clostridial myonecrosis, is a rapidly progressive, life-threatening bacterial infection caused by Clostridium perfringens. It produces gas and toxins that cause muscle death, systemic toxicity, and septic shock. It typically occurs after deep, dirty wounds or surgery. Symptoms include severe pain, swelling, crackling sensation of gas under the skin, and rapidly spreading black, foul-smelling tissue. Requires emergency surgical removal of dead tissue, antibiotics, and hyperbaric oxygen. Death rate is high even with treatment.

Gastritis is inflammation of the stomach lining. It is often from H. pylori infection, NSAIDs, alcohol, or stress. Symptoms include upper abdominal pain, nausea, bloating, and feeling full. Acute gastritis is mild and self-limiting. Chronic gastritis can lead to ulcers or stomach cancer if caused by H. pylori or autoimmune disease. Severity is mild to moderate, with rare severe bleeding.

Gastroduodenal ulcers, also called peptic ulcers, are open sores in the stomach or duodenum. They are often from H. pylori or NSAIDs. Symptoms include burning upper abdominal pain, bloating, and nausea. Most are moderate and heal with antibiotics and acid suppression. Complications include bleeding which is life-threatening, perforation which causes life-threatening abdominal infection, or obstruction which blocks food passage.

GERD is chronic acid reflux causing heartburn, regurgitation, chest pain, and cough. Most cases are mild and controlled with lifestyle changes and antacids or acid reducers. Severe chronic GERD can cause inflammation of the esophagus, narrowing, Barrett esophagus which is a precancerous condition, and rarely esophageal adenocarcinoma which is life-threatening. Severity ranges from mild to moderate.

Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is bleeding anywhere from the mouth to the anus. Upper bleeding comes from the esophagus, stomach, or duodenum. Lower bleeding comes from the colon or rectum. Causes include ulcers, varicose veins in the esophagus, diverticula, hemorrhoids, and cancer. Symptoms include vomiting blood, black tarry stool, bright red blood in stool, and signs of shock such as low blood pressure and fast heart rate. Significant bleeding is a medical emergency and can be life-threatening.

Gastroparesis is delayed emptying of the stomach without a physical blockage. It is often due to diabetes, after surgery, or unknown causes. Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, feeling full quickly after starting to eat, bloating, and abdominal pain. It is not life-threatening but can cause malnutrition and dehydration. Severe cases require prokinetic drugs, dietary changes, or a gastric pacemaker.

Genital herpes is a sexually transmitted infection caused by HSV-2 or HSV-1. It causes painful blisters or ulcers in the genital area, which recur periodically. It is not curable but manageable. It is not life-threatening but increases HIV transmission risk. Severe cases occur in newborns, called neonatal herpes, or immunocompromised people. For most adults, severity is mild to moderate due to pain and stigma.

Gestational diabetes is glucose intolerance first recognized during pregnancy, usually in the second or third trimester. It is usually mild and managed with diet, exercise, or insulin. However, untreated, it increases risks for a large baby, birth trauma, preeclampsia, and low blood sugar in the newborn. Most cases resolve after delivery but increase future risk of type two diabetes.

Glaucoma is a group of eye diseases that damage the optic nerve. It is usually due to increased pressure inside the eye, leading to irreversible vision loss and blindness if untreated. Open-angle chronic glaucoma is without symptoms until late. Acute angle-closure glaucoma is an emergency. It is treatable with drops, laser, or surgery to prevent further loss, but lost vision cannot be restored. Severity ranges from mild if detected early to severe if advanced with blindness.

Glucocorticoid deficiency, also called adrenal insufficiency, means the adrenal glands produce insufficient cortisol. It causes fatigue, weight loss, low blood pressure, nausea, and darkening of the skin. Acute adrenal crisis, triggered by stress or surgery, is life-threatening, causing shock and electrolyte disturbances. It is manageable with lifelong steroid replacement.

Goiter is an abnormal enlargement of the thyroid gland. It can be diffuse or have nodules. It is usually painless and mild, causing no symptoms. Large goiters can cause compression symptoms such as difficulty swallowing, breathing, or voice changes. It may be associated with normal, overactive, or underactive thyroid function. Cancer is rare.

Gonorrhea is a common bacterial sexually transmitted infection caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae. It causes urethritis with discharge and painful urination, cervicitis, and rectal infections. It is usually mild and curable with antibiotics, although resistance is increasing. Untreated, it can cause pelvic inflammatory disease, infertility, and disseminated infection that affects joints, skin, and heart, which is severe.

Gout is a form of arthritis caused by uric acid crystal deposits in joints. It causes sudden, severe pain, redness, and swelling, often in the big toe. Acute flares are mild to moderate, self-limited, and treatable with anti-inflammatory medications. Chronic tophaceous gout causes joint damage, deposits under the skin called tophi, and kidney stones.

Granuloma inguinale, also called donovanosis, is a rare, chronic bacterial sexually transmitted infection caused by Klebsiella granulomatis. It causes painless, progressive ulcers in the genital region that bleed easily. It is not life-threatening but can cause scarring, swelling, and genital disfigurement if untreated. It is curable with antibiotics.

Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder causing hyperthyroidism, which is an overactive thyroid. It leads to weight loss, rapid heartbeat, heat intolerance, anxiety, and goiter. It also causes Graves ophthalmopathy with bulging eyes and eye inflammation. Thyroid storm, which is a sudden worsening, is life-threatening. Most cases are moderate and controlled with antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

Guillain-Barré syndrome is an autoimmune disorder where the immune system attacks peripheral nerves. It causes rapidly progressive weakness, absent reflexes, and numbness. It often follows an infection such as Campylobacter or a virus. Severe cases cause respiratory failure requiring a breathing machine. Most recover with immunotherapy, but residual weakness is common. Severity ranges from mild with walking difficulty to life-threatening with breathing paralysis.

Gum disease includes gingivitis and periodontitis. It is inflammation and infection of the gums due to bacterial plaque. Gingivitis is mild and reversible, causing red, swollen, bleeding gums. Periodontitis is advanced and destroys bone and connective tissue, leading to tooth loss. Chronic periodontitis is also linked to cardiovascular disease and diabetes. Severity ranges from mild for gingivitis to moderate for tooth loss.

Gynecomastia is benign enlargement of male breast tissue due to hormonal imbalance where estrogen is higher than androgen. It is common in newborns, during puberty, and in older men. It is not dangerous or cancerous, though may cause psychological distress. Pseudogynecomastia from fat is also benign. Treatment is observation, medication, or surgery if persistent and bothersome.

Hammer toe is a deformity of the second, third, or fourth toe where the toe bends upward at the middle joint, resembling a hammer. It is often caused by ill-fitting shoes, muscle imbalance, or arthritis. Symptoms include pain, corns, calluses, and difficulty finding comfortable footwear. It is not life-threatening but can cause discomfort and mobility issues. Severity ranges from mild and flexible and correctable to moderate and rigid requiring surgery.

Hashimoto thyroiditis is an autoimmune disorder where the immune system attacks the thyroid gland. It leads to chronic inflammation and eventually hypothyroidism, which is an underactive thyroid. Symptoms include fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, and goiter. It is not life-threatening and is easily managed with thyroid hormone replacement. Severity is mild, though untreated hypothyroidism can become moderate, with a rare life-threatening condition called myxedema coma.

Head and neck cancers include malignancies of the mouth, throat, voice box, nose, sinuses, and salivary glands. They are often linked to tobacco, alcohol, and HPV infection. Symptoms include a lump, sore throat that does not heal, hoarseness, difficulty swallowing, and ear pain. Early-stage cancer is treatable with surgery or radiation and is moderate. Advanced or metastatic disease is life-threatening with poorer survival.

Head injury encompasses trauma to the scalp, skull, or brain. It ranges from minor bumps to severe traumatic brain injury. Mild head injury such as concussion causes temporary confusion or headache and resolves. Moderate to severe head injury causes prolonged loss of consciousness, bleeding inside the skull, brain swelling, seizures, and permanent neurological deficits. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening depending on the type and extent of bleeding.

Post-dural puncture headache is a complication of lumbar puncture, also called a spinal tap. It occurs when cerebrospinal fluid leaks through the puncture site, causing a headache that worsens when upright and improves when lying down. It is usually mild to moderate, self-limiting within days to weeks. Severe cases may require an epidural blood patch. It is not life-threatening.

A heart attack, also called myocardial infarction, occurs when blood flow to a part of the heart is blocked. It is usually by a blood clot in a coronary artery. It causes chest pain, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, and arm or jaw pain. It is a medical emergency. Immediate treatment with aspirin, angioplasty, or clot-busting drugs saves heart muscle and life. Severity ranges from mild with a small area of damage and good recovery to life-threatening with a large area causing heart failure, arrhythmias, and death.

Heart block is a delay or complete block of electrical conduction from the upper to the lower chambers of the heart. It ranges from first-degree which is mild and without symptoms to third-degree which is complete heart block. Third-degree heart block causes dizziness, fainting, heart failure, and can lead to sudden cardiac death, requiring a pacemaker. Severity ranges from mild for first-degree to life-threatening for third-degree.

Heart contusion, also called myocardial contusion, is a bruise of the heart muscle caused by blunt chest trauma such as a car accident or fall. It can cause chest pain, arrhythmias, low blood pressure, and heart failure. Most are mild and heal with monitoring, but severe contusions can cause cardiogenic shock, heart rupture, or death.

Heart failure is a chronic condition where the heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body needs. It causes shortness of breath, fatigue, swelling called edema, and reduced exercise capacity. It is progressive and usually incurable. Severity ranges from mild with no symptoms but activity restriction to severe with symptoms at rest, frequent hospitalizations, and life-threatening. Advanced heart failure has high death rate and may require transplant or a ventricular assist device.

Heat exhaustion is a heat-related illness caused by excessive sweating and dehydration in hot environments. It leads to heavy sweating, rapid pulse, dizziness, nausea, headache, and weakness. It is moderate and reversible with cooling and rehydration. If untreated, it can progress to heat stroke, which is life-threatening.

Heat stroke is the most severe heat-related illness. The body temperature rises above one hundred four degrees Fahrenheit with central nervous system dysfunction such as confusion, seizures, or coma. It causes multi-organ failure, muscle breakdown, kidney injury, and death if not treated immediately with rapid cooling and intravenous fluids. This is a life-threatening medical emergency.

A hemangioma is a benign vascular tumor made of blood vessels that appears as a bright red birthmark. It is common in infants and is called strawberry hemangioma. Most grow for a few months then shrink without treatment. They are painless and not dangerous unless they interfere with vision, breathing, or break down into an ulcer. Severe cases may require medication or laser.

Hemarthrosis is bleeding into a joint space. It most commonly affects the knee, ankle, or elbow. Causes include trauma, bleeding disorders such as hemophilia, or blood thinner use. Symptoms include joint pain, swelling, warmth, and decreased range of motion. Repeated hemarthrosis can cause chronic arthritis and joint damage.

A hematoma is a collection of blood outside blood vessels, usually from trauma. It forms a swollen, bruised area. Most are superficial and mild, resolving spontaneously. Subdural or epidural hematomas inside the skull are life-threatening emergencies. Muscle hematomas can cause compartment syndrome if large. Severity ranges from mild for a bruise to life-threatening for a brain hematoma.

Hemiplegia is paralysis of one side of the body. It is usually caused by stroke, traumatic brain injury, or cerebral palsy. It results in loss of movement, muscle weakness, spasticity, and difficulty with daily activities. It is not life-threatening directly but leads to disability and complications such as pressure sores, contractures, and pneumonia. Severity ranges from mild weakness called hemiparesis to complete paralysis.

Hemochromatosis is a genetic disorder causing excessive iron absorption and accumulation in organs such as the liver, heart, pancreas, and joints. Without treatment, iron overload causes cirrhosis, diabetes, heart failure, arthritis, and bronze discoloration of the skin. It is manageable with regular blood removal. Severity ranges from without symptoms to severe organ damage if untreated. Early diagnosis and treatment normalize life expectancy.

Hemolytic anemia is the premature destruction of red blood cells. It leads to fatigue, jaundice, dark urine, and an enlarged spleen. Causes include autoimmune conditions, inherited disorders such as sickle cell, infections, or drug reactions. Mild cases are manageable. Severe acute hemolysis from transfusion reaction or autoimmune crisis causes rapid anemia, kidney failure, shock, and death. Severity ranges from mild and chronic compensated to life-threatening and acute severe hemolysis.

Hemophilia is an inherited bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of clotting factor eight for hemophilia A or factor nine for hemophilia B. Severity depends on factor levels. Mild hemophilia causes bleeding only after major injury or surgery. Moderate causes bleeding after minor injury. Severe causes spontaneous bleeding into joints and muscles. Life-threatening complications include bleeding inside the skull and major trauma. With factor replacement therapy, life expectancy is near normal.

Hemorrhoids are swollen veins in the rectum or anus. They cause itching, pain, and bleeding, with bright red blood on toilet paper. Internal hemorrhoids are usually painless. External hemorrhoids can be painful. They are very common, not dangerous, and often resolve with dietary fiber, fluids, and topical treatments. Severe hemorrhoids may require rubber band ligation or surgery.

Hepatic encephalopathy is brain dysfunction caused by the liver inability to remove toxins, such as ammonia, from the blood. It is usually due to cirrhosis or acute liver failure. Symptoms range from mild confusion, sleep reversal, and a flapping tremor of the hands to stupor, coma, and death. It is reversible with lactulose, antibiotics, and treating triggers such as bleeding or infection. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening coma.

Toxin-induced hepatitis is liver inflammation caused by drugs such as acetaminophen overdose, antibiotics, or NSAIDs, industrial chemicals, or natural toxins like mushrooms. Acetaminophen overdose is the most common cause of acute liver failure in the United States, which is life-threatening. Severity ranges from mild with elevated liver enzymes to life-threatening with sudden severe liver failure and death. Treatment includes N-acetylcysteine for acetaminophen and supportive care.

A herniated, or slipped, disk occurs when the soft inner gel of a spinal disk leaks through a tear in the outer layer. It presses on nearby spinal nerves. It causes back or neck pain, radiating pain such as sciatica for lumbar herniation, numbness, and weakness. Most improve with conservative treatment such as rest, physical therapy, and pain medication within weeks. Severe cases with cauda equina syndrome, which causes loss of bowel or bladder control, require emergency surgery.

Herpangina is a viral illness, usually caused by Coxsackievirus, in children. It causes sudden fever, sore throat, and painful blisters or ulcers on the tonsils and soft palate. It is self-limiting, lasting three to seven days, and mild. Treatment is supportive with hydration and pain relief. Complications are rare.

A hiatal hernia occurs when part of the stomach bulges up through the diaphragm into the chest cavity. Most are small and without symptoms. Larger hernias cause gastroesophageal reflux with heartburn, regurgitation, and sometimes difficulty swallowing. Rarely, a strangulated hiatal hernia where the stomach twists causes severe chest pain and vomiting, and is a surgical emergency that is life-threatening. Severity is mild for most.

Hidradenitis suppurativa is a chronic inflammatory skin condition. It causes painful, recurrent boils, abscesses, and tunnels called sinus tracts in areas with sweat glands such as the armpits, groin, and buttocks. It is not infectious or life-threatening but significantly impairs quality of life, causes scarring, and can lead to restricted movement. Severity ranges from mild with occasional nodules to severe with widespread, draining tunnels requiring surgery.

High blood pressure, also called hypertension, is persistently elevated blood pressure. It is often without symptoms and is called the silent killer. Untreated, it leads to heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, and death. Severity ranges from elevated, to stage one, to stage two, to hypertensive crisis. Mild to moderate hypertension is manageable with lifestyle and medication. Severe hypertension is dangerous.

Hirschsprung disease is a congenital condition where nerve cells are missing from part of the colon. This causes severe constipation, abdominal distension, and failure to pass meconium in newborns. It can lead to enterocolitis, which is inflammation and infection of the bowel, which is life-threatening. Surgical removal of the affected bowel segment is curative. Severity ranges from moderate with surgery to life-threatening with enterocolitis.

Hirsutism is excessive male-pattern hair growth in women on the face, chest, and back. It is often due to polycystic ovary syndrome, hormonal imbalance, or genetics. It is not dangerous but can cause psychological distress. Severity is mild from a medical standpoint, though cosmetic concerns may be significant. Treatment includes hair removal methods and hormonal therapy.

Histoplasmosis is a fungal infection caused by inhaling Histoplasma capsulatum spores found in bat or bird droppings. Most cases are mild or without symptoms. Acute pulmonary histoplasmosis causes flu-like symptoms. Disseminated histoplasmosis spreads to bone marrow, liver, and spleen and occurs in immunocompromised people such as those with HIV or transplant recipients. It is life-threatening if untreated. Severity ranges from mild and self-limited to life-threatening disseminated form.

Hormone disorders encompass imbalances in endocrine glands such as the thyroid, adrenal, pituitary, pancreas, and reproductive glands. Examples include hypothyroidism which is mild, hyperthyroidism which is moderate, Cushing syndrome which is moderate, and adrenal insufficiency which can be moderate to life-threatening in crisis. Severity varies widely from mild manageable conditions to life-threatening emergencies such as thyroid storm, adrenal crisis, and diabetic ketoacidosis.

Human papillomavirus is the most common sexually transmitted infection. Most strains are harmless and clear spontaneously. Low-risk strains cause genital warts, which are mild. High-risk strains such as types 16 and 18 cause cervical, anal, and oropharyngeal cancers, which are severe long-term risks. HPV itself is mild, but its cancer-causing potential makes screening with Pap smear and vaccination important.

HIV is a retrovirus that attacks CD4 immune cells, leading to immunosuppression. Untreated, it progresses to AIDS, which is acquired immunodeficiency syndrome, causing opportunistic infections such as PCP, toxoplasmosis, and tuberculosis, and cancers such as Kaposi sarcoma and lymphoma, which are life-threatening. With antiretroviral therapy, HIV is a chronic, manageable condition with near-normal life expectancy.

Huntington disease is an inherited, progressive neurodegenerative disorder. It causes chorea which are involuntary movements, cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms such as depression and psychosis, and eventually death. Onset is typically thirty to fifty years, with survival ten to twenty years after diagnosis. It is incurable and uniformly fatal.

Hydatidiform mole, also called molar pregnancy, is an abnormal pregnancy with overgrowth of placental tissue and no viable fetus. It causes vaginal bleeding, excessively high hCG hormone levels, and grape-like vesicles on ultrasound. Most are benign. However, it can progress to invasive mole or choriocarcinoma, which is malignant and life-threatening but highly curable with chemotherapy.

A hydrocele is a fluid-filled sac around the testicle, causing painless scrotal swelling. It is common in newborns, where it resolves spontaneously, and in older men. It is benign and not dangerous. Large hydroceles may cause discomfort or cosmetic concern. Surgery called hydrocelectomy is optional.

Hydrocephalus is excessive accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid in the brain ventricles, causing increased pressure inside the skull. It causes headache, nausea, vomiting, vision problems, and in infants, an enlarged head. Without treatment such as a shunt or surgical opening, it leads to brain damage, seizures, and death.

Hydronephrosis is swelling of the kidney due to urine backup from an obstruction such as a kidney stone, tumor, enlarged prostate, or a congenital condition. It can be on one side or both sides. Mild hydronephrosis is without symptoms and reversible. Severe, prolonged obstruction causes permanent kidney damage and kidney failure.

Hypercalcemia is excess calcium in the blood. It is often due to overactive parathyroid glands or cancer. Mild hypercalcemia has no symptoms. Moderate hypercalcemia causes fatigue, kidney stones, and constipation. Severe hypercalcemia, typically above fourteen milligrams per deciliter, causes confusion, coma, cardiac arrhythmias, and death. Severity ranges from mild and chronic to life-threatening crisis.

Hypercholesterolemia, or high cholesterol, is elevated LDL cholesterol. It is a major risk factor for atherosclerosis, heart disease, and stroke. It is often without symptoms. Mild to moderate elevation is common and manageable with statins, diet, and exercise. Severe familial hypercholesterolemia can cause early heart attacks and is serious.

Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe, persistent nausea and vomiting during pregnancy. It leads to weight loss, dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, and ketosis. It is more severe than morning sickness and can require hospitalization for intravenous fluids and anti-nausea medication. While not usually life-threatening for the mother, severe cases can cause brain damage from thiamine deficiency or acute kidney injury.

Hypergammaglobulinemia is an excess of gamma globulins, which are antibodies, in the blood. It is often reactive to chronic infections, autoimmune diseases, or liver disease. It is usually without symptoms and just a laboratory finding. Rarely, a condition called monoclonal gammopathy of unknown significance can progress to multiple myeloma, which is severe.

Hyperhidrosis is excessive sweating beyond what is needed to regulate body temperature. It often affects the palms, soles, underarms, or face. It is not dangerous but can cause social embarrassment, skin breakdown, and infections. Severity ranges from mild and manageable with antiperspirants to moderate and interfering with daily life. Treatment includes topical agents, Botox, iontophoresis, or surgery.

Hyperkalemia is elevated potassium in the blood. It is often due to kidney failure, medications such as ACE inhibitors or potassium-sparing diuretics, or tissue breakdown. It causes muscle weakness, fatigue, and characteristic changes on an ECG. Severe hyperkalemia above six point five milliequivalents per liter causes cardiac arrhythmias and sudden death.

Hyperlipidemia is elevated lipids, which are cholesterol and triglycerides, in the blood. It is a major risk factor for cardiovascular disease. It is usually without symptoms. Very high triglycerides above one thousand milligrams per deciliter can cause pancreatitis, which is severe. Severity ranges from mild moderate elevation to moderate with very high triglycerides risking pancreatitis.

Hypernatremia is elevated sodium in the blood, typically above one hundred forty-five milliequivalents per liter. It is usually due to dehydration, diabetes insipidus, or excessive salt intake. Symptoms include thirst, dry mucous membranes, confusion, and seizures. Severe hypernatremia above one hundred sixty causes brain shrinkage, bleeding inside the skull, coma, and death, especially in infants and the elderly.

Hyperopia, also called farsightedness, is a refractive error where distant objects are clear but near objects are blurry. It is due to the eyeball being too short. Most cases are mild and correctable with glasses, contact lenses, or LASIK. Severe hyperopia can cause eye strain, headaches, and lazy eye in children. It is not dangerous.

Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state is a life-threatening complication of type two diabetes. It is marked by extreme hyperglycemia above six hundred milligrams per deciliter, severe dehydration, and high blood osmolality without significant ketosis, unlike diabetic ketoacidosis. Symptoms include altered mental status, weakness, and seizures. It requires intravenous fluids, insulin, and electrolyte replacement. Death rate is high.

Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy include gestational hypertension which is mild, preeclampsia which is moderate to severe, eclampsia which causes seizures and is life-threatening, and HELLP syndrome which involves hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets. Preeclampsia causes high blood pressure, protein in the urine, and organ dysfunction, risking both mother and baby. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening.

Hypertensive heart disease is heart damage caused by chronic high blood pressure. It leads to thickening of the left ventricle, diastolic dysfunction, and eventually heart failure. It also speeds up coronary artery disease, causing heart attacks, and arrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation. It is progressive but manageable with blood pressure control. Severity ranges from without symptoms to heart failure and death.

Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy is a genetic heart disease where the heart muscle becomes abnormally thick. This often obstructs blood flow from the left ventricle. It causes shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, and fainting. It is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes. Severity ranges from without symptoms to life-threatening arrhythmias and sudden death.

Hypocalcemia is low calcium in the blood. It is often due to underactive parathyroid glands, vitamin D deficiency, or kidney disease. Symptoms include muscle cramps, tingling around the mouth and fingertips, and signs of nerve irritability. Severe hypocalcemia causes tetany, seizures, spasm of the voice box, and cardiac arrhythmias, which are life-threatening.

Hypoglycemia is low blood glucose, typically below seventy milligrams per deciliter. It is common in diabetic patients on insulin or sulfonylureas. Symptoms include sweating, tremor, palpitations, confusion, and weakness. Severe hypoglycemia below fifty milligrams per deciliter causes seizures, coma, and death. It is an emergency requiring glucose by mouth or intravenously.

Hypokalemia is low potassium in the blood, typically below three point five milliequivalents per liter. It is due to diuretics, vomiting, diarrhea, or poor intake. Symptoms include weakness, fatigue, muscle cramps, and changes on an ECG. Severe hypokalemia below two point five causes ascending paralysis, muscle breakdown, and cardiac arrhythmias, which can be fatal.

Hyponatremia is low sodium in the blood, typically below one hundred thirty-five milliequivalents per liter. It is often due to syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone, diuretics, heart failure, or excessive water intake. Symptoms range from mild nausea and headache to moderate confusion and seizures to severe coma and brain herniation, which is life-threatening. Rapid correction can cause brain damage.

Hypothermia is dangerously low body temperature below ninety-five degrees Fahrenheit or thirty-five degrees Celsius. It is due to cold exposure. Mild hypothermia causes shivering and confusion. Moderate hypothermia causes slow heart rate, dilated pupils, and loss of shivering. Severe hypothermia below eighty-two degrees Fahrenheit causes coma, cardiac arrhythmias, and death. It is a medical emergency requiring rewarming.

Hypothyroidism is an underactive thyroid gland. It causes fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, and slow heart rate. Most cases are mild and easily treated with levothyroxine. Severe, untreated hypothyroidism can lead to myxedema coma, which is rare but life-threatening. Severity ranges from mild and without symptoms to moderate and symptomatic to severe with coma.

Hypovolemia is decreased blood volume. It is usually from dehydration, bleeding, burns, or vomiting and diarrhea. Mild hypovolemia causes thirst and dry mouth. Moderate causes fast heart rate, dizziness, and low urine output. Severe hypovolemia, which is shock, causes low blood pressure, confusion, no urine output, and death. Severity ranges from mild and reversible with oral fluids to life-threatening shock requiring transfusion.

Idiopathic absence of menstruation, also called primary or secondary amenorrhea, is the lack of menstrual periods without an identifiable cause after ruling out pregnancy, hormonal, structural, or genetic disorders. It is not life-threatening but may indicate underlying issues or affect fertility. Evaluation by a gynecologist is recommended.

Idiopathic excessive menstruation, also called menorrhagia, is abnormally heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding without an identifiable cause after ruling out structural, hormonal, or bleeding disorders. It can cause iron deficiency anemia and reduced quality of life but is not life-threatening. Treatment includes hormonal therapy or tranexamic acid.

Idiopathic infrequent menstruation, also called oligomenorrhea, is menstrual cycles longer than thirty-five days or fewer than nine periods per year without an identifiable cause. It is mild if not associated with infertility or other symptoms. It may resolve spontaneously or require ovulation induction for fertility.

Idiopathic irregular menstrual cycles refer to unpredictable cycle lengths without an identifiable cause after evaluation. It is common in adolescents and perimenopause and not dangerous. It may affect fertility.

Idiopathic nonmenstrual bleeding, also called intermenstrual bleeding, is bleeding between periods without an identifiable cause. It is often benign such as mid-cycle spotting but requires evaluation to rule out polyps, fibroids, infection, or malignancy. Severity is mild if benign, moderate if from an underlying problem.

Idiopathic painful menstruation, also called primary dysmenorrhea, is pelvic pain during menstruation without an identifiable pelvic problem such as endometriosis or fibroids. It is caused by prostaglandin-induced uterine contractions. It is not dangerous and responds to NSAIDs or oral contraceptives. Severity ranges from mild and manageable to moderate and activity-limiting.

Ileus is a temporary, non-mechanical obstruction of the bowel where the normal muscle contractions stop. It often occurs after abdominal surgery, infection, or medications such as opioids. Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension, and no bowel movements. It is usually self-limiting and resolves in days without surgery. Untreated, it can lead to bowel perforation and sepsis, which is severe.

Impetigo is a highly contagious bacterial skin infection caused by Staphylococcus or Streptococcus. It is common in children and causes honey-colored crusted sores or blisters, usually on the face and hands. It is mild and responds to topical or oral antibiotics. Complications are rare, with a possible risk of kidney inflammation.

Impulse control disorders include intermittent explosive disorder, kleptomania, pyromania, and pathological gambling. They involve failure to resist impulses that harm oneself or others. They are not life-threatening but can cause legal, financial, and relationship problems. Severity ranges from mild and occasional to moderate and impairing function. Treatment includes psychotherapy and sometimes medication.

Indigestion, also called dyspepsia, is upper abdominal discomfort, bloating, nausea, and feeling full quickly after eating. It is common, usually mild, and often related to diet, stress, or GERD. It is not dangerous but can mimic heart attack if there is chest pain. Most cases resolve with antacids or lifestyle changes.

Induced abortion is the intentional termination of pregnancy. When performed legally and safely with medication or surgery, complications are rare and severity is mild. Unsafe abortions can cause hemorrhage, infection, uterine perforation, sepsis, and death, which is severe to life-threatening. Severity depends on the method and setting.

Wound infection occurs when bacteria, often Staphylococcus or Streptococcus, colonize a break in the skin. It causes redness, warmth, pain, swelling, and pus. Most are mild to moderate, treated with antibiotics and wound care. Severe infections spread to deeper tissues, causing cellulitis or abscess, or cause sepsis, which is life-threatening.

Infectious gastroenteritis, also called stomach flu, is inflammation of the stomach and intestines caused by viruses such as norovirus or rotavirus, bacteria such as Salmonella or E. coli, or parasites such as Giardia. Symptoms include diarrhea, vomiting, nausea, abdominal cramps, and fever. Most cases are mild and self-limiting within twenty-four to forty-eight hours. Severe cases cause dehydration and require hospitalization. Severity is mild to moderate, rarely life-threatening in infants or elderly.

An ingrown toenail occurs when the nail edge grows into the surrounding skin, usually on the big toe. It causes pain, redness, swelling, and sometimes infection. It is mild and often resolves with proper trimming, soaks, and antibiotic ointment. Severe cases require partial nail removal. It is not dangerous.

An inguinal hernia is a bulging of tissue, often intestine, through a weak spot in the groin muscles. It causes a visible lump and discomfort with lifting or coughing. Most are reducible and mild. Strangulated hernia, where the blood supply is cut off, causes severe pain, nausea, vomiting, and is a surgical emergency that is life-threatening. Severity ranges from mild and without symptoms to life-threatening with strangulation.

Ankle injuries include sprains, which are ligament stretch or tear, strains, and fractures. Most ankle sprains are mild, causing pain and swelling but heal with rest, ice, compression, and elevation. Severe sprains or fractures are moderate to severe. Severity is mild for minor sprains, moderate for fractures or complete ligament tears.

Internal organ injuries affect solid organs such as the liver, spleen, and kidneys, or hollow organs such as the bowel and bladder. They result from blunt or penetrating trauma such as car accidents, falls, or stabbings. They cause internal bleeding, abdominal infection, shock, and can be fatal. Severity ranges from mild for a small bruise that is self-limited to life-threatening for laceration of the liver or spleen or perforation of the bowel with sepsis.

Abdominal injuries range from superficial bruises which are mild to blunt or penetrating trauma that causes internal organ damage, hemorrhage, and abdominal infection, which is life-threatening. Severity depends on the mechanism and organs involved. A simple abdominal wall bruise is mild. A liver laceration or bowel perforation is life-threatening.

Arm injuries include bruises, lacerations, fractures, dislocations, and nerve injuries. Most are mild such as contusions and small cuts. Fractures and dislocations are moderate to severe but rarely life-threatening unless associated with blood vessel injury or compartment syndrome.

Facial injuries include abrasions, lacerations, fractures of the nasal bones, upper jaw, lower jaw, and eye sockets. Most are mild such as scrapes and bruises. Fractures are moderate. Severe facial trauma with airway blockage, massive bleeding, or associated traumatic brain injury is life-threatening. Severity varies widely.

Finger injuries include cuts, crush injuries, fractures, dislocations, and tendon injuries such as mallet finger. Most are mild and heal with splinting or simple wound care. Severe injuries with tendon or nerve damage or amputation are moderate. Severity is mild for most.

Hand injuries range from minor cuts and bruises which are mild to fractures, tendon lacerations, nerve injuries, and crush injuries which are moderate. Most are not life-threatening but can cause permanent disability if not treated properly.

Hip injuries include contusions and strains which are mild, hip fractures especially in the elderly which are moderate to severe, and dislocations which are severe and often from car accidents. Hip fractures often require surgery and are associated with high death rate in the elderly due to complications. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening.

Knee injuries include sprains, ligament tears such as ACL and MCL, meniscus tears, patellar dislocations, and fractures. Minor sprains are mild. ACL tears and meniscus tears are moderate and may require surgery. Severe fractures or nerve and blood vessel injuries are severe. Severity ranges from mild to moderate.

Leg injuries include contusions, lacerations, fractures of the tibia and fibula, and compartment syndrome which is an emergency. Most soft tissue injuries are mild. Fractures are moderate. Compartment syndrome, where swelling causes nerve and muscle death, is severe and limb-threatening.

Shoulder injuries include rotator cuff tears, dislocations, fractures of the clavicle or humerus, and AC joint separations. Most are mild to moderate, recovering with rest and physical therapy. Severe fractures or complex dislocations may require surgery.

Spinal cord injury is damage to the spinal cord from trauma such as car accidents, falls, or violence. It causes loss of motor, sensory, and automatic function below the level of injury. Neck spinal cord injury causes paralysis of all four limbs and respiratory failure, which is life-threatening. Back spinal cord injury causes paralysis of the legs. Spinal cord injury is not curable. Severity ranges from incomplete with some function preserved to complete with paralysis and loss of bowel and bladder control. It is life-altering and can be life-threatening in the acute phase.

Trunk injuries affect the chest, abdomen, pelvis, or back. Bruises and small lacerations are mild. Rib fractures with lung collapse or organ injury to the liver or spleen are moderate to severe. Major blunt trauma to the trunk can cause life-threatening internal bleeding, injury to the main artery, or tear of the diaphragm.

Insect bites from mosquitoes, fleas, ants, bees, and wasps usually cause localized redness, itching, and swelling which is mild. Bee and wasp stings can cause anaphylaxis, which is life-threatening, in allergic individuals. Spider bites from black widow or brown recluse can cause systemic symptoms or skin death, which is moderate. Mosquitoes transmit diseases such as malaria and dengue, which can be severe.

Insulin overdose from exogenous insulin causes severe hypoglycemia, which is low blood sugar. This can lead to confusion, seizures, coma, and death. It is a medical emergency requiring intravenous glucose or glucagon.

Interstitial lung disease is a group of lung disorders causing progressive scarring of lung tissue, making it hard to breathe. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis has a median survival of three to five years. Other types such as sarcoidosis or hypersensitivity pneumonitis are treatable. Severity ranges from mild with slow progression to life-threatening with end-stage fibrosis and respiratory failure.

Intertrigo is a red, irritated rash in skin folds such as the armpits, groin, under the breasts, and between the toes. It is caused by moisture, friction, and heat. It is mild and often resolves with keeping the area dry and using barrier creams. It can become secondarily infected with yeast or bacteria, which is moderate.

Intestinal cancer, also called small bowel cancer, is rare. Types include adenocarcinoma, neuroendocrine tumors, lymphomas, and sarcomas. Symptoms include abdominal pain, weight loss, bleeding, and obstruction. Prognosis depends on stage. Early-stage is curable and moderate. Advanced or metastatic is life-threatening.

Intestinal disease includes inflammatory bowel disease such as Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis, infections, celiac disease, malabsorption, lack of blood flow to the bowel, and cancer. Severity varies widely. Irritable bowel syndrome is mild. Celiac disease is mild to moderate with a gluten-free diet. Crohn disease is moderate. Lack of blood flow to the bowel is life-threatening.

Malabsorption is the inability to absorb nutrients from the intestine. It is due to celiac disease, pancreatic insufficiency, Crohn disease, or short bowel syndrome. It causes diarrhea, weight loss, anemia, vitamin deficiencies, and osteoporosis. It is not life-threatening but can cause severe malnutrition if untreated.

Intestinal obstruction is a blockage of the bowel. It can be mechanical from a hernia, tumor, scar tissue, or volvulus, or functional from ileus. Symptoms include abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, and constipation. Untreated, it leads to bowel perforation, abdominal infection, sepsis, and death. It is a surgical emergency.

Intracerebral hemorrhage is bleeding within the brain tissue itself. It is usually from high blood pressure, trauma, or amyloid angiopathy. It causes sudden severe headache, weakness, speech problems, and decreased consciousness. It is a medical emergency. Death rate is high at forty to fifty percent within thirty days. Survivors often have significant disability.

Intracranial abscess, also called brain abscess, is a pus-filled pocket in the brain. It is due to spread from ear, sinus, or dental infection, or from trauma. It causes headache, fever, focal neurological deficits, seizures, and altered mental status. Without drainage and antibiotics, it causes brain herniation and death.

Intracranial hemorrhage is bleeding inside the skull. Types include epidural, subdural, subarachnoid, and intracerebral. Causes include trauma, aneurysm rupture, and high blood pressure. It causes sudden severe headache, vomiting, seizures, coma, and death. All types are medical emergencies with high death rates.

Intussusception is a condition where part of the intestine folds into the segment ahead of it, like a telescope. It causes obstruction, severe abdominal pain, currant jelly stools, vomiting, and lethargy. It is most common in infants and young children. Without treatment such as air enema or surgery, it leads to bowel death, perforation, abdominal infection, and death.

Iridocyclitis, also called anterior uveitis, is inflammation of the iris and ciliary body. It is often autoimmune such as in ankylosing spondylitis or juvenile arthritis, or infectious. Symptoms include eye pain, redness, sensitivity to light, blurred vision, and a small pupil. It can cause glaucoma, cataracts, and vision loss if untreated. Prompt treatment with corticosteroids is essential.

Iron deficiency anemia is the most common type of anemia. It is caused by blood loss from menstruation or gastrointestinal bleeding, poor diet, or malabsorption. Symptoms include fatigue, pale skin, brittle nails, and pica which is craving ice or dirt. It is mild to moderate and reversible with oral iron supplements and treating the underlying cause. Severe chronic deficiency can cause heart failure, which is rare.

Irritable bowel syndrome is a functional bowel disorder. It causes abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits such as diarrhea, constipation, or both, without visible damage. It is not dangerous or life-threatening but significantly affects quality of life. Severity ranges from mild and bothersome to moderate and disabling. Treatment includes diet, fiber, antispasmodics, and stress reduction.

Bowel ischemia, also called mesenteric ischemia, is reduced blood flow to the intestines. It can be acute from a blood clot or chronic from atherosclerosis. Acute mesenteric ischemia causes severe, out-of-proportion abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, and bloody stools. It progresses to bowel death, perforation, abdominal infection, septic shock, and death. It is a surgical emergency with high death rate of fifty to eighty percent.

Ischemic heart disease, also called coronary artery disease, is narrowing of the coronary arteries due to atherosclerosis. This reduces blood flow to the heart. It causes angina, heart attack, heart failure, arrhythmias, and sudden death. It is the leading cause of death worldwide. Severity ranges from stable angina which is moderate to acute coronary syndrome which is life-threatening.

Pruritus, or itching, without a primary rash is often due to dry skin, systemic disease such as liver, kidney, or thyroid disease, or lymphoma, or drugs. It is not dangerous but can be distressing. Severity is mild to moderate, treatable with moisturizers, antihistamines, or treating the underlying cause.

Jaw disorders include temporomandibular joint disorder, fractures, dislocations, and bone death. TMJ disorder causes jaw pain, clicking, locking, and headaches. It is mild to moderate and treated with conservative measures such as splints and physical therapy. Jaw fractures are moderate to severe.

Joint effusion, also called water on the knee, is excess fluid in a joint. It is usually from injury, arthritis, or infection. It causes swelling, stiffness, and reduced range of motion. Mild effusions resolve with rest and ice. Large effusions require drainage. Septic arthritis, which is an infected effusion, is an emergency and severe.

Juvenile idiopathic arthritis is chronic arthritis in children. It causes joint pain, swelling, stiffness, and potentially growth problems and eye inflammation called uveitis. It is not life-threatening but can be disabling if uncontrolled. Modern treatments such as biologics have greatly improved outcomes. Severity ranges from mild with few joints involved to severe with systemic symptoms such as fever and rash.

Kaposi sarcoma is a cancer of blood vessel walls caused by human herpesvirus eight. It is most common in HIV and organ transplant recipients. Skin lesions that are purple, brown, or red patches or nodules are moderate. Visceral kaposi sarcoma that affects the lungs or gastrointestinal tract causes breathing problems and bleeding and can be life-threatening. With antiretroviral therapy and chemotherapy, severity has decreased.

Kidney cancer, most commonly renal cell carcinoma, often presents with blood in the urine, flank pain, or a mass. Early-stage kidney cancer is moderate and curable with surgery. Advanced or metastatic kidney cancer is life-threatening, though targeted therapies and immunotherapy have improved survival.

disease_name: kidney disease due to longstanding hypertension
description: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis is chronic kidney damage caused by long-standing, uncontrolled high blood pressure. It leads to progressive decline in kidney function, eventually to end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplant. It is a leading cause of chronic kidney disease. Severity ranges from mild with small amounts of protein in the urine to severe and life-threatening with kidney failure.

Kidney failure, also called end-stage renal disease, is the final stage of chronic kidney disease where kidney function is below fifteen percent of normal. It causes buildup of waste products called uremia, fluid overload, electrolyte imbalances, anemia, and requires dialysis or kidney transplant for survival. Without treatment, it is fatal.
