Kidney stones, also called renal calculi, are hard mineral and salt deposits that form in the kidneys. They cause severe flank pain called renal colic, blood in the urine, nausea, and vomiting. Most stones pass spontaneously within days and are moderate. Large stones causing obstruction, infection, or intractable pain require procedures such as shockwave lithotripsy or ureteroscopy. Rarely, untreated obstruction leads to kidney damage or sepsis, which is severe.

Knee ligament tears such as ACL, MCL, PCL, and LCL, and meniscus tears are common sports injuries. They cause pain, swelling, instability, and locking. ACL tears often require surgical reconstruction. MCL tears often heal with bracing. Meniscus tears may require arthroscopic surgery. Severity is moderate. It is not life-threatening but can lead to long-term arthritis if untreated.

Labyrinthitis is inflammation of the inner ear labyrinth. It is usually viral and causes sudden vertigo, which is spinning, hearing loss, nausea, and involuntary eye movements. It is self-limiting but can last days to weeks. It is not dangerous but can cause falls. Treatment is symptomatic with antihistamines or steroids.

Lactose intolerance is the inability to digest lactose, which is milk sugar, due to lactase deficiency. It causes bloating, diarrhea, gas, and abdominal pain after dairy intake. It is not dangerous but causes discomfort. Severity is mild, managed by a lactose-free diet or lactase supplements.

Laryngitis is inflammation of the voice box, called the larynx. It is usually viral or from overuse. It causes hoarseness, voice loss, and sore throat. It is mild and self-limiting, resolving in days to weeks. Chronic laryngitis may indicate reflux, allergy, or smoking.

Lateral epicondylitis, also called tennis elbow, is tendinopathy of the common extensor tendon at the outer elbow. It causes pain with gripping or extending the wrist. It is not dangerous and often resolves with rest, bracing, physical therapy, and steroid injections.

Leukemia is cancer of blood-forming tissues such as the bone marrow. It leads to abnormal white blood cells. Acute types such as ALL and AML progress rapidly and are life-threatening without immediate treatment. Chronic types such as CLL and CML progress slowly. Overall, untreated leukemia is fatal. With modern chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and stem cell transplant, many achieve remission. Severity is life-threatening, though some chronic leukemias are now manageable long-term.

Lewy body dementia is a progressive neurodegenerative dementia. It has features of Parkinson disease such as rigidity, tremor, and shuffling gait, visual hallucinations, and fluctuating cognition. It is the second most common dementia after Alzheimer disease. It is incurable and eventually fatal, with survival five to eight years after diagnosis.

Lice infestation, called pediculosis, is caused by head, body, or pubic lice. It causes intense itching and small red bumps. It is not dangerous, except body lice may transmit typhus in rare cases. It is highly contagious but treatable with topical pediculicides such as permethrin or ivermectin.

Lichen planus is an inflammatory skin condition of unknown cause. It presents as itchy, flat-topped purple bumps on the wrists, ankles, or mucous membranes of the mouth or genitals. It is not contagious or dangerous but can be very itchy and sometimes painful in the oral form. Most cases resolve in one to two years.

Lichen simplex chronicus is a skin condition caused by chronic scratching of an itchy area, creating a vicious cycle. It leads to thickened, leathery, darkened plaques. It is not dangerous and resolves with stopping scratching, topical steroids, and anti-itch medications.

A lipoma is a benign, slow-growing fatty lump under the skin. It is soft, movable, and painless. It is not dangerous and rarely becomes cancerous. Treatment is only for cosmetic reasons or if it causes symptoms.

Liver cancer, most commonly hepatocellular carcinoma, often occurs in cirrhotic livers from hepatitis B or C or alcohol. Symptoms include abdominal pain, weight loss, yellowing of the skin, and fluid in the abdomen. Early-stage liver cancer is curable with resection or transplant, which is moderate. Most cases are diagnosed at an advanced stage, which is life-threatening with poor survival of months.

Liver disease includes fatty liver, also called NAFLD or NASH, hepatitis from viruses, alcohol, or autoimmunity, cirrhosis, and liver cancer. Fatty liver is mild. Hepatitis is moderate to severe. Cirrhosis and liver failure are life-threatening. Severity ranges from without symptoms and elevated liver enzymes to death from liver failure.

Lumbago, or low back pain, is a common condition. It is often from muscle strain, disc problems, or arthritis. Most cases are acute and mild to moderate, resolving with rest, pain relievers, and physical therapy. Chronic lumbago can be moderate and disabling. It is rarely dangerous except with cauda equina syndrome, which is an emergency.

Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death worldwide, strongly linked to smoking. Types include non-small cell and small cell. Early-stage lung cancer can be cured with surgery and is moderate. Most cases are diagnosed at an advanced stage, which is life-threatening with five-year survival of five to twenty percent depending on the subtype and treatment.

Lung contusion is bruising of lung tissue from blunt chest trauma such as a car accident or fall. It causes bleeding into the air sacs, leading to low oxygen and breathing distress. It is usually moderate, improving over days to weeks. Severe bilateral contusions can cause acute respiratory distress syndrome and death.

Lyme disease is a tick-borne infection caused by Borrelia burgdorferi. Early disease causes a bullseye rash, fever, fatigue, and joint pain. It is mild and curable with antibiotics. Late disseminated disease weeks to months later causes Lyme arthritis, nervous system problems such as meningitis and facial palsy, and heart block, which can be severe but rarely fatal.

Lymphadenitis is inflammation of lymph nodes, usually from infection in the drainage area. It causes tender, swollen nodes, such as neck nodes with strep throat. It is mild and resolves when the underlying infection is treated. Suppurative lymphadenitis that forms an abscess may require drainage and is moderate.

Lymphangitis is inflammation of lymphatic vessels, usually from bacterial infection spreading from a wound. It causes red streaks from the wound toward lymph nodes, with pain, fever, and chills. It can progress to sepsis, which is life-threatening, if untreated. Prompt antibiotics are essential.

Lymphedema is chronic swelling due to lymphatic system damage or malformation. It often occurs after cancer surgery such as breast cancer causing arm lymphedema. It is not life-threatening but can cause discomfort, heaviness, decreased function, and recurrent skin infections. Severity ranges from mild pitting swelling to severe firm, fibrotic swelling.

Lymphogranuloma venereum is a sexually transmitted infection caused by Chlamydia trachomatis. It causes genital ulcers followed by painful groin lymph node swelling. It is curable with antibiotics. If untreated, it causes chronic swelling and rectal narrowing, which is moderate.

Lymphoma is cancer of the lymphatic system. Hodgkin lymphoma is often curable even at advanced stages with chemotherapy and radiation. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma has many subtypes. Some are indolent and slow-growing, while others are aggressive and life-threatening without treatment. Severity ranges from moderate to life-threatening depending on the type and stage.

Macular degeneration is a progressive eye disease causing loss of central vision needed for reading and recognizing faces. It is due to damage to the macula, which is the central retina. Age-related macular degeneration is the leading cause of blindness in older adults. Dry AMD is mild to moderate and slow-progressing. Wet AMD can cause rapid vision loss but is treatable with injections.

Hypomagnesemia, or magnesium deficiency, causes muscle cramps, tremors, weakness, arrhythmias, and seizures. It is often mild and correctable with oral magnesium supplements. Severe deficiency below one milligram per deciliter can cause life-threatening arrhythmias or tetany.

Malaria is a parasitic infection transmitted by mosquitoes. It causes fever, chills, headache, and vomiting. Uncomplicated malaria is moderate and curable with antimalarials. Severe malaria caused by Plasmodium falciparum causes cerebral malaria with seizures and coma, respiratory distress, kidney failure, and death, especially in children and pregnant women.

Male genital infections include balanitis which is inflammation of the glans, epididymitis, orchitis which is testicular infection, and urethritis from sexually transmitted infections. Most are mild to moderate and treatable with antibiotics or antifungals. Untreated, they can cause infertility or spread systemically, which is severe.

Malignant hypertension, also called hypertensive emergency, is severely high blood pressure, often above one hundred eighty over one hundred twenty, with acute end-organ damage. It affects the brain causing encephalopathy or stroke, the heart causing heart attack or failure, the kidneys causing acute injury, and the eyes causing retinal hemorrhage. It is a medical emergency requiring immediate blood pressure reduction with intravenous drugs to prevent death.

Marijuana abuse is problematic use leading to impaired work or school, social problems, and health risks such as chronic bronchitis, cognitive impairment, and psychosis. It is not directly life-threatening but can cause accidents while driving and cannabis hyperemesis syndrome, which is severe vomiting. Withdrawal is mild with anxiety and irritability.

Mastectomy is surgical removal of one or both breasts, typically for breast cancer treatment or prevention. The procedure itself is not a disease. It causes pain, recovery, and psychological impact. Complications such as infection, bleeding, or lymphedema are mild to moderate. It is not life-threatening unless from surgical complications.

Mastoiditis is infection of the mastoid bone behind the ear. It is a complication of untreated middle ear infection. It causes ear pain, fever, redness, swelling behind the ear, and a protruding ear. It requires intravenous antibiotics and sometimes surgery to remove infected bone. If untreated, it can spread to the brain, causing meningitis or brain abscess, which is life-threatening.

Meckel diverticulum is a congenital outpouching of the small intestine. Most are without symptoms and mild. Complications occur in two to four percent and include painless rectal bleeding, intestinal obstruction from twisting or telescoping, and inflammation mimicking appendicitis. These complications are moderate to severe and require surgery.

Melanoma is the most dangerous form of skin cancer. It arises from melanocytes and can spread to lymph nodes, liver, lungs, and brain. Early-stage melanoma that is thin and localized is curable with surgery and is moderate. Advanced melanoma is life-threatening, though immunotherapy and targeted therapy have dramatically improved survival.

Meniere disease is an inner ear disorder causing episodes of vertigo which is spinning, fluctuating hearing loss, ringing in the ears, and a feeling of fullness in the ear. It is not life-threatening but can be disabling during attacks. Over years, hearing loss becomes permanent. Treatment includes a low-salt diet, diuretics, and vestibular rehabilitation.

Meningioma is a usually benign tumor arising from the meninges, which are the coverings of the brain. It grows slowly and is often without symptoms, which is mild. Symptomatic meningiomas cause headaches, seizures, or focal neurological deficits. Malignant meningiomas are rare and aggressive. Most are treated with surgery or radiation, and the prognosis is excellent.

Meningitis is inflammation of the meninges, the coverings of the brain and spinal cord. It is most often infectious. Bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency causing fever, stiff neck, severe headache, sensitivity to light, and altered mental status. It has high death rates of ten to twenty percent even with antibiotics. Survivors may have deafness or brain damage. Viral meningitis is usually milder.

Menopause is the natural cessation of menstruation, defined as twelve consecutive months without a period, typically between ages forty-five and fifty-five. Symptoms include hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal dryness, mood changes, and sleep disturbances. It is not a disease but a normal transition. Severity ranges from without symptoms to moderate with symptoms impairing quality of life.

Metabolic disorders include inherited conditions such as PKU, Gaucher disease, and Tay-Sachs disease, and acquired conditions such as diabetes and metabolic syndrome. Severity varies widely. PKU is mild to moderate with dietary restriction. Tay-Sachs is life-threatening in childhood. Metabolic syndrome with obesity, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol is mild to moderate but increases cardiovascular risk.

Metastatic cancer is cancer that has spread from its original site to distant organs such as the liver, lungs, bones, or brain. It is generally incurable, though some can be controlled for years with modern therapies. Life expectancy varies from months to years. It inevitably leads to death unless from other causes.

Migraine is a neurological disorder causing recurrent, severe headaches often with nausea, vomiting, sensitivity to light, and visual disturbances called aura. It is not life-threatening but can be disabling during attacks. Chronic migraine with more than fifteen days per month is moderate. Treatment includes acute medications and preventive medications.

Missed abortion, also called early pregnancy failure, is when the embryo or fetus has died but the body has not expelled it. It causes no symptoms or loss of pregnancy symptoms and is detected on ultrasound. It is not life-threatening unless retained for weeks causing infection, which is severe. Treatment is either expectant management, medication, or surgical removal.

Mitral valve disease includes mitral stenosis, which is narrowing, and mitral regurgitation, which is leaking. It is often due to rheumatic fever or degenerative changes. Mild disease is without symptoms and moderate. Severe disease causes heart failure, shortness of breath, fatigue, and atrial fibrillation, which increases stroke risk. It may require valve repair or replacement.
Mittelschmerz is mid-cycle lower abdominal pain occurring when the egg is released from the ovary. It is benign, lasts hours to a day, and is mild. No treatment is needed.

Molluscum contagiosum is a viral skin infection causing small, painless, dome-shaped, pearly bumps with central dimples. It is common in children and sexually active adults. It is benign and self-limiting, resolving in six to eighteen months, but it is contagious. Treatment is for cosmetic reasons or to prevent spread.

Mononeuritis, or mononeuropathy, is damage to a single peripheral nerve. Examples include carpal tunnel syndrome, ulnar neuropathy, and peroneal palsy. Causes include trauma, compression, diabetes, and inflammation. It causes pain, numbness, and weakness in the distribution of that nerve. Most are mild and improve with treating the cause, splinting, or surgery.

Infectious mononucleosis, or mono, is caused by Epstein-Barr virus. It causes fever, sore throat, swollen lymph nodes, and fatigue that can sometimes last months. It is usually mild to moderate in adolescents and young adults. Complications include splenic rupture which is rare but life-threatening, hepatitis, and airway obstruction.

Moyamoya disease is a progressive cerebrovascular disorder where the internal carotid arteries become narrowed. This leads to the development of small, fragile collateral vessels that look like a puff of smoke. It causes recurrent strokes and transient ischemic attacks, especially in children and young adults. Without revascularization surgery, recurrent strokes cause significant disability and death.

Mucositis is painful inflammation and ulceration of the mucous membranes of the mouth, throat, and digestive tract. It is usually a side effect of chemotherapy or radiation therapy. It is not dangerous but can cause severe pain, difficulty eating, and risk of infection in immunocompromised people. Severity ranges from mild discomfort to severe requiring opioids or a feeding tube.

Multiple myeloma is a cancer of plasma cells in the bone marrow. It causes anemia, bone pain with lytic lesions, kidney failure, high calcium, and recurrent infections. It is incurable but treatable. Median survival has improved with novel agents to five to ten plus years, but it remains life-threatening with eventual relapse.

Multiple sclerosis is a chronic autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the myelin sheath of the central nervous system. It causes relapsing-remitting or progressive neurological symptoms such as vision loss, weakness, numbness, and balance issues. It is not directly fatal, but severe progressive disease leads to disability, infections, and reduced life expectancy by five to ten years.

Mumps is a viral infection causing painful swelling of the parotid salivary glands in the cheeks, fever, and headache. It is usually mild in children. Complications include testicular inflammation which rarely causes sterility, ovarian inflammation, meningitis, pancreatitis, and deafness. These are mild to moderate.

Muscle spasm, also called a cramp, is a sudden, involuntary contraction of a muscle, causing pain and tightness. Common causes include dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, overuse, and neurological conditions. Most are mild and self-limiting, relieved by stretching, hydration, and electrolytes. Severe or recurrent spasms may indicate an underlying disease.

Muscular dystrophy is a group of genetic disorders causing progressive muscle weakness and wasting. Duchenne muscular dystrophy is the most common and severe, affecting boys. It leads to wheelchair dependence by early teens, respiratory failure, and death by the twenties or thirties. Becker muscular dystrophy is milder. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening.

Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder causing fluctuating muscle weakness, especially of the eye muscles, face, throat, and limbs. It is not curable but treatable. Myasthenic crisis, which is severe weakness of the respiratory muscles, causes respiratory failure and is life-threatening, requiring mechanical ventilation. Severity ranges from mild ocular form to severe generalized form.

Myelodysplastic syndromes are a group of blood cancers where the bone marrow fails to produce healthy blood cells. This leads to anemia, bleeding, and infections. They can progress to acute myeloid leukemia. Low-risk disease is moderate and manageable with supportive care. High-risk disease is life-threatening with poor survival without stem cell transplant.

Myocarditis is inflammation of the heart muscle. It is often due to viral infection such as Coxsackie or COVID-19. It causes chest pain, arrhythmias, heart failure, and sudden death. Mild cases recover spontaneously and are moderate. Severe myocarditis causes fulminant heart failure, cardiogenic shock, and death. It may require mechanical circulatory support.

Myoclonus is sudden, involuntary jerking of a muscle or group of muscles. Benign forms such as hiccups and sleep starts are mild. Pathologic myoclonus from post-hypoxic states, epilepsy, or neurodegenerative diseases can be moderate to severe. Severe myoclonus impairs movement and daily function but is rarely life-threatening.

Myopia, also called nearsightedness, is a refractive error where distant objects appear blurry while near objects are clear. It is due to the eyeball being too long. It is mild and easily corrected with glasses, contacts, or LASIK. High myopia above negative six diopters increases the risk of retinal detachment, glaucoma, and myopic maculopathy, which are moderate to severe.

Myositis is muscle inflammation, often autoimmune such as polymyositis or dermatomyositis, or due to infection. It causes muscle weakness, pain, and swelling. It is not usually life-threatening but can cause significant disability. Severe myositis can involve the heart causing myocarditis or the lungs causing interstitial lung disease, which are life-threatening.

Narcolepsy is a chronic sleep disorder causing excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy which is sudden muscle weakness triggered by emotion, sleep paralysis, and hallucinations. It is not life-threatening but can cause accidents such as while driving. Treatment includes stimulants, sodium oxybate, and lifestyle modifications.

Nasal polyps are benign, teardrop-shaped growths in the nasal passages or sinuses. They are often associated with chronic sinusitis, allergies, or asthma. They cause nasal congestion, runny nose, facial pain, and loss of smell. They are not dangerous but can be bothersome. Treatment includes nasal steroids, oral steroids, or surgery.

Necrotizing fasciitis is a rare but life-threatening bacterial infection also called flesh-eating disease. It destroys skin, subcutaneous tissue, and fascia. It spreads rapidly, causing severe pain, swelling, redness, blisters, and systemic toxicity. Without emergency surgical removal of dead tissue and broad-spectrum antibiotics, death rate approaches one hundred percent, but overall is twenty to thirty percent even with treatment.

Neonatal jaundice is yellowing of the skin and eyes in newborns due to high bilirubin. Physiological jaundice on days two to five is mild and self-resolving. Pathologic jaundice that is excessive or prolonged can cause kernicterus, which is bilirubin brain toxicity, leading to cerebral palsy, hearing loss, and death. Most cases are mild and treated with phototherapy.

Nerve impingement near the shoulder includes brachial plexopathy, thoracic outlet syndrome, or suprascapular nerve entrapment. It causes pain, numbness, and weakness in the arm or hand. Most are mild to moderate, treated with physical therapy, ergonomic changes, or surgery. Severe cases cause muscle wasting.

Neuralgia is severe, sharp, burning pain along a nerve distribution. It is often due to nerve irritation or damage. Examples include trigeminal neuralgia of the face and postherpetic neuralgia after shingles. It is not life-threatening but can be very painful and disabling. Treatment includes anticonvulsants, antidepressants, or nerve blocks.

Neurofibromatosis is a genetic disorder causing benign tumors on nerves, skin changes called cafe-au-lait spots, and sometimes skeletal abnormalities. Type one is more common and mild to moderate. Type two causes tumors on the hearing nerves, leading to hearing loss and brain tumors. Tumors can compress the spinal cord or become malignant in rare cases. Severity ranges from mild with only skin lesions to severe with spinal cord compression or blindness.

Drug-induced peripheral neuropathy is nerve damage caused by medications such as chemotherapy agents like vincristine and cisplatin, antibiotics like metronidazole, antiretrovirals, and amiodarone. It causes numbness, tingling, and pain in the hands and feet. It is usually mild and improves when the drug is stopped, though some chemotherapy neuropathy can be permanent.

Neurosis is an outdated term for mental disorders causing distress without gross reality distortion. It includes anxiety, depression, OCD, and phobias. It is not life-threatening but impairs functioning. Modern terminology uses specific disorders such as panic disorder and generalized anxiety disorder.

Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease ranges from simple steatosis, which is fatty liver and mild, to nonalcoholic steatohepatitis with inflammation and scarring. Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis can progress to cirrhosis and liver cancer, which are severe to life-threatening. Fatty liver is common and reversible. Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis with significant scarring is moderate, and cirrhosis is life-threatening.

Noninfectious gastroenteritis includes gastroenteritis from food toxins, medications such as NSAIDs or antibiotics, or chemicals. Symptoms are similar to infectious with diarrhea, vomiting, and nausea. Most are mild and self-limiting. Supportive care is sufficient.

Normal pressure hydrocephalus is a type of hydrocephalus in older adults where enlarged brain ventricles cause gait disturbance with a magnetic gait, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline resembling dementia. It is treatable by inserting a shunt to drain fluid, which often improves symptoms. Without treatment, it progresses but is not quickly fatal.

Nose disorders include deviated septum, nasal polyps, sinusitis, rhinitis, and septal perforation. Most are mild and manageable with medication or minor procedures. Rarely, nasal tumors or severe trauma can be moderate to severe.

Obesity, defined as a body mass index of thirty or higher, is a chronic disease of excess body fat. It increases the risk of diabetes, hypertension, heart disease, stroke, sleep apnea, arthritis, and some cancers such as breast and colon. It is not directly fatal but leads to life-threatening complications. Severity ranges from moderate for class one obesity to severe for class three obesity with a body mass index of forty or higher.

OCD is a chronic mental disorder with unwanted, recurrent thoughts called obsessions and repetitive behaviors called compulsions performed to reduce anxiety. It is not life-threatening but can cause significant distress and impairment. Severity ranges from mild and manageable to severe and disabling. Treatment includes cognitive behavioral therapy and SSRIs.

Obstructive sleep apnea causes repeated collapse of the upper airway during sleep. It leads to loud snoring, gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness, and fatigue. Untreated, it increases the risk of hypertension, heart attack, stroke, and sudden death. It is treatable with CPAP, oral appliances, or surgery. Severity ranges from mild to severe, with severe being life-threatening if untreated.

Omphalitis is infection of the umbilical stump in newborns. It causes redness, swelling, pus discharge, and fever. It can spread to the abdominal wall, causing peritonitis and sepsis, which is life-threatening, if untreated. It requires intravenous antibiotics and sometimes surgery.

Onychomycosis is a fungal infection of the nails. It causes thickening, yellow or brown discoloration, and crumbling of the nail. It is not dangerous but may be unsightly and cause pain or secondary bacterial infection. Treatment includes oral antifungals or topical lacquers.

Open wounds from trauma range from superficial abrasions which are mild to deep lacerations, puncture wounds, and avulsions which are moderate. There is a risk of infection, bleeding, and damage to underlying structures such as nerves, vessels, and tendons. Most are mild and heal with cleaning and dressing. Deep wounds require suturing or surgery.

Surgical incisions are clean wounds that are intentionally made. Normal healing is mild. Complications include wound opening, infection, or blood collection, which are moderate. Severity depends on the presence of complications, but is generally mild.

Abdominal open wounds from penetrating trauma risk injury to intra-abdominal organs such as the bowel, liver, spleen, and kidneys, as well as peritonitis and hemorrhage. A superficial abdominal laceration that does not enter the abdominal cavity is mild. A deep wound with organ injury or bowel protruding from the wound is life-threatening.

Open wounds of the arm, such as cuts and lacerations, are usually mild unless they involve major blood vessels like the brachial artery, nerves, or deep muscle injury. Most heal with cleaning and closure. Severity is mild to moderate if deep or complex.
Back wounds are typically mild unless they penetrate into the spine or chest cavity, risking spinal cord injury or lung collapse. Most are superficial and heal well.
Cheek wounds are usually mild, though they may affect facial nerve branches or the parotid duct. Deeper wounds may require plastic surgery for a good cosmetic outcome.

Open chest wounds from penetrating trauma risk lung collapse, blood in the chest cavity, heart injury, or lung laceration. An open pneumothorax, also called a sucking chest wound, is life-threatening and requires immediate covering and a chest tube.

Ear wounds range from lacerations to partial or complete ear avulsion. Cartilage exposure risks infection of the cartilage. Most are mild to moderate. Severity is mild for simple cuts and moderate for avulsions needing microsurgery.

Open globe injury, which is a penetrating eye wound, is an eye emergency. It causes vision loss, infection inside the eye, and potential loss of the eye. Immediate protection, antibiotics, and surgical repair are needed.
Facial wounds are common. Most are mild, such as small lacerations. Deep wounds may damage facial nerves, the parotid duct, or muscles, and require plastic surgery for optimal cosmetic outcome.

Finger wounds range from small cuts which are mild to deep lacerations with tendon or nerve injury which require surgical repair and are moderate. Severity depends on depth and which structures are involved.
Foot wounds depend on depth. Superficial wounds are mild. Deep wounds with tendon, nerve, or vessel injury are moderate. Puncture wounds risk bone infection, especially in diabetics.

Hand wounds are common. Deep lacerations can injure flexor tendons, extensor tendons, nerves such as the median and ulnar nerves, and arteries such as the palmar arch. Such injuries require specialized hand surgery and are moderate. Simple cuts are mild.

An open wound of the head includes scalp lacerations, which can bleed profusely due to rich blood supply but are often mild. However, if the wound penetrates the skull, it can cause traumatic brain injury, bleeding inside the skull, or meningitis, which are severe to life-threatening. Severity depends on depth and associated injuries.

An open wound of the jaw may involve skin, muscle, or underlying bone. If it connects with the mouth, there is a high risk of infection. Fractures of the lower jaw may be present. Most require surgical cleaning and closure, sometimes by an oral surgeon. Severity is moderate, but airway blockage is rare.

Open wounds of the knee range from superficial abrasions which are mild to deep lacerations that may involve the joint capsule or patellar tendon, or cause infection in the joint, which is moderate to severe. Any wound over the knee joint requires careful evaluation for extension into the joint.

Lip wounds are common, especially in children. They can bleed significantly but usually heal well with simple closure. Wounds that cross the vermilion border, which is the lip line, require meticulous repair for cosmetic outcome. Infection risk is low.
Intraoral wounds such as bitten tongue, cheek, or palate are usually mild and heal rapidly due to rich blood supply. However, deep wounds may require suturing. The risk of infection is low.

An open wound of the neck is a surgical emergency because it can injure major blood vessels such as the carotid artery and jugular vein, the airway and esophagus, or nerves. Deep or penetrating neck wounds can cause massive bleeding, airway blockage, or air embolism.
Nasal wounds are usually mild, though they can bleed profusely. Wounds involving nasal cartilage may require careful closure to prevent deformity. Bite wounds have an infection risk.

Shoulder wounds are often mild unless they penetrate into the shoulder joint or involve major nerves such as the brachial plexus or vessels such as the axillary artery. Most superficial wounds heal well.

Oppositional defiant disorder is a childhood behavioral disorder characterized by angry or irritable mood, argumentative or defiant behavior, and vindictiveness. It is not life-threatening but can impair social, academic, and family functioning. Early intervention improves outcomes.

Optic neuritis is inflammation of the optic nerve. It causes sudden vision loss, eye pain especially with movement, and color desaturation. It is often associated with multiple sclerosis. Vision usually recovers partially or fully over weeks, but some residual deficit may remain. It is vision-threatening.

Oral leukoplakia is a white patch on the mouth lining that cannot be scraped off and is not due to another disease. It is a precancerous lesion. One to five percent progress to squamous cell carcinoma over years. Severity is mild but requires monitoring or biopsy to exclude dysplasia or cancer.

Oral mucosal lesions include aphthous ulcers which are canker sores, lichen planus, erythema multiforme, and viral lesions such as herpes. Most are benign and mild, healing spontaneously. Some, such as erosive lichen planus, cause pain and may require treatment. Malignant lesions are rare.

Oral thrush is a yeast infection causing creamy white plaques on the tongue and mouth lining that scrape off, leaving red, bleeding areas. It is common in infants, the elderly, and immunocompromised people such as those with HIV or on chemotherapy, and after antibiotics. It is mild and treatable with topical or oral antifungals. In immunocompromised people, it can become systemic, which is severe.

Orbital cellulitis is an infection of the soft tissues behind the eye. It is usually from the spread of sinusitis or facial injury. It causes eye pain, swelling of the eyelids with bulging of the eye, fever, double vision, and decreased vision. It is an eye emergency because it can cause cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, and blindness. Intravenous antibiotics and sometimes surgical drainage are required.

Orthostatic hypotension is a drop in blood pressure when standing up. It causes dizziness, lightheadedness, and fainting. Causes include dehydration, medications, aging, and autonomic dysfunction. It is usually mild and manageable with hydration, compression stockings, and medication adjustment. Severe cases cause recurrent falls and injuries.

Osteoarthritis is degenerative joint disease from wear and tear of cartilage. It causes pain, stiffness, and reduced mobility. It is not life-threatening but can be disabling. Severity ranges from mild with occasional pain to severe with chronic pain requiring joint replacement. Management includes exercise, weight loss, medications, and surgery.

Osteochondroma is the most common benign bone tumor. It appears as a bony outgrowth near growth plates, usually in children and adolescents. It is typically without symptoms and mild. Rarely, it can cause pain, nerve compression, or fracture, which is moderate. Malignant transformation is extremely rare.

Osteochondrosis is a group of disorders affecting bone growth centers in children. Examples include Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease of the hip, Osgood-Schlatter disease of the knee, and Scheuermann disease of the spine. It causes pain and sometimes deformity but often resolves with growth. Severity is mild to moderate, though Perthes disease may lead to early arthritis.

Osteomyelitis is a bone infection, usually bacterial. It can occur from direct spread from an open fracture or surgery, or from the bloodstream. It causes bone pain, fever, swelling, and can lead to bone death, chronic infection, sepsis, and limb loss. Treatment requires prolonged intravenous antibiotics and often surgical removal of dead bone.

Osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease with low bone mass and deterioration of bone structure. It increases the risk of fractures of the hip, spine, and wrist. It is usually without symptoms until a fracture occurs. Hip fractures in the elderly have high rates of death and disability. Severity ranges from mild with low bone density to severe with multiple fragility fractures and life-threatening hip fracture.

Otitis externa is infection of the ear canal, often caused by Pseudomonas or Staphylococcus. It causes ear pain that is worse with pulling the ear, itching, discharge, and swelling. It is mild and treated with antibiotic ear drops. Severe cases, called malignant otitis externa, occur in diabetics and immunocompromised people and spread to the skull base, which is severe and life-threatening.

Otitis media is a middle ear infection. It is common in children and causes ear pain, fever, and hearing loss. Most are mild and resolve with observation or antibiotics. Complications such as mastoiditis and meningitis are rare but severe. Chronic otitis media with fluid causes hearing issues that are moderate.

Otosclerosis is abnormal bone remodeling in the middle ear. It causes conductive hearing loss by fixing the stapes bone. It is not life-threatening. Hearing loss is usually mild to moderate and can be treated with hearing aids or stapedectomy surgery.

Ovarian cancer is a malignancy of the ovaries. It is often diagnosed at an advanced stage because symptoms such as bloating, pelvic pain, and feeling full early are vague. Early-stage ovarian cancer is curable and moderate. Advanced stage three or four is life-threatening with five-year survival of thirty to forty percent. Treatment includes surgery and chemotherapy.

Ovarian cysts are fluid-filled sacs on the ovary. They are very common and usually benign. Most are functional cysts that resolve spontaneously and are mild. Large cysts can cause pain or twisting of the ovary, which is moderate to severe. Cancerous cysts are rare. Severity is mild for functional cysts and moderate for large or persistent cysts.

Ovarian torsion is the twisting of the ovary on its supporting ligaments, cutting off blood supply. It causes sudden, severe lower abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. It is a surgical emergency. Without prompt untwisting surgery, the ovary becomes dead and must be removed. Severity is severe due to potential loss of the ovary, but not life-threatening unless complicated by infection.

Overflow incontinence is involuntary leakage of urine due to a chronically overfull bladder. It is often from bladder outlet obstruction such as enlarged prostate or neurogenic bladder from diabetes or spinal cord injury. It causes frequent or constant dribbling. It is not life-threatening but reduces quality of life. Treatment addresses the underlying cause with catheterization, medication, or surgery.

Postoperative pain is expected after surgery, typically lasting days to weeks. It is usually mild to moderate and managed with multimodal analgesia including opioids, NSAIDs, and local anesthetics. Severe uncontrolled pain can delay recovery. It is not life-threatening but requires proper management.

Neck pain disorders include cervical strain, cervical spondylosis, whiplash, and disc herniation. Most are mild to moderate and resolve with conservative care such as physical therapy and analgesics. Severe causes such as spinal cord compression, infection, or tumor are rare.

Pancreatic cancer is one of the deadliest cancers. It is often diagnosed late due to vague symptoms such as weight loss, yellowing of the skin, and abdominal pain. Only twenty percent are resectable at diagnosis. Overall five-year survival is about ten percent. It is life-threatening. Even with treatment, the prognosis is poor.

A panic attack is a sudden episode of intense fear with physical symptoms such as palpitations, sweating, trembling, shortness of breath, chest pain, and fear of dying. It is not life-threatening but is very distressing. Severity is mild and self-limited, but it can lead to panic disorder if recurrent. Treatment includes therapy and SSRIs.

Panic disorder is recurrent, unexpected panic attacks with persistent worry about future attacks. This leads to avoidance behavior called agoraphobia. It is not life-threatening but impairs quality of life. Severity ranges from mild to moderate. Effective treatments include cognitive behavioral therapy and SSRIs.

Parasitic diseases include protozoa such as malaria, toxoplasmosis, and giardiasis, helminths such as tapeworms and roundworms, and ectoparasites such as scabies and lice. Severity varies widely. Giardiasis is mild. Malaria is moderate to life-threatening. Neurocysticercosis is severe. Filariasis causes chronic disability. Treatment is with antiparasitic drugs.

A parathyroid adenoma is a benign tumor of one or more parathyroid glands. It causes primary hyperparathyroidism with elevated calcium. Mild hypercalcemia may be without symptoms and is mild. Severe hypercalcemia above fourteen milligrams per deciliter causes confusion, kidney stones, and arrhythmias, which is severe. Most are mild and curable with surgical removal.

Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder. It causes tremor, rigidity, slowness of movement, and postural instability. It is not directly fatal but leads to disability, falls, aspiration pneumonia, and death from complications. Average survival is ten to twenty years after diagnosis. Severity ranges from mild and responsive to medication to severe and advanced with wheelchair dependence and dementia.

Paronychia is infection of the skin around a fingernail or toenail. It is often caused by Staphylococcus or Candida. It causes redness, swelling, and pain. Most are mild and resolve with warm soaks and antibiotics. An abscess requires drainage and is moderate. Severe spreading infection called felon is rare.

Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia is a sudden, rapid heart rate of one hundred fifty to two hundred fifty beats per minute originating above the ventricles. It causes palpitations, dizziness, chest discomfort, and sometimes fainting. Episodes usually stop on their own or respond to simple maneuvers or medication. It is not life-threatening in otherwise healthy hearts, but prolonged episodes can cause low blood pressure. Treatment includes catheter ablation which can be curative.

Ventricular tachycardia is a fast, life-threatening heart rhythm originating in the ventricles. It often occurs in patients with structural heart disease such as prior heart attack or cardiomyopathy. Sustained ventricular tachycardia causes palpitations, dizziness, fainting, and can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation, which causes sudden cardiac death. It is a medical emergency requiring cardioversion or antiarrhythmic drugs.

A pelvic fistula is an abnormal connection between pelvic organs. Examples include rectovaginal, vesicovaginal, and enterovesical fistulas. Causes include childbirth trauma, surgery, Crohn disease, and cancer. Symptoms include leakage of stool or urine from the vagina, recurrent urinary tract infections, and fecal incontinence. It is not life-threatening but severely impairs quality of life. Surgical repair is often needed.

Pelvic inflammatory disease is infection of the upper genital tract including the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries. It is usually from sexually transmitted infections such as chlamydia and gonorrhea. Symptoms include lower abdominal pain, fever, abnormal discharge, and pain with intercourse. Untreated pelvic inflammatory disease leads to infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain, which is moderate to severe. It is curable with antibiotics. Severe cases may require hospitalization.

Pelvic organ prolapse occurs when pelvic floor muscles weaken, allowing pelvic organs such as the bladder, uterus, or rectum to descend into the vagina. Symptoms include pelvic pressure, a bulging sensation, and urinary or bowel issues. It is not life-threatening but reduces quality of life. Severity ranges from mild and without symptoms to moderate and symptomatic, requiring a pessary or surgery.

Pemphigus is a group of rare autoimmune blistering diseases. It causes painful blisters and erosions on the skin and mucous membranes of the mouth and genitals. Without treatment, widespread skin loss leads to infection, dehydration, and death, which is life-threatening. With corticosteroids and immunosuppressants, the death rate has dropped to five to ten percent.

Pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardium, the lining around the heart. It causes sharp, positional chest pain that improves by leaning forward, fever, and a pericardial friction rub. Most cases are viral or unknown cause and mild to moderate, resolving with NSAIDs and colchicine. Complications include fluid around the heart and cardiac tamponade, which is life-threatening.

Peripheral arterial disease is atherosclerosis of the leg arteries. It causes leg pain with walking called claudication, cold feet, and non-healing ulcers. It is a marker for coronary and cerebrovascular disease. Severe peripheral arterial disease with critical limb ischemia leads to gangrene and amputation, which is severe. Management includes lifestyle changes, antiplatelet drugs, and revascularization.

Peripheral arterial embolism is a sudden blockage of an artery, usually in the leg, by a blood clot often from the heart in atrial fibrillation. It causes acute pain, paleness, pulselessness, paralysis, and coolness. It is a surgical emergency. Without surgical removal of the clot, limb loss occurs within hours. Severity is severe with limb-threatening to life-threatening if massive embolism causes shock.

Peripheral nerve disorders include mononeuropathies such as carpal tunnel syndrome and ulnar neuropathy, and polyneuropathies such as diabetic and alcoholic neuropathy. Symptoms include numbness, tingling, pain, and weakness. Most are mild to moderate and not life-threatening. Treatment addresses the underlying cause such as diabetes control or B12 supplements. Severe polyneuropathy causes disability but is rarely fatal.

Perirectal infections include anal abscesses and fistulas. An abscess is a painful, swollen area near the anus with fever, requiring incision and drainage, which is moderate. Fistulas are tunnels from the anal canal to the skin, causing recurrent drainage, which is mild to moderate. Untreated, infection can spread to the perineum, causing Fournier gangrene, which is life-threatening.

Peritonitis is inflammation of the peritoneum, the abdominal lining. It is usually from bacterial contamination from a perforated appendix, ulcer, diverticulitis, or from surgery or a dialysis catheter. It causes severe abdominal pain, a rigid board-like abdomen, fever, nausea, and septic shock. It is a medical emergency requiring antibiotics and often surgery. Death rate is significant at five to fifty percent depending on the cause.

A peritonsillar abscess is a collection of pus between the tonsil and the pharyngeal wall. It is a complication of tonsillitis. It causes severe sore throat, a hot potato voice, drooling, difficulty opening the mouth, and fever. It requires drainage and antibiotics. Airway blockage is rare but possible and severe.

Chronic unexplained vomiting includes functional vomiting, cyclic vomiting syndrome, and rumination syndrome. It leads to dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, and malnutrition. It is not directly life-threatening but can cause serious complications such as tears in the esophagus, aspiration pneumonia, and metabolic problems. Severity ranges from mild and manageable to moderate requiring hospital intravenous fluids.

Personality disorders such as borderline, narcissistic, and antisocial are enduring patterns of inner experience and behavior that differ from cultural expectations. They cause distress or impairment. They are not life-threatening but severely impact relationships, work, and mental health. Borderline personality disorder carries a suicide risk that is moderate.

Peyronie disease is excessive scarring in the penis, causing curvature during erection, pain, and sometimes erectile dysfunction. It is not life-threatening but can cause sexual dysfunction and psychological distress. Severity is mild to moderate. Treatments include oral medications, injections, or surgery for severe curves.

Pharyngitis, or sore throat, is usually viral and self-limiting, which is mild. Bacterial strep throat is mild but requires antibiotics to prevent rheumatic fever. Severe complications such as peritonsillar abscess or retropharyngeal abscess are rare but moderate.

Phimosis is the inability to retract the foreskin over the glans penis. It is physiological in children and normal, which is mild. Pathologic phimosis in adults is scarred and can cause pain, difficulty urinating, ballooning of the foreskin, and increased infection risk. Severe phimosis with urinary retention or paraphimosis, where the foreskin is trapped behind the glans, is an emergency and moderate.

A pilonidal cyst is a skin cyst near the tailbone, often containing hair and debris. Asymptomatic cysts are mild. Infected cysts cause a painful abscess that is moderate, requiring incision and drainage. Chronic pilonidal disease may require surgical excision.

A pinguecula is a yellowish, raised growth on the conjunctiva next to the cornea, often on the nasal side. It is caused by UV exposure, dust, and dry eyes. It is benign, painless, and mild. It rarely becomes inflamed, which is moderate. It does not grow over the cornea.

Pinworm infection is a common intestinal parasite in children. It causes perianal itching, especially at night. It is mild and easily treated with oral mebendazole or pyrantel pamoate. No serious complications.

Pituitary adenomas are benign tumors of the pituitary gland. Non-functional microadenomas smaller than one centimeter are mild and often found incidentally. Macroadenomas larger than one centimeter can cause mass effects such as headache and visual field defects, and pituitary hormone deficits, which are moderate. Functional adenomas cause hormone excess such as prolactinoma, Cushing disease, or acromegaly, which are moderate.

Pituitary disorders include hypopituitarism with hormone deficiency and hyperpituitarism with hormone excess such as prolactinoma, acromegaly, and Cushing disease. Hypopituitarism is mild and managed with hormone replacement. Pituitary apoplexy, which is sudden bleeding into a pituitary tumor, causes severe headache, vision loss, and adrenal crisis, which is life-threatening. Severity ranges from mild to life-threatening.

Pityriasis rosea is a common, self-limiting skin rash. It starts with a herald patch, which is a single large scaly patch, followed by smaller oval patches along skin lines in a Christmas tree pattern. It lasts six to eight weeks, causes mild itching, and is not dangerous.

Placenta previa is when the placenta partially or completely covers the cervix. It causes painless vaginal bleeding in the second or third trimester. It is a serious pregnancy complication because it can cause massive hemorrhage during labor or before, endangering mother and fetus. Management includes pelvic rest, hospitalization, and planned cesarean section.

Placental abruption is the premature separation of the placenta from the uterus. It causes vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, uterine contractions, and fetal distress. It is a medical emergency. Severe abruption leads to maternal hemorrhage, shock, blood clotting problems, and fetal death. Immediate delivery is often required.

Plantar fasciitis is inflammation of the thick band of tissue on the bottom of the foot called the plantar fascia. It causes heel pain, especially with the first steps in the morning. It is not dangerous and resolves with stretching, orthotics, night splints, and sometimes steroid injections or shockwave therapy.

Pleural effusion is excess fluid in the pleural space between the lung and chest wall. Small effusions are mild and without symptoms. Large effusions cause shortness of breath and chest pain, which is moderate. Empyema which is pus and malignant effusion are severe. Massive effusion can cause respiratory failure, which is life-threatening.

Pneumoconiosis is a lung disease from inhaling mineral dust. Types include coal worker pneumoconiosis or black lung, silicosis, and asbestosis. It causes progressive scarring of the lungs, shortness of breath, and increased risk of lung cancer. Severity ranges from mild and without symptoms to severe with respiratory failure and death. It is not reversible.

Pneumonia is infection of the lung air sacs. It causes fever, cough, sputum, shortness of breath, and chest pain. Most community-acquired pneumonia is mild to moderate and treatable with oral antibiotics. Severe pneumonia in the elderly, immunocompromised, or those with other diseases causes respiratory failure, sepsis, and death, which is life-threatening.

Pneumothorax is air in the pleural space causing lung collapse. Spontaneous pneumothorax in tall, thin young males causes sudden chest pain and shortness of breath. Small ones are mild and resolve with observation. Tension pneumothorax, where air accumulation under pressure shifts the heart, is life-threatening, causing low blood pressure, breathing distress, and cardiac arrest. It requires immediate needle decompression.

Analgesic poisoning includes acetaminophen, NSAIDs, and opioids. Acetaminophen overdose causes severe liver injury and failure, which is life-threatening, and is treatable with N-acetylcysteine. NSAID overdose usually causes stomach upset which is mild, but severe poisoning causes kidney failure. Opioid poisoning causes respiratory depression, coma, and death, which is life-threatening and reversible with naloxone.

Anticonvulsant poisoning from phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, or lamotrigine causes neurological symptoms such as nystagmus, ataxia, and drowsiness. In severe overdose, it causes coma and respiratory depression. Valproate can cause liver damage. Most cases are mild to moderate and treated with supportive care and sometimes activated charcoal.

Antidepressant poisoning includes SSRIs, tricyclics, and MAOIs. SSRI overdose is usually mild, causing serotonin syndrome with agitation, high body temperature, and rigidity, but is rarely fatal. Tricyclic antidepressant overdose is life-threatening, causing widened heart rhythm, ventricular arrhythmias, seizures, and death. MAOI overdose causes dangerously high blood pressure.

Antihypertensive poisoning includes beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, ACE inhibitors, and clonidine. It causes low blood pressure, slow heart rate, and cardiovascular collapse. Beta-blocker and calcium channel blocker overdose are severe, causing cardiogenic shock and death, which is life-threatening. ACE inhibitor overdose causes low blood pressure but is less severe.

Antimicrobial poisoning from antibiotics, antivirals, and antifungals usually causes mild symptoms such as nausea, diarrhea, and rash. It is rarely life-threatening except for very high doses, such as intravenous acyclovir causing kidney failure or aminoglycosides causing kidney and ear toxicity. Isoniazid overdose causes seizures, which is moderate.

Antipsychotic poisoning from typical agents like haloperidol and atypical agents like quetiapine and olanzapine causes drowsiness, low blood pressure, heart rhythm changes, and anticholinergic effects. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is rare but life-threatening, with fever, rigidity, and autonomic instability.

Ethylene glycol, which is antifreeze, poisoning causes inebriation, then metabolic acidosis, calcium oxalate crystals in the urine, acute kidney injury, and death. It is a medical emergency treated with fomepizole or ethanol and hemodialysis.

Gas poisoning includes carbon monoxide and cyanide. Carbon monoxide poisoning from car exhaust or house fires causes headache, confusion, coma, and death. It is treated with one hundred percent oxygen or hyperbaric oxygen. Cyanide poisoning causes rapid loss of consciousness, seizures, respiratory arrest, and is treated with a cyanide antidote kit.

Opioid poisoning from heroin, fentanyl, or prescription painkillers causes respiratory depression, pinpoint pupils, unresponsiveness, and death. It is a life-threatening emergency, reversed by naloxone. Respiratory support is crucial.

Sedative poisoning from benzodiazepines, barbiturates, or z-drugs causes drowsiness, slurred speech, ataxia, and in severe cases, coma and respiratory depression, which is life-threatening. Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines but may cause seizures.

Polycystic kidney disease is a genetic disorder causing multiple cysts in the kidneys. It leads to progressive kidney enlargement and chronic kidney disease. Most patients develop end-stage renal disease by age fifty to seventy. It also causes liver cysts and brain aneurysms, which can rupture and cause life-threatening bleeding. Severity ranges from moderate with preserved function to life-threatening with kidney failure or ruptured aneurysm.

PCOS is a hormonal disorder affecting women of reproductive age. It causes irregular periods, ovarian cysts, excess androgen leading to hirsutism and acne, and insulin resistance. It is not life-threatening but increases the long-term risk of diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and endometrial cancer.

Polycythemia vera is a blood disorder with overproduction of red blood cells. This causes increased blood thickness, leading to headaches, itching after warm baths, and increased risk of blood clots such as stroke, heart attack, and pulmonary embolism, and bleeding. Untreated, it is life-threatening due to clot events. Treatment with phlebotomy, hydroxyurea, and aspirin improves survival.

Polymyalgia rheumatica is an inflammatory disorder causing pain and stiffness in the shoulders, hips, neck, and thighs, especially in the morning. It is often associated with giant cell arteritis, which causes headache, jaw pain with chewing, and blindness, which is severe. PMR alone is mild and responds dramatically to low-dose corticosteroids.

PTSD is a psychiatric disorder after exposure to trauma. It causes intrusive memories such as flashbacks, avoidance, negative mood, and hyperarousal such as insomnia and irritability. It is not life-threatening but significantly impairs functioning and increases suicide risk. Treatment includes psychotherapy and SSRIs.

Postoperative infections occur after surgery. They can be incisional or organ or space infections. Symptoms include fever, wound redness, swelling, pain, and pus drainage. Most are mild to moderate, treated with antibiotics and sometimes drainage. Deep organ or space infections can cause sepsis, which is life-threatening.

Postpartum depression is moderate to severe depression following childbirth, within four weeks to one year. Symptoms include sadness, exhaustion, difficulty bonding with the baby, and rarely suicidal or infanticidal thoughts. It is treatable with therapy and antidepressants. Severity ranges from mild baby blues to severe postpartum psychosis, which is a risk to the child.

Preeclampsia is a pregnancy complication after twenty weeks. It causes high blood pressure, protein in the urine, and organ dysfunction in the liver, kidneys, or brain. Symptoms include headache, vision changes, and upper abdominal pain. It can progress to eclampsia with seizures, which is life-threatening, and HELLP syndrome. Immediate delivery is often required.

Pregnancy is a normal physiological state, not a disease. However, it can have complications such as gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, ectopic pregnancy, and miscarriage. A healthy pregnancy is mild and low risk. High-risk pregnancies with multiple gestations, chronic hypertension, or diabetes have moderate risk.

Premature atrial contractions are early heartbeats originating in the atria. They are common in healthy people. They are almost always benign and mild, causing occasional palpitations. No treatment is needed unless very symptomatic or if there is underlying heart disease.

Premature ovarian failure, also called primary ovarian insufficiency, is loss of ovarian function before age forty. It causes infertility, irregular periods, and estrogen deficiency symptoms such as hot flashes and vaginal dryness. It is not life-threatening but increases the risk of osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease. Hormone replacement therapy manages symptoms.

Premature rupture of membranes is rupture of the amniotic sac before labor. Preterm premature rupture of membranes before thirty-seven weeks increases the risk of infection of the amniotic sac, cord prolapse, and preterm delivery. Amniotic sac infection is life-threatening to both mother and fetus. Severity ranges from mild at term to life-threatening with preterm rupture and infection.

Premature ventricular contractions are early heartbeats originating in the ventricles. Isolated PVCs in a healthy heart are benign and mild. Frequent PVCs with more than twenty percent burden can cause heart muscle weakness, which is moderate. In the setting of heart disease, PVCs may trigger ventricular tachycardia, which is life-threatening.

Premenstrual syndrome includes physical symptoms such as bloating and breast tenderness and emotional symptoms such as irritability and mood swings in the luteal phase. Premenstrual dysphoric disorder is a severe form that is moderate. Symptoms resolve with menstruation.

Presbycusis is age-related sensorineural hearing loss, typically high-frequency loss, gradual and in both ears. It is not life-threatening but reduces quality of life and communication. Treatment includes hearing aids.

Presbyopia is age-related loss of near vision due to stiffening of the lens. It makes reading difficult, usually starting at age forty to forty-five. It is not a disease but a normal aging change. Corrected with reading glasses, bifocals, or monovision LASIK.

Priapism is a persistent, painful erection lasting more than four hours unrelated to sexual stimulation. It is a urologic emergency. Prolonged erection causes lack of blood flow, scarring, and permanent erectile dysfunction. Causes include sickle cell disease, medications such as trazodone, and leukemia. Treatment includes aspiration, injection of medication, or surgery.

Primary immunodeficiencies are genetic disorders impairing immune function. Examples include common variable immunodeficiency and severe combined immunodeficiency. Severity ranges from mild, such as IgA deficiency which is often without symptoms, to life-threatening, such as severe combined immunodeficiency which requires bone marrow transplant in infancy. Many cause recurrent infections, autoimmunity, and cancer.

Primary insomnia is difficulty falling or staying asleep without a medical or psychiatric cause. It is not life-threatening but impairs daytime function, mood, and quality of life. Severity ranges from mild and occasional to moderate and chronic and disabling. Treatment includes cognitive behavioral therapy and sometimes medication.

Primary kidney disease includes glomerulonephritis, nephrotic syndrome, and interstitial nephritis. It causes progressive loss of kidney function. Severity varies. Minimal change disease is mild and steroid-responsive. Membranous nephropathy is moderate. Crescentic glomerulonephritis is rapidly progressive and life-threatening without immunosuppression. Some progress to end-stage renal disease, which is life-threatening without dialysis or transplant.

Primary thrombocythemia, also called essential thrombocythemia, is a blood disorder with excessive platelet production. It increases the risk of blood clots such as stroke, heart attack, and DVT, and bleeding. It is usually without symptoms and mild but can cause moderate to severe complications. Treatment with low-dose aspirin and hydroxyurea reduces risk.

This category includes hyperemesis gravidarum which is mild to moderate, preeclampsia which is moderate to severe, gestational diabetes which is mild, placental abruption which is life-threatening, uterine rupture which is life-threatening, and eclampsia which is life-threatening. Severity depends entirely on the specific complication.

Prostate cancer is the most common cancer in men. Most are low-grade, slow-growing, and mild, often monitored with active surveillance. Aggressive, high-grade, or metastatic prostate cancer is life-threatening. Treatment includes surgery, radiation, hormone therapy, and chemotherapy.

Prostatitis is inflammation of the prostate. Acute bacterial prostatitis causes fever, chills, pain, and urinary symptoms and is moderate, requiring antibiotics. Chronic prostatitis or chronic pelvic pain syndrome is mild to moderate, causing persistent discomfort and urinary issues. It is not life-threatening.

Protein deficiency, also called protein-energy malnutrition, occurs in starvation, eating disorders, and chronic diseases. It leads to muscle wasting, swelling, anemia, and impaired immunity. Severe deficiency in children called kwashiorkor or marasmus is life-threatening. In adults, mild deficiency is moderate and reversible with nutrition.

Pseudohypoparathyroidism is a genetic disorder where the body is resistant to parathyroid hormone. It causes low calcium and high phosphorus. Symptoms include tetany and seizures if low calcium is severe. Most cases are mild and managed with calcium and vitamin D supplements.

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, formerly called pseudotumor cerebri, is increased pressure inside the skull without a brain tumor. It causes headaches, vision changes, and pulsatile tinnitus. It is not life-threatening but can cause permanent vision loss if untreated, which is severe. Treatment includes weight loss, acetazolamide, and sometimes surgical shunting.

Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune skin condition causing red, scaly plaques, most often on the elbows, knees, and scalp. It is not life-threatening but reduces quality of life. Mild psoriasis involves less than five percent of body surface area. Moderate to severe psoriasis may require systemic treatment with biologics. Psoriatic arthritis causes joint damage and is moderate.

Psychosexual disorders include sexual dysfunction such as low desire, erectile dysfunction, and anorgasmia, and paraphilias which are unusual sexual interests. They are not life-threatening but cause distress and relationship problems. Treatment includes sex therapy, psychotherapy, and sometimes medication.

Psychotic disorders such as schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, delusional disorder, and brief psychotic disorder involve hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thinking. They are not directly life-threatening but increase the risk of self-harm, suicide, and inability to care for oneself. Treatment includes antipsychotics and therapy. Severity ranges from mild and well-controlled to severe with frequent episodes and disability.

A pterygium is a fleshy, triangular growth of conjunctiva onto the cornea. It is often from UV exposure. It is benign and mild, causing irritation or redness. If it grows over the visual axis, it blurs vision, which is moderate, and requires surgical removal.

Pulmonary congestion is fluid accumulation in the lungs. It is most often due to heart failure, called cardiogenic pulmonary edema. It causes shortness of breath, difficulty breathing when lying flat, and crackles in the lungs. Severe pulmonary edema causes respiratory failure and death, which is life-threatening. Treatment includes diuretics, oxygen, and treating the underlying cause.

Pulmonary embolism is a blockage of a pulmonary artery by a blood clot, usually from a deep vein thrombosis. It causes chest pain, shortness of breath, rapid breathing, coughing up blood, and collapse. Massive pulmonary embolism causes obstructive shock and death, with high death rates. Treatment includes blood thinners, clot-busting drugs, or surgical removal of the clot.

Pulmonary eosinophilia, also called eosinophilic pneumonia, is inflammation of the lungs with high eosinophils. It is often due to drugs, parasites, or allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis. It causes cough, fever, and wheezing. Most cases are mild to moderate and respond to corticosteroids.

Pulmonary fibrosis is scarring of lung tissue. It causes progressive shortness of breath, dry cough, and low oxygen. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis has a median survival of three to five years and is life-threatening. Other causes from drugs or connective tissue diseases may stabilize with treatment. Severity ranges from moderate with slow progression to life-threatening with severe fibrosis and respiratory failure.

Pulmonary hypertension is high blood pressure in the pulmonary arteries. It causes right heart failure, shortness of breath, fatigue, and fainting. Untreated, it is progressive and fatal, with a median survival of two to three years for some types. Treatment includes vasodilators, oxygen, and diuretics.

Pulmonic valve disease includes stenosis which is narrowing and regurgitation which is leaking. It is less common than left-sided valve disease. Mild pulmonic stenosis is often congenital and mild. Severe stenosis causes right heart failure, which is moderate. Most cases are without symptoms and mild, requiring no intervention.

Pyelonephritis is a kidney infection, usually ascending from a urinary tract infection. It causes fever, flank pain, nausea, and vomiting. It is moderate and requires oral or intravenous antibiotics. Severe pyelonephritis causes sepsis, which is life-threatening, and kidney abscess. Most patients recover fully with treatment.

Pyloric stenosis is narrowing of the pylorus, which is the stomach outlet. It usually occurs in infants at two to eight weeks old. It causes projectile vomiting, dehydration, and failure to thrive. It is treatable with surgery called pyloromyotomy, which is curative. Without treatment, severe dehydration and electrolyte problems can be life-threatening.

Pyogenic skin infections are pus-forming. They include impetigo, folliculitis, furuncles which are boils, and carbuncles. Most are mild, treated with topical or oral antibiotics. Severe infections such as cellulitis or abscess are moderate. Spread to the bloodstream causing sepsis is rare but life-threatening.

Raynaud disease is episodic spasm of the blood vessels in the fingers and toes triggered by cold or stress. It causes color changes from white to blue to red, numbness, and pain. Primary Raynaud is benign and mild. Secondary Raynaud from autoimmune diseases such as scleroderma or lupus can cause tissue damage, ulcers, and gangrene, which is moderate to severe.

Reactive arthritis, also called Reiter syndrome, is joint inflammation triggered by a gastrointestinal or genitourinary infection. It often occurs with conjunctivitis and urethritis. It is usually mild and self-limiting, resolving in months. Chronic or severe cases may require NSAIDs, steroids, or stronger medications.

Rectal disorders include hemorrhoids, anal fissures, proctitis, and rectal prolapse. Most are mild, such as hemorrhoids and fissures, and treatable with conservative measures or minor procedures. Rectal prolapse can be moderate and require surgery. Cancer is severe.

Restless leg syndrome is a neurological disorder causing an uncontrollable urge to move the legs, especially at night, with uncomfortable sensations such as creeping and crawling. It is not life-threatening but disrupts sleep and quality of life. Treatment includes iron supplementation if deficient, dopamine agonists, or gabapentin.

Retinal detachment is the separation of the sensory retina from the underlying layer. It causes sudden flashes of light, floaters, and a curtain-like shadow over vision. It is an eye emergency because without prompt surgical repair, it leads to permanent blindness.

disease_name: retinopathy due to high blood pressure
description: Hypertensive retinopathy is damage to retinal blood vessels from chronic or severe high blood pressure. Mild retinopathy with arteriolar narrowing and AV nicking is without symptoms. Moderate retinopathy with hemorrhages and exudates indicates target organ damage. Malignant hypertension causes swelling of the optic disc and vision loss, which is severe.

Rhabdomyolysis is the breakdown of muscle tissue, releasing myoglobin into the bloodstream. It causes dark urine, muscle pain, weakness, and elevated creatine kinase. It can lead to acute kidney injury, high potassium, low calcium, and compartment syndrome. Severe rhabdomyolysis causes kidney failure and death, which is life-threatening. Severity ranges from mild with no symptoms to life-threatening.

Rheumatic fever is an inflammatory complication of untreated strep throat. It causes arthritis, heart inflammation with valve damage, chorea, skin rash, and nodules under the skin. Heart inflammation can cause permanent valve damage called rheumatic heart disease, which is moderate to severe, leading to heart failure and death.

Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune disease causing symmetric inflammation of the small joints of the hands and feet. It causes pain, swelling, morning stiffness, joint erosion, and deformity. Systemic complications include inflammation of blood vessels, lung disease, heart disease, and lymphoma. Severity ranges from mild and manageable with medications to severe and disabling. It reduces life expectancy.

Rocky Mountain spotted fever is a tick-borne bacterial infection. It causes fever, headache, rash that starts on the wrists and ankles, and abdominal pain. Without treatment with doxycycline, it causes inflammation of blood vessels, multi-organ failure, and death, with death rates over twenty percent. With early treatment, death rates are below one percent.

Rosacea is a chronic skin condition causing facial redness, flushing, visible blood vessels, bumps and pus-filled lesions, and sometimes eye irritation called ocular rosacea. It is not dangerous but can be cosmetically bothersome. Treatment includes topical metronidazole, azelaic acid, oral doxycycline, and laser for blood vessels.

Rotator cuff injury includes tendinitis, tendinosis, and partial or full-thickness tears of the shoulder tendons. Symptoms include shoulder pain especially with overhead activity, weakness, and limited range of motion. Most are mild to moderate, treated with physical therapy, anti-inflammatory drugs, and steroid injections. Large tears may require surgery.

Salivary gland disorders include sialadenitis which is infection, sialolithiasis which are stones, and Sjögren syndrome which is autoimmune. Most cause pain, swelling, and dry mouth. Infections and stones are mild to moderate and treatable with antibiotics or stone removal. Sjögren syndrome is chronic but mild with symptomatic management. Malignant tumors are rare but severe.

Sarcoidosis is a granulomatous disorder of unknown cause. It affects the lungs in ninety percent of cases, as well as the skin, eyes, lymph nodes, and heart. Many cases are without symptoms or self-limited and mild. Severe sarcoidosis causes pulmonary fibrosis, heart arrhythmias causing sudden death, and nervous system involvement, which is life-threatening.

Scabies is a contagious skin infestation by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei. It causes intense itching, worse at night, and linear burrows. It is not dangerous but very uncomfortable. Treatment is topical permethrin or oral ivermectin.

A scar is fibrous tissue replacing normal skin after injury from surgery, trauma, or burn. Most scars are without symptoms and mild. Hypertrophic scars and keloids cause itching, pain, and cosmetic concern, which is mild to moderate. Scars are not life-threatening.

Scarlet fever is a complication of strep throat caused by an erythrogenic toxin. It causes a fine red rash with a sandpaper texture that blanches with pressure, a strawberry tongue, and fever. It is mild and resolves with antibiotics. Rheumatic fever is a possible complication if untreated and is moderate to severe.

Schizophrenia is a severe mental disorder with positive symptoms such as hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized speech, and negative symptoms such as flat affect, lack of pleasure, and lack of motivation. It is not directly fatal but increases suicide risk by five to ten percent and mortality from physical illness. Treatment includes antipsychotics and psychosocial support.

Sciatica is pain radiating along the sciatic nerve through the buttock, back of the leg, and foot. It is often from a lumbar disc herniation or spinal stenosis. Most cases are mild and resolve with conservative treatment such as physical therapy, anti-inflammatory drugs, and steroid injections. Severe cases with progressive weakness or cauda equina syndrome causing loss of bowel or bladder control require surgery, which is severe.

Scleritis is severe inflammation of the sclera, which is the white of the eye. It causes deep, boring eye pain, redness, and tenderness. It is often associated with autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. It can lead to thinning of the sclera, perforation, and vision loss, which is severe. Treatment includes NSAIDs and immunosuppressants.

Scleroderma, also called systemic sclerosis, is an autoimmune connective tissue disease. It causes skin thickening, fibrosis of internal organs such as the lungs, heart, kidneys, and digestive tract, and vascular damage. Limited scleroderma, also called CREST syndrome, is moderate. Diffuse scleroderma with organ involvement such as pulmonary hypertension or scleroderma renal crisis is life-threatening.

Scoliosis is a lateral curvature of the spine. It is often idiopathic in adolescents. Mild curves under twenty degrees are without symptoms and not treated. Moderate curves from twenty to forty degrees may require bracing. Severe curves over fifty degrees can cause breathing problems from restrictive lung disease and pain, which is moderate.

Scurvy is vitamin C deficiency. It causes fatigue, gum disease with swollen bleeding gums, bruising, small red spots from bleeding, poor wound healing, and joint pain. It is rare in developed countries. It is treatable with vitamin C supplementation and fully reversible. Without treatment, severe scurvy causes internal bleeding and death, which is life-threatening.

Seasonal allergic rhinitis, also called hay fever, is caused by pollen from grass, trees, and weeds. It causes sneezing, runny nose, itchy eyes, and congestion. It is not dangerous but reduces quality of life. Treatment includes antihistamines, nasal steroids, and immunotherapy.

A sebaceous cyst, also called an epidermoid cyst, is a benign, slow-growing, painless lump under the skin filled with keratin. It is not dangerous. If infected, it becomes red, painful, and swollen, which is moderate. Treatment is surgical removal.

Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic, inflammatory skin condition causing scaly, flaky, itchy patches on the scalp as dandruff, the face such as the eyebrows and nasolabial folds, the chest, and the back. It is not dangerous but can be bothersome. Treatment includes antifungal shampoos and topical steroids.

Seborrheic keratosis is a benign, common, waxy, stuck-on-looking skin growth, usually brown or black. It is not cancerous and does not become cancerous. It is without symptoms and only removed for cosmetic reasons.

Sensorineural hearing loss occurs from damage to the cochlea or auditory nerve. Causes include aging, noise trauma, ototoxic drugs, and Meniere disease. It is often permanent. Mild loss causes difficulty hearing faint speech. Severe loss requires hearing aids or cochlear implants. It is not life-threatening.

Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock is sepsis with persistent low blood pressure requiring vasopressors, with high death rates of thirty to fifty percent.
