Symptoms include fever, low blood pressure, confusion, and high lactate. Early antibiotics and resuscitation are critical.

Septic arthritis is bacterial infection of a joint, most commonly the knee, hip, or shoulder. It causes severe pain, swelling, redness, and inability to move the joint. It is an orthopedic emergency because joint destruction occurs within days. Treatment is emergency joint drainage and intravenous antibiotics. Death rate is low, but permanent joint damage is high.

Shingles is the reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus, which causes chickenpox. It causes a painful, blistering rash in a band on one side of the body. It is self-limiting in two to four weeks. Complications include postherpetic neuralgia with persistent pain, which is moderate to severe, and in immunocompromised people, widespread shingles, which is life-threatening. Antiviral drugs reduce severity.

Sialoadenitis is inflammation of the salivary glands, usually the parotid or submandibular glands. It is often from infection or a stone. Symptoms include swelling, pain, fever, and pus. Most cases are mild and treated with antibiotics, hydration, and sour candies to stimulate saliva. Severe abscess may require drainage.

Sick sinus syndrome is a group of heart rhythm disorders caused by dysfunction of the sinoatrial node. It leads to slow heart rate, pauses, palpitations, and fainting. It is often associated with atrial fibrillation, called tachy-brady syndrome. Treatment is pacemaker implantation, which is curative. Without treatment, symptoms cause falls and injuries.

Sickle cell anemia is a genetic blood disorder where red blood cells become sickle-shaped under low oxygen. It causes chronic hemolytic anemia, painful vaso-occlusive crises, acute chest syndrome, stroke, and organ damage. Life expectancy is reduced to forty to sixty years. It is severe and life-threatening due to complications such as stroke, sepsis, and kidney failure.

Sickle cell crisis is an acute worsening of sickle cell disease. Types include vaso-occlusive crisis causing severe pain in bones, chest, and abdomen, aplastic crisis with low reticulocytes, sequestration crisis with spleen enlargement and shock, and acute chest syndrome with fever, chest pain, and low oxygen. It is life-threatening if not treated promptly with intravenous fluids, pain control, and sometimes blood transfusion.

Sinus bradycardia is a slow heart rate below sixty beats per minute but with a normal rhythm. It is common in athletes and is physiological and without symptoms, which is mild. Symptomatic bradycardia with dizziness or fainting may require evaluation for heart block or hypothyroidism and is moderate.

Sjögren syndrome is an autoimmune disease causing dry eyes and dry mouth. It is often associated with rheumatoid arthritis or lupus. It is not life-threatening but impairs quality of life. Severe Sjögren syndrome can cause lymphoma in five to ten percent of cases, which is moderate to severe.

Skin cancer includes basal cell carcinoma which is most common, mild, and rarely spreads, squamous cell carcinoma which is moderate and can spread, and melanoma which is life-threatening. Basal cell and squamous cell carcinoma are highly curable with excision. Melanoma has high death rates if spread.

Skin disorders encompass acne, eczema, psoriasis, dermatitis, infections, and benign tumors. Most are mild, affecting only the skin, and not life-threatening. Severe skin disorders such as pemphigus, toxic epidermal necrolysis, and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma are rare.

Skin pigmentation disorders include vitiligo which causes depigmented patches, melasma which causes hyperpigmentation, and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. They are not dangerous or life-threatening, but cause cosmetic concerns. Vitiligo is associated with autoimmune diseases but is typically mild.

Skin polyps, also called skin tags or acrochordons, are benign, pedunculated growths common in skin folds such as the neck, armpits, and groin. They are not dangerous. Removal is for cosmetic reasons or irritation.

Tobacco addiction is chronic nicotine dependence. It leads to lung cancer, COPD, heart disease, stroke, and many other cancers such as bladder and pancreas. It is the leading preventable cause of death worldwide. Severity is moderate for the addiction itself to life-threatening for long-term complications. Quitting reduces risk.

Social anxiety disorder, also called social phobia, is intense fear of social situations such as judgment or embarrassment, leading to avoidance. It is not life-threatening but impairs work, relationships, and quality of life. Severity ranges from mild and situational discomfort to moderate and severe avoidance. Treatment includes cognitive behavioral therapy and SSRIs.

Soft tissue sarcomas are malignant tumors of connective tissue such as muscle, fat, nerves, and blood vessels. They are rare but aggressive. Early, localized sarcoma is moderate and curable with surgery and radiation. Metastatic sarcoma is life-threatening with poor survival.

Somatic symptom disorder, formerly called somatization, involves physical symptoms such as pain, fatigue, and stomach issues with no identifiable organic cause, causing distress. It is not life-threatening but reduces quality of life. Treatment includes cognitive behavioral therapy and psychiatric care.
A spermatocele is a benign, fluid-filled cyst in the epididymis. It often causes painless scrotal swelling. It is not dangerous and requires no treatment unless painful or large, which is rare.

Hereditary spherocytosis is a genetic red blood cell defect causing spherical red blood cells instead of biconcave. It leads to hemolytic anemia, jaundice, and an enlarged spleen. Most cases are mild and compensated. Severe cases require spleen removal.

Spina bifida is a neural tube defect where the spine does not close fully. Spina bifida occulta is mild and without symptoms. Meningocele is moderate. Myelomeningocele is severe, with the spinal cord exposed, causing paralysis, loss of bladder and bowel control, fluid buildup in the brain, and brain malformation.

Spinal stenosis is narrowing of the spinal canal causing nerve compression. It leads to back or neck pain, numbness, weakness, and neurogenic claudication which is pain when walking. It is typically age-related. Most cases are mild to moderate, managed with physical therapy and pain medication. Severe cases require surgery.

Spinocerebellar ataxias are genetic neurodegenerative disorders causing progressive loss of coordination, slurred speech, and eventually disability. They are not curable and lead to wheelchair dependence, difficulty swallowing, and reduced life expectancy. Severity is severe to life-threatening in late stages.

Spondylitis is inflammation of the vertebrae, also called axial spondyloarthritis or ankylosing spondylitis. It causes chronic back pain and stiffness, eventually causing spinal fusion called bamboo spine. It is not life-threatening but can be disabling. Severity ranges from mild pain to moderate with spinal fusion and limited mobility. Treatment includes NSAIDs and biologics.

Spondylolisthesis is a slipping forward of one vertebra over the one below. It is often from a stress fracture. Low-grade slips are mild and often without symptoms. High-grade slips cause back pain, nerve compression, and walking problems, which are moderate.

Spondylosis is degenerative arthritis of the spine. It is common in the neck or lower back. It causes back or neck pain, stiffness, and sometimes nerve compression. It is very common with aging. Most cases are mild to moderate and managed conservatively.

Spontaneous abortion, also called miscarriage, is pregnancy loss before twenty weeks. It is usually due to chromosomal abnormalities. It causes vaginal bleeding and cramping. Most are mild and self-limited. Heavy bleeding, infection, or retained products of conception called septic abortion are moderate to severe and can be life-threatening if sepsis occurs.

Sporotrichosis is a fungal infection from soil or plant matter such as rose thorns. It causes skin ulcers and nodules along lymphatic channels, which is mild to moderate. Pulmonary or widespread sporotrichosis in immunocompromised people is severe and life-threatening. Treatment is oral itraconazole.

A sprain is a ligament injury. A strain is a muscle or tendon injury. Grade one sprains are mild with stretching and heal in days. Grade two are moderate with partial tear. Grade three are severe with complete tear requiring surgery. Most sprains and strains are mild to moderate and resolve with rest, ice, compression, and elevation.

Nasolacrimal duct obstruction, or tear duct stenosis, blocks tear drainage. It causes excessive tearing, recurrent eye infections called dacryocystitis, and mucus discharge. It is common in infants and resolves spontaneously. In adults, it may require probing or surgery. It is not dangerous but annoying.

Stomach, or gastric, cancer is often diagnosed late due to vague symptoms such as indigestion, feeling full early, and weight loss. Early-stage gastric cancer is curable and moderate. Advanced gastric cancer is life-threatening with poor survival. H. pylori eradication reduces risk.

Strep throat is Group A Streptococcus pharyngitis. It causes sore throat, fever, tonsillar pus, and swollen neck lymph nodes. It is mild and curable with antibiotics. Untreated, it can cause rheumatic fever, which is moderate to severe, and peritonsillar abscess.

Stress incontinence is involuntary leakage of urine with coughing, sneezing, laughing, or exercise. It is common in women after childbirth or menopause. It is not life-threatening but reduces quality of life. Treatment includes pelvic floor exercises, a pessary, or surgery such as a sling.

Esophageal stricture is narrowing of the esophagus. It is often from GERD, causing peptic stricture, radiation, or swallowing caustic substances. It causes progressive difficulty swallowing, especially solids. It is treatable with endoscopic dilation and acid-reducing medication. Severe strictures cause food impaction and weight loss.

Stroke is sudden brain damage from lack of blood flow in eighty-five percent of cases or bleeding in fifteen percent. Symptoms include sudden weakness, numbness, speech difficulty, facial droop, and vision loss. Stroke is a medical emergency. Time is brain. Ischemic stroke is treated with clot-busting drugs or clot removal. Hemorrhagic stroke may require surgery. Severity is life-threatening with high death rates and disability.

A stye, also called a hordeolum, is an acute bacterial infection of an eyelash follicle or oil gland. It causes a painful, red lump on the eyelid margin. It is mild and usually resolves with warm compresses. Antibiotics are used if persistent.

Subacute, or De Quervain, thyroiditis is viral inflammation of the thyroid gland. It causes a painful goiter, fever, and hyperthyroid symptoms, followed later by hypothyroid phase. It is self-limiting over weeks to months and treated with NSAIDs or steroids for pain.

Subarachnoid hemorrhage is bleeding into the subarachnoid space. It is usually from a ruptured cerebral aneurysm. It causes a thunderclap headache, which is the worst headache of life, stiff neck, vomiting, and loss of consciousness. Re-bleeding and blood vessel spasm cause high death rates of thirty to fifty percent. Emergency neurosurgery is required.

Subconjunctival hemorrhage is a red patch on the white of the eye from a broken blood vessel. It is often from coughing, sneezing, or minor trauma. It is painless and harmless, resolving in one to two weeks.

Subdural hematoma is bleeding between the dura and arachnoid mater. It is usually from head trauma, especially in elderly people on blood thinners. Acute subdural hematoma causes rapid neurological decline and death. Chronic subdural hematoma causes gradual headache, confusion, and weakness. Surgical removal may be needed. Severity ranges from moderate for chronic to life-threatening for acute with mass effect.

Substance-related mental disorders include substance use disorders and substance-induced psychiatric disorders such as psychosis, depression, anxiety, and delirium. Severity ranges from mild use with mild impairment to severe addiction and psychosis. Overdose and withdrawal can be life-threatening. Treatment includes detoxification, rehabilitation, and therapy.

disease_name: syndrome of inappropriate secretion of adh (siadh)
description: SIADH is excessive release of antidiuretic hormone. It causes water retention and low sodium. Mild SIADH is without symptoms and mild. Severe low sodium below one hundred twenty-five milliequivalents per liter causes headache, seizures, coma, and death, which is life-threatening. Treatment includes fluid restriction and sometimes medications.

Syphilis is a bacterial sexually transmitted infection. Stages include primary with a chancre, secondary with rash, fever, and swollen lymph nodes, latent, and tertiary with involvement of the brain and spinal cord, heart, and gums. Early syphilis is mild and curable with penicillin. Tertiary syphilis causes serious and potentially fatal complications such as aortic aneurysm, dementia, and nerve damage.

Syringomyelia is a fluid-filled cyst within the spinal cord. It causes progressive spinal cord damage with weakness, sensory loss that is dissociated meaning loss of pain and temperature with preserved touch, and scoliosis. It is often congenital with Chiari malformation. Without surgical decompression, it causes severe disability.

Systemic lupus erythematosus is a systemic autoimmune disease. It affects the skin, joints, kidneys causing lupus nephritis, heart, lungs, blood cells, and brain. Severity ranges from mild with rash and arthritis to life-threatening with kidney inflammation, neuropsychiatric lupus, heart inflammation, and severe low platelets. Treatment includes hydroxychloroquine, steroids, and immunosuppressants.

Teething syndrome is the normal process of primary teeth erupting in infants from six to twenty-four months. It causes mild irritability, drooling, gum swelling, and low-grade fever. It is not dangerous. Over-the-counter teething rings and acetaminophen help.

disease_name: temporary or benign blood in urine
description: Benign hematuria, or blood in the urine, can occur after exercise, called marching hematuria, infection, or benign causes. Microscopic hematuria is often benign. However, persistent hematuria requires evaluation for stones, infection, or cancer, which is moderate to severe. Isolated, self-limited hematuria is mild.

Temporomandibular joint disorder causes jaw pain, clicking, locking, headaches, and difficulty chewing. It is usually benign and self-limiting. Treatment includes splints, physical therapy, and anti-inflammatory drugs.

Tendinitis is inflammation of a tendon. Examples include Achilles, patellar, rotator cuff, and lateral epicondyle which is tennis elbow. It causes pain and swelling near the joint. Most cases are mild to moderate and resolve with rest, ice, anti-inflammatory drugs, and physical therapy. Chronic cases may require steroid injection or surgery.

Tension headaches are the most common headache type. They cause mild to moderate bilateral pressing or tightening pain like a band around the head. They are not life-threatening and respond to over-the-counter pain relievers and stress reduction.

Testicular cancer is the most common cancer in young men aged fifteen to thirty-five. It presents as a painless testicular lump. It is highly curable, with over ninety-five percent survival even with spread. Treatment includes removal of the testicle and chemotherapy or radiation for advanced stages.

Testicular disorders include epididymitis which is mild, orchitis which is mild, testicular torsion which is severe and an emergency, hydrocele which is mild, varicocele which is mild, and cancer which is moderate. Severity varies widely. Torsion leads to loss of the testicle within hours and is severe.

Testicular torsion is twisting of the spermatic cord, cutting off blood supply to the testicle. It causes sudden, severe testicular pain, nausea, vomiting, and a high-riding testicle. It is a urologic emergency. The salvage rate is high if surgery is performed within six hours. After twelve hours, testicle loss is likely. Severity is severe due to potential loss of the testicle.

Thalassemia is a genetic blood disorder with reduced hemoglobin production. Thalassemia minor, or trait, is mild and without symptoms. Thalassemia intermedia is moderate, causing chronic anemia and sometimes requiring transfusions. Thalassemia major, called Cooley anemia, is severe, requiring lifelong transfusions and iron chelation. Without treatment, it is life-threatening in childhood.

Thoracic aortic aneurysm is a dilation of the aorta in the chest. Most are without symptoms and mild. The major risk is rupture or dissection, which causes sudden severe chest or back pain, low blood pressure, and death. Elective surgical repair when the size exceeds five point five centimeters prevents rupture. Severity is mild if small and monitored, life-threatening if ruptured.

Thoracic outlet syndrome is compression of nerves or blood vessels between the collarbone and first rib. It causes arm and hand numbness, weakness, and sometimes swelling or discoloration from vein or artery compression. Most cases are mild to moderate and managed with physical therapy. Severe arterial thoracic outlet syndrome requires surgery.

Threatened pregnancy is any condition threatening the viability of the pregnancy, such as bleeding or cramps, but the cervix is closed. It can progress to miscarriage or continue normally. It is not immediately life-threatening but causes anxiety and requires monitoring. Severe hemorrhage is rare.

Thrombocytopenia is a low platelet count below one hundred fifty thousand. Mild thrombocytopenia from fifty thousand to one hundred thousand is often without symptoms. Moderate from twenty thousand to fifty thousand causes easy bruising. Severe below twenty thousand causes spontaneous bleeding such as small red spots, mucosal bleeding, and bleeding inside the skull, which is life-threatening. Causes include ITP, drugs, infections, and TTP or HUS.

Thrombophlebitis is inflammation of a vein with a blood clot. Superficial thrombophlebitis near the skin causes pain, redness, and a palpable cord. It is mild to moderate, treated with anti-inflammatory drugs and warm compresses. Deep vein thrombophlebitis, or DVT, is more serious because the clot can travel to the lungs, causing a pulmonary embolism, which is life-threatening.

Thyroid cancer includes papillary which is most common and has an excellent prognosis, follicular, medullary, and anaplastic. Papillary and follicular are mild to moderate and highly curable with surgery, with over ninety-five percent five-year survival. Anaplastic thyroid cancer is life-threatening with a median survival of less than six months. Severity ranges from mild for papillary microcarcinoma to life-threatening for anaplastic.

Thyroid disease includes hypothyroidism which is mild and treatable with levothyroxine, hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease which is moderate, thyroid nodules which are mostly benign and mild, and thyroid cancer which is moderate to severe. Most are not life-threatening if treated. Thyroid storm from severe hyperthyroidism is life-threatening.

Thyroid nodules are very common, occurring in over fifty percent of adults. Over ninety percent are benign and mild. Malignant nodules, which are five to ten percent, are usually papillary cancer, which is moderate and curable. Evaluation includes ultrasound and fine needle aspiration.

Tic disorders include transient tic disorder, chronic tic disorder, and Tourette syndrome. They involve repetitive, involuntary movements or vocalizations. They are not life-threatening but can be socially disabling, which is moderate. Most are mild and improve with age. Treatment includes behavioral therapy and medications for severe tics.

Tietze syndrome is benign, painful swelling of the costochondral joints, especially the second and third ribs. It causes chest pain. It is not life-threatening and resolves with anti-inflammatory drugs or steroid injection. It differs from costochondritis, which has no swelling.

Tinnitus is the perception of sound such as ringing or buzzing without an external source. Idiopathic tinnitus is common and often associated with hearing loss. It is not dangerous but can be annoying or distressing. Treatment includes sound therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy, and addressing underlying causes such as noise or ototoxic drugs.

Tonsillar hypertrophy, or enlarged tonsils, is common in children. It causes snoring, mouth breathing, sleep apnea, and difficulty swallowing. Mild enlargement is normal and mild. Severe enlargement causing obstructive sleep apnea is moderate and may require tonsillectomy.

Tonsillitis is inflammation of the tonsils. It is usually viral or bacterial, causing sore throat, fever, and difficulty swallowing. Most viral cases are mild and self-limiting. Bacterial strep requires antibiotics but remains mild. Recurrent tonsillitis with seven or more episodes per year may lead to tonsillectomy, which is moderate.

A tooth abscess is a collection of pus from bacterial infection of the tooth pulp. It causes severe tooth pain, swelling, fever, and sensitivity to hot and cold. It can spread to surrounding tissues, causing facial cellulitis, Ludwig angina, or cavernous sinus thrombosis, which is life-threatening. Prompt dental treatment with root canal or extraction and antibiotics is needed.

Tooth disorders include dental caries which are cavities, enamel defects, tooth fracture, discoloration, and malocclusion. Most are mild and treatable with fillings, crowns, or orthodontics. Severe untreated caries leads to an abscess, which is moderate.

Torticollis, also called wry neck, is twisting of the neck to one side due to spasm of the sternocleidomastoid muscle. Congenital torticollis in infants is mild and resolves with stretching. Acquired torticollis in adults may be from sleeping wrong or from cervical dystonia. It is not life-threatening.

Tourette syndrome is a tic disorder with multiple motor tics and at least one vocal tic for more than one year, starting before age eighteen. It is not life-threatening but can cause social impairment. Most cases are mild. Severe tics can cause self-injury, which is moderate. Treatment includes behavioral therapy and medications such as clonidine or antipsychotics.

Toxic multinodular goiter, also called Plummer disease, is hyperthyroidism caused by autonomously functioning thyroid nodules. It is generally milder than Graves disease, causing weight loss, palpitations, and atrial fibrillation, which is moderate. Treatment includes radioactive iodine, antithyroid drugs, or surgery.

Toxoplasmosis is a parasitic infection often from cat feces or undercooked meat. In healthy people, it is without symptoms or mild, causing flu-like symptoms. In immunocompromised people such as those with HIV or transplant recipients, and in pregnant women, it is severe, causing brain inflammation, eye inflammation, and congenital infection in the fetus, which is life-threatening.

Tracheitis, or bacterial tracheitis, is infection of the trachea. It is usually in children and causes fever, severe cough, noisy breathing called stridor, and breathing distress. It is a medical emergency because it can block the airway. It requires intravenous antibiotics and sometimes intubation and a breathing machine.

A transient ischemic attack, also called a mini-stroke, is temporary neurological dysfunction such as weakness, numbness, or speech difficulty lasting minutes to less than twenty-four hours without permanent brain damage. It is a major warning sign for future stroke, with a ten to twenty percent risk within ninety days. The TIA itself is mild and reversible, but it requires urgent evaluation.

Trichiasis is eyelashes growing inward toward the eye. It causes irritation, redness, tearing, and can cause corneal abrasion or ulcer, risking vision loss. It is mild to moderate. Treatment includes removal of the lashes, electrolysis, or surgery.

Trichinosis, also called trichinellosis, is a parasitic disease from eating undercooked meat such as pork or bear containing Trichinella larvae. The early intestinal phase causes nausea and diarrhea. The later muscle phase causes fever, muscle pain, swelling around the eyes, and high eosinophils. Severe cases cause heart inflammation, brain inflammation, and death, which is life-threatening.

Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis. In women, it causes vaginitis with green, frothy discharge, itching, and odor. In men, it usually causes no symptoms. It is mild and curable with metronidazole or tinidazole.

Tricuspid valve disease includes stenosis which is narrowing, usually from rheumatic fever, and regurgitation which is leaking, often from right ventricle enlargement due to pulmonary hypertension or left heart disease. Mild tricuspid regurgitation is common and benign. Severe tricuspid regurgitation causes right heart failure, which is moderate.

Trigeminal neuralgia is severe, paroxysmal, electric shock-like facial pain in the distribution of the trigeminal nerve. It is often triggered by touch, chewing, or wind. It is not life-threatening but is extremely painful and disabling. Treatment includes carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, or surgery such as microvascular decompression.

Trigger finger, also called stenosing tenosynovitis, is catching or locking of a finger in a bent position due to inflammation of the flexor tendon sheath. It causes pain, clicking, and difficulty extending the finger. Most cases are mild and resolve with steroid injection. Surgery to release the tendon sheath is curative.

Tuberculosis is an airborne bacterial infection primarily causing lung disease with cough, coughing up blood, fever, and night sweats. Latent tuberculosis is without symptoms and mild. Active tuberculosis is moderate to severe and can be life-threatening if it spreads to the brain, causing meningitis, or causes massive bleeding. It is curable with long-course antibiotics.

Tuberous sclerosis complex is a genetic disorder causing benign tumors in the brain, kidneys, heart, lungs, and skin. Manifestations include seizures, infantile spasms, intellectual disability, autism, and kidney tumors that can bleed. Severity ranges from mild with only skin lesions to severe with uncontrollable epilepsy, kidney failure, and brain tumors.

Turner syndrome is a chromosomal disorder in females with a single X chromosome. It causes short stature, webbed neck, broad chest, ovarian failure causing infertility, and congenital heart defects such as coarctation of the aorta and bicuspid aortic valve. Severity ranges from mild with near-normal life to severe with heart complications and life-threatening aortic dissection. With medical monitoring, most have normal life expectancy.

Typhoid fever is a bacterial infection from contaminated food or water. It causes prolonged fever, headache, abdominal pain, and rose spots. Without antibiotics, it causes intestinal perforation, septic shock, and death, which is life-threatening. With treatment, death rates are less than one percent.

Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease causing diffuse inflammation of the colon lining. It leads to bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, urgency, and weight loss. Severity ranges from mild with limited disease to severe with involvement of the entire colon, toxic megacolon, and risk of perforation, which is life-threatening. Long-term ulcerative colitis increases colorectal cancer risk.

Urethral disorders include urethritis which is inflammation from sexually transmitted infections, urethral stricture which is scarring, caruncle which is a benign polyp, and diverticulum. Most are mild to moderate, causing pain, bleeding, and obstructive symptoms. Severe stricture can cause urinary retention and kidney damage, which is moderate.

Urethral stricture is narrowing of the urethra due to scar tissue from trauma, instrumentation, or infection. It causes a weak urinary stream, straining to void, and urinary retention. It is not life-threatening but reduces quality of life. Treatment includes dilation, cutting the stricture, or surgical repair.

Posterior urethral valves are congenital obstructing membranes in the male urethra. They cause bladder outlet obstruction, kidney swelling, and kidney damage before birth. Severity ranges from mild with late onset and mild symptoms to severe with prenatal kidney swelling and end-stage renal disease, which is life-threatening. Treatment is endoscopic valve ablation.

Urethritis is inflammation of the urethra. It is usually from sexually transmitted infections such as gonorrhea or chlamydia. It causes painful urination, discharge, and itching. It is mild and curable with antibiotics. Untreated, it can lead to epididymitis or pelvic inflammatory disease, which is moderate.

Urge incontinence, also called overactive bladder, is a sudden, intense urge to urinate followed by involuntary leakage. It is not life-threatening but reduces quality of life. Treatment includes bladder training, pelvic floor exercises, anticholinergic drugs, or nerve stimulation.

Urinary tract infection is bacterial infection of the bladder or urethra. It causes painful urination, frequency, urgency, and lower abdominal pain. It is mild and treatable with oral antibiotics. Untreated urinary tract infections can ascend to the kidneys, causing pyelonephritis, which is moderate to severe. Recurrent infections are common.

Urinary tract obstruction blocks urine flow. It can be from an enlarged prostate, stone, stricture, or tumor. Acute obstruction causes severe pain called renal colic and no urine output, which is an emergency. Chronic obstruction causes kidney swelling, kidney damage, and kidney failure, which is life-threatening. Post-renal acute kidney injury is reversible with prompt relief of the obstruction by catheter, stent, or surgery.

Uterine atony is failure of the uterus to contract after delivery. It causes severe postpartum hemorrhage. It is a major cause of maternal death worldwide. Treatment includes uterine massage, medications to contract the uterus, balloon tamponade, and sometimes hysterectomy.

Uterine cancer is most commonly endometrial cancer of the lining. Early-stage endometrial cancer is highly curable with hysterectomy and is moderate. Advanced or aggressive subtypes such as serous, clear cell, and carcinosarcoma are life-threatening. Uterine sarcomas have a poor prognosis.

Uterine fibroids, also called leiomyomas, are benign smooth muscle tumors of the uterus. They are common in reproductive age. Most are without symptoms and mild. Symptomatic fibroids cause heavy bleeding, pelvic pressure, pain, and infertility, which is moderate. Treatment includes hormonal therapy, myomectomy, or uterine artery embolization.

Uveitis is inflammation of the uveal tract, which includes the iris, ciliary body, and choroid. It causes eye pain, redness, sensitivity to light, floaters, and vision loss. Anterior uveitis, or iritis, is common and usually moderate, treatable with steroids. Posterior uveitis affecting the retina and choroid can cause permanent vision loss, which is severe. Complications include glaucoma, cataract, and macular swelling.

VACTERL association is a non-random set of congenital anomalies including vertebral defects, anal atresia, cardiac defects, tracheo-esophageal fistula, renal anomalies, and limb abnormalities. Severity depends on the specific combination. Many require multiple surgeries in infancy. Some combinations are life-threatening, such as esophageal atresia and complex heart defects.

Vaginal cysts, such as Gartner duct, inclusion, and Bartholin cysts, are benign, fluid-filled sacs. Small cysts are without symptoms and mild. Large or infected cysts cause pain, discomfort, or pain with intercourse, which is moderate. Treatment includes observation, marsupialization, or excision.

Vulvovaginal candidiasis is a yeast infection causing itching, burning, thick white discharge like cottage cheese, and redness. It is mild and treatable with topical or oral antifungals such as miconazole or fluconazole. Recurrent infections require evaluation.

Vaginismus is involuntary spasm of the pelvic floor muscles around the vagina, preventing penetration such as sexual intercourse or tampon use. It is not life-threatening but causes significant sexual distress and pain. Treatment includes pelvic floor physical therapy, vaginal dilators, and cognitive behavioral therapy.

Vaginitis is inflammation of the vagina from infection such as bacterial vaginosis, yeast, or trichomoniasis, or from irritation such as atrophic vaginitis. Symptoms include discharge, itching, odor, and pain with intercourse. It is mild to moderate and treatable with antibiotics, antifungals, or estrogen.

Valley fever, also called coccidioidomycosis, is a fungal infection from inhaling Coccidioides spores in the southwestern United States. Most cases are mild, causing flu-like symptoms that are self-limited. Severe cases cause widespread disease affecting the skin, bones, and meningitis in immunocompromised people, which is life-threatening.

Varicocele is enlargement of veins within the scrotum, similar to varicose veins. It is common and usually without symptoms and mild. Large varicoceles cause testicular pain, testicle shrinkage, and infertility, which is moderate. Treatment is surgical ligation or embolization.

Varicose veins are swollen, twisted veins, usually in the legs, from incompetent valves. They cause pain, heaviness, itching, and skin changes. Most are mild and cosmetic. Severe varicose veins cause venous ulcers, bleeding, or blood clot inflammation, which is moderate. Treatment includes compression stockings, sclerotherapy, laser, or surgery.

Vasculitis is inflammation of blood vessels, leading to reduced blood flow and organ damage to the skin, kidneys, lungs, nerves, and brain. Severity varies. Mild forms such as cutaneous small-vessel vasculitis are limited to the skin. Life-threatening forms such as ANCA-associated GPA, microscopic polyangiitis, polyarteritis nodosa, and giant cell arteritis can cause blindness or stroke. Treatment includes immunosuppressants.

Chronic venous insufficiency is poor venous return from the legs due to valve damage. It causes leg swelling, pain, skin changes called stasis dermatitis, and venous ulcers. Mild venous insufficiency causes swelling and varicose veins and is mild. Severe venous insufficiency causes recurrent ulcers and skin hardening, which is moderate. Treatment includes compression, leg elevation, and venous procedures.

Vertebrobasilar insufficiency is reduced blood flow in the back part of the brain. It causes dizziness, vertigo, double vision, unsteadiness, and drop attacks. It is often from atherosclerosis or embolism. It is a risk factor for stroke, which is severe. Treatment includes antiplatelet drugs and risk factor modification.

Vesicoureteral reflux is the backward flow of urine from the bladder to the ureters or kidneys. It is common in children and causes recurrent urinary tract infections and kidney scarring. Low-grade reflux often resolves spontaneously and is mild. High-grade reflux may require antibiotics or surgical reimplantation, which is moderate.

Viral exanthem is a widespread rash from a viral infection such as measles, rubella, roseola, enterovirus, or EBV. It is usually mild and self-limiting, resolving with the infection. Serious causes such as measles or varicella can be severe due to complications.

Viral hepatitis includes types A, B, C, D, and E. Hepatitis A and E are acute and self-limiting, mild to moderate. Hepatitis B and C can become chronic, leading to cirrhosis and liver cancer, which are severe to life-threatening. Acute liver failure from any hepatitis is life-threatening.

Viral warts, also called verruca, are caused by HPV. They form rough, benign growths on the skin, such as common warts, plantar warts, and flat warts. They are not dangerous but can be painful, such as plantar warts, or unsightly. Most resolve spontaneously. Treatment includes salicylic acid, cryotherapy, or laser.

Vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness, dry eyes, corneal ulcers called Bitot spots, and blindness. It also impairs immunity. Severe deficiency causes softening and death of the cornea and blindness, which is moderate. It is rare in developed countries. Supplementation restores vision.

Vitamin B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia with fatigue and paleness, and neurological symptoms such as numbness and tingling in the hands and feet, cognitive changes, and unsteadiness. It is usually mild and reversible with B12 supplementation. Long-term deficiency causes permanent nerve damage, which is moderate.

Vitamin D deficiency causes bone pain, muscle weakness, and in children, rickets with bone deformities. In adults, it causes osteomalacia which is soft bones, and osteoporosis with increased fracture risk. It is common, especially in elderly and dark-skinned individuals. Supplementation corrects the deficiency.

Vitreous degeneration, also called syneresis, is age-related liquefaction of the vitreous gel in the eye. It causes floaters. It is benign and very common. No treatment is needed. Posterior vitreous detachment is a specific form that can cause a retinal tear, which is an emergency.

Vitreous hemorrhage is bleeding into the vitreous cavity of the eye. It can be from diabetic retinopathy, retinal tear, trauma, or subarachnoid hemorrhage called Terson syndrome. It causes sudden floaters, dark streaks, or severe vision loss, which is moderate. If from a retinal tear, emergency laser or freezing treatment is needed to prevent detachment, which is severe.

Vocal cord polyp is a benign growth on the vocal fold, usually from voice overuse or trauma. It causes hoarseness and voice fatigue. It is not dangerous. Treatment includes voice rest, voice therapy, or surgical removal.

Volvulus is twisting of a loop of intestine, most often the sigmoid colon or cecum. It causes intestinal obstruction and cuts off blood supply. It causes sudden abdominal pain, distension, vomiting, and constipation. It is a surgical emergency. Without untwisting and removal of dead bowel, bowel death, perforation, abdominal infection, and death occur.

von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. It is due to deficiency of von Willebrand factor. Most cases are mild, causing easy bruising and prolonged bleeding from cuts or after surgery. Severe cases cause joint bleeds, though rare. Treatment includes desmopressin or factor replacement.

Vulvar cancer is a malignancy of the external genitalia. It often presents as a lump, itching, or bleeding. Early-stage vulvar cancer is curable with surgery and is moderate. Advanced or metastatic vulvar cancer is life-threatening.

Vulvar disorders include vulvovaginitis, lichen sclerosus, lichen planus, vulvodynia, and cysts. Most are mild, causing itching and discomfort. Lichen sclerosus causes scarring and predisposes to vulvar cancer, which is moderate.

Vulvodynia is chronic vulvar pain such as burning or stinging without an identifiable cause. It is often provoked by touch or intercourse. It is not life-threatening but impairs quality of life and sexual function. Treatment includes topical lidocaine, pelvic floor therapy, and sometimes tricyclic antidepressants.

Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome is a neurological disorder caused by thiamine deficiency, often in alcoholics. Wernicke encephalopathy is acute and causes confusion, unsteadiness, eye movement abnormalities, and eye muscle weakness. It is reversible with thiamine. Korsakoff syndrome is chronic and causes severe memory loss, confabulation, and is often permanent. Untreated Wernicke encephalopathy can be fatal.

White blood cell diseases include leukopenia which is low white blood cells and mild to moderate, leukocytosis which is high white blood cells and mild, and malignancies such as leukemia and lymphoma, which are life-threatening. Severity ranges from mild benign neutropenia to life-threatening acute leukemia.

Whooping cough, also called pertussis, is a highly contagious bacterial respiratory infection. It causes severe coughing fits ending with a whooping sound, coughing followed by vomiting, and pauses in breathing. It is mild in vaccinated older children and adults, but severe in infants, causing breathing pauses, pneumonia, seizures, and death.

Wilson disease is a genetic disorder of copper metabolism. It leads to copper accumulation in the liver, causing hepatitis and cirrhosis, and in the brain, causing movement disorders and psychiatric symptoms. Untreated, it is fatal. With lifelong chelation or zinc therapy, patients have normal life expectancy. Severity ranges from mild and without symptoms to severe with sudden liver failure or severe neurological impairment.

Yeast infection, called candidiasis, can affect the mouth as thrush, skin as intertrigo, vagina as vulvovaginitis, or bloodstream as systemic candidiasis. Mucosal and skin infections are mild and treatable. Systemic candidiasis in immunocompromised people is life-threatening.

Zenker diverticulum is a pouch in the pharyngoesophageal junction due to weakness in the back wall of the pharynx. It causes difficulty swallowing, regurgitation of undigested food, bad breath, and aspiration pneumonia, which is severe. Small pouches are without symptoms and mild. Large pouches require surgical or endoscopic repair.
